syllabus_desc: Explain the fundamental difference between piston and gas turbine (jet) engines.
concept: Piston engines work through reciprocating motion—pistons go up and down, driving a crankshaft that ultimately turns your main rotor shaft. Fuel and air burn in discrete pulses inside cylinders, each pulse pushing a piston down. Gas turbine engines work continuously—air flows through a compressor, fuel burns steadily in a combustor, and hot expanding gases drive turbines. Piston engines are efficient at lower speeds (why we use them for general aviation training at places like Ardmore), thermally efficient because of high compression ratios, and simpler mechanically. Turbines produce thrust directly, no main rotor shaft needed, but are fuel-hungry at low speeds and need expensive materials. For training aircraft with moderate power requirements, piston's the sensible choice.
exam_tip: Piston = reciprocating pistons, pulses of combustion, propeller. Turbine = continuous flow, hot gas drives turbines, direct thrust. Know why piston's used in your training aircraft.
key_facts: Piston: reciprocating motion, discrete combustion pulses
Turbine: continuous flow, continuous combustion
Piston: efficient low-speed, high compression ratio
Turbine: direct thrust, fuel-hungry at low speed
Training aircraft use piston for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the basic types of piston aircraft engine.
concept: Aircraft piston engines come in several flavours. You've got in-line engines where cylinders line up in a row (older design, compact lengthwise but tall). Radial engines with cylinders arranged in a circle around a central crankshaft (old warbird types, rarely seen now). Flat or horizontally-opposed engines where cylinders are opposite each other (like your modern Lycoming or Continental—what you'll see in training twin at Hamilton or Christchurch). There's also V-engines with two banks of cylinders at an angle. For modern light aircraft, horizontally-opposed dominates because it's compact, good centre of gravity, and reasonable power-to-weight.
exam_tip: In-line, radial, flat, V-type. Know which one's in your aircraft. Most modern trainers = horizontally-opposed.
key_facts: In-line: cylinders in row
Radial: cylinders in circle around crankshaft
Flat/horizontally-opposed: opposite cylinders
V-type: two banks at angle
Modern trainers use horizontally-opposed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the fundamental operating principle of a piston four-stroke cycle engine.
concept: Four-stroke's the standard. First stroke (intake): piston moves down, intake valve opens, fuel-air mixture fills the cylinder. Second stroke (compression): both valves close, piston compresses the mixture, temperature and pressure rise. Third stroke (power/expansion): spark ignites the mixture, explosion pushes the piston down hard, producing useful work. Fourth stroke (exhaust): piston rises, exhaust valve opens, burnt gases exit. Then the cycle repeats. Each cylinder fires in sequence, so on a four-cylinder engine with proper timing, there's always one cylinder on the power stroke, giving continuous smooth delivery of power to the crankshaft. This is your light twin at Ardmore.
exam_tip: Intake, compression, power, exhaust. Know what happens in each. Spark timing happens during compression stroke, ignition during power stroke. Understand the thermodynamic sequence.
key_facts: Intake: valve open, mixture in
Compression: both closed, pressure rises
Power: spark ignites, explosion drives piston
Exhaust: valve open, gases out
Two complete crankshaft rotations = one cycle
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (a) cylinders; (b) cylinder heads; (c) pistons; (d) connecting rods; (e) crankshaft; (f) valves; (g) valve operating mechanism; (h) camshaft; (i) spark plugs; (j) injectors.
concept: Cylinders are the pressure vessels where combustion happens—strong, usually cast aluminium. Cylinder heads seal the cylinder tops, house the spark plugs and valve seats, and create the combustion chamber shape. Pistons are the moving part inside cylinders, sealed with rings to prevent gas escape; they transfer combustion force to the connecting rod. Connecting rods link pistons to the crankshaft, converting up-down motion to rotational motion. The crankshaft's the main shaft transmitting power, turning at half the piston speed. Intake and exhaust valves let mixture in and gases out, operated by a camshaft (rotating shaft with lobes that push valve lifters). Spark plugs ignite the mixture at the right moment. In fuel-injected engines, injectors spray fine fuel mist into cylinders instead of carburettor mixing.
exam_tip: Understand the chain: mixture in via intake valve or injector, spark from plug ignites it, explosion pushes piston, connecting rod turns crankshaft, exhaust valve releases gases. Each component's purpose fits the cycle.
key_facts: Cylinders: pressure vessels for combustion
Heads: seal top, house plugs/valves
Pistons: transfer force to connecting rod
Connecting rods: convert linear to rotational motion
Crankshaft: rotates, transmits power
Valves: control intake/exhaust timing
Camshaft: operates valves via mechanical linkage
Spark plugs: ignite fuel-air mixture
Injectors: spray fuel (fuel injection engines)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) cycle; (b) stroke; (c) top dead centre (TDC); (d) bottom dead centre (BDC); (e) bore; (f) clearance volume; (g) swept volume; (h) compression ratio; (i) firing interval; (j) firing order; (k) manifolds; (l) manifold pressure; (m) crank angle.
concept: Cycle's the complete four-stroke sequence repeated. Stroke is one piston motion (down or up), so four strokes = one cycle = two crankshaft rotations. Top dead centre (TDC) is the highest point the piston reaches—that's when spark happens. Bottom dead centre (BDC) is lowest. Bore is cylinder diameter, swept volume is the volume the piston displaces in one stroke (bore area × stroke length). Clearance volume is what's left at TDC—the combustion chamber. Compression ratio is swept plus clearance divided by clearance; higher ratios burn fuel more efficiently but need higher octane fuel. Firing interval's the crankshaft angle between successive cylinder fires (360 degrees divided by cylinder count for a four-cylinder). Firing order is the sequence cylinders fire in (usually 1-3-4-2 on a typical four-cylinder to balance forces). Manifolds are the piping—intake manifold distributes fuel-air mixture, exhaust manifold collects burnt gases. Manifold pressure is the pressure in the intake manifold, critical for power calculations. Crank angle is the crankshaft's position measured from TDC in degrees.
exam_tip: Definitions, definitions, definitions. These terms appear constantly in exam questions and when discussing engine performance. Know exactly what each means.
key_facts: Cycle = complete four-stroke sequence
Stroke = one piston motion
TDC/BDC = highest/lowest piston positions
Bore = cylinder diameter
Swept volume = bore area × stroke
Compression ratio = (swept + clearance) / clearance
Firing interval = 360 / cylinder count degrees
Firing order = sequence of ignition
Manifolds = intake/exhaust piping
Manifold pressure = pressure in intake
Crank angle = position from TDC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) valve lag; (b) valve lead; (c) valve overlap.
concept: Valve lag is when the exhaust valve doesn't open exactly when the piston reaches bottom dead centre (BDC)—it opens slightly after, so burnt gases still escape even as the piston starts up. Valve lead is when the intake valve opens before TDC on the intake stroke—it opens early while pressure's still expelling exhaust, creating turbulence that helps scavenge (clear) the cylinder. Valve overlap is the brief period where both intake and exhaust valves are simultaneously open—sounds wrong, but it's actually brilliant for gas flow. At the moment exhaust closes, the next piston downstroke creates negative pressure, and having the intake already cracked open lets fresh mixture rush in smoothly, improving scavenging and filling. It's a timing compromise; get it wrong and you waste mixture or trap exhaust.
exam_tip: Lag = opens late. Lead = opens early. Overlap = both open briefly. Understand why: exhaust scavenging and intake filling efficiency. Diagrams help here.
key_facts: Valve lag: exhaust opens after BDC
Valve lead: intake opens before TDC
Valve overlap: brief period both open
Purposes: scavenging exhaust, filling intake
Timing critical for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of valve overlap.
concept: Valve overlap improves cylinder scavenging—removing residual burnt gases so the next intake stroke can fill with fresh mixture. When both valves are open briefly and the piston's near BDC, the outgoing exhaust creates pressure that pushes out the last bits of burnt gas, and the negative pressure from the descending piston draws fresh mixture in. This overlap creates a natural 'sweeping' action that improves gas exchange. Better scavenging means more fresh mixture per cycle, which means more power. Better filling means the engine operates more efficiently across the RPM range. Modern engines' camshaft profiles are optimised for this overlap; a wider overlap helps engine breathing at higher RPMs, though it can cause issues at very low idle speeds if not compensated for. That's why idle's rougher on some high-performance engines.
exam_tip: Advantages: improved scavenging, better cylinder filling, increased power output, better breathing at altitude. Know why engines with wide overlap might have rough idle.
key_facts: Improves scavenging of exhaust gases
Improves fresh mixture intake
Increases power output
Better engine breathing at altitude
Can cause rough idle if excessive
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the term ignition timing, and the need for spark advance.
concept: Ignition timing is when the spark occurs relative to the piston position. You can't wait until TDC to spark—combustion takes a few milliseconds to develop, and you need maximum pressure right after TDC (when the piston's driving downward). So spark happens before TDC, called 'spark advance.' Typically 20-30 degrees before TDC depending on the engine and operating conditions. At takeoff with high power, you want more advance; at cruise, less. Some older engines have manual advance/retard controls; modern ones adjust automatically. Spark too early and you get detonation (pre-ignition). Spark too late and combustion finishes after the power stroke starts, wasting energy. Getting it right is critical for smooth, efficient operation.
exam_tip: Spark at TDC? No, too late. Spark before TDC = spark advance. Why? Combustion needs time to build pressure. Understand the timing relationship to piston motion.
key_facts: Ignition timing = spark position relative to piston
Spark advance = occurs before TDC
Typical 20-30 degrees before TDC
Combustion takes milliseconds to develop
Too early = detonation, too late = power loss
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between detonation and pre-ignition.
concept: Detonation is when the last bit of unburnt fuel-air mixture explodes spontaneously from pressure and heat, after the spark has already ignited the first bit. You get a two-stage burn instead of smooth controlled combustion, and the shockwave can damage pistons and valves. Pre-ignition is when the mixture ignites before the spark plug fires—usually from a hot spot in the combustion chamber (carbon deposit, overheated valve, bad spark plug). Both cause a knocking sound, rough running, and power loss. Detonation's usually caused by low-octane fuel, high ambient temperature, high manifold pressure, or too much spark advance. Pre-ignition can happen with any fuel if the combustion chamber's too hot. Lean mixture, high compression, and high power all increase risk. They're distinct problems requiring different fixes—add higher octane for detonation, reduce power or cool the engine for pre-ignition.
exam_tip: Detonation = after spark, unburnt fuel ignites from pressure. Pre-ignition = before spark, from hot spot. Both knock. Different causes, different fixes.
key_facts: Detonation: unburnt fuel explodes after spark
Pre-ignition: mixture ignites before spark
Both cause knocking, rough running
Detonation: low octane, hot, high power
Pre-ignition: hot spots in chamber
Different fixes required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms; (a) force; (b) work; (c) power; (d) energy; (e) torque.
concept: Force is a push or pull; measured in Newtons (N) or pounds (lb). Work is force applied over distance; it's measured in Joules (J) or foot-pounds (ft-lb). Power is the rate at which work is done—how much work per unit time; measured in watts (W) or horsepower (hp). Energy is the capacity to do work; same units as work. Torque is a twisting force—applied at a distance from a pivot point. Think of tightening a bolt with a wrench; the force you apply multiplied by the wrench length is torque (Newton-metres or foot-pounds). For engines: the piston's combustion force (force) pushes the crankshaft through an arc (work). The engine's rotating speed determines how fast this happens (power). A strong engine produces both high force (torque) and high power because of speed. Understanding these relationships helps explain why an engine's performance is defined by both torque and power—high torque at low RPM gives slow, strong output; high RPM at lower torque gives fast output.
exam_tip: Force = push/pull. Work = force × distance. Power = work / time. Torque = force × distance (rotational). Energy = capacity to work.
key_facts: Force measured in Newtons or pounds
Work: force applied over distance
Power: work per unit time (watts/hp)
Energy: capacity to do work
Torque: twisting force at distance
Combustion force = work. Repeated = power
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between horsepower and kilowatts.
concept: Horsepower and kilowatts are both units of power; they're just different scales. One horsepower is defined as 746 watts. So 1 hp = 0.746 kW, and conversely, 1 kW ≈ 1.34 hp. Modern specifications often list engine power in kilowatts for international standardisation, but traditional aircraft specs (especially in older documents) use horsepower. A typical light training twin might be rated at 160 hp per engine = about 119 kW per engine. A 100 hp trainer = about 75 kW. When you see a spec, just mentally convert if needed. Most exam questions will specify which unit they're using.
exam_tip: 1 hp = 746 watts = 0.746 kW. Multiply hp by 0.746 to get kW. Multiply kW by 1.34 to get hp.
key_facts: 1 hp = 746 watts
1 kW ≈ 1.34 hp
Both units express power (rate of work)
Know conversion for international specs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) brake power; (b) rated power; (c) rated altitude (critical altitude); (d) full throttle height.
concept: Brake power is the actual power delivered by the engine shaft—the useful power you can extract and use (driving the propeller, etc.). It's measured by applying a controlled load (brake) to the shaft and measuring the torque and RPM needed to resist it. Rated power is the maximum continuous power the engine's approved to produce without damage; exceeding it shortens engine life. Most engines have both a takeoff power (high for 5 minutes) and a cruise power rating (sustainable indefinitely). Rated altitude (or critical altitude) is the altitude at which the engine can no longer produce rated power, even at full throttle. Above this altitude, power drops because air density's too low. A naturally-aspirated engine might have critical altitude of 8000-10,000 feet. Full throttle height is similar—it's the altitude where the engine reaches its rated power at full throttle (below this, it reaches rated power before hitting full throttle; above, it can't reach rated power even throttle-firewalled). For training, these matter because you know your engine's limitations—in the heat at Ardmore, you might not achieve rated power at sea level due to temperature. At altitude climbing out from Christchurch, power decreases as you climb.
exam_tip: Brake power = shaft power (what's available). Rated = max continuous approved. Critical altitude = loses rated power. Full throttle height = reaches rated power.
key_facts: Brake power: useful shaft power output
Rated power: maximum continuous approved
Takeoff vs cruise ratings differ
Critical altitude: power falls below rated
Full throttle height: reaches rated at full throttle
Power decreases above critical altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) thermal efficiency; (b) mechanical efficiency; (c) volumetric efficiency.
concept: Thermal efficiency is how well the engine converts fuel's chemical energy into useful mechanical work. A perfect engine would convert 100% of fuel energy to work; reality's maybe 30-35% for a piston engine (the rest is lost as heat in exhaust, cooling system, friction, etc.). Modern engines with high compression ratios achieve better thermal efficiency than older, low-compression designs. Mechanical efficiency is how much of the power generated by combustion actually makes it to the crankshaft output (as opposed to being lost to internal friction, bearing drag, etc.). A well-tuned engine might lose 10-20% of combustion power to internal friction. Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of actual air mass drawn into the cylinder versus the theoretical maximum air mass at atmospheric pressure and temperature. A naturally-aspirated engine at sea level and low RPM achieves close to 100% volumetric efficiency. At altitude, with thinner air, it drops dramatically (maybe 60-70%). A supercharged or turbocharged engine can exceed 100% because the compressor forces more air than would normally flow in. These three efficiencies together define overall engine performance and power output.
exam_tip: Thermal = fuel to work conversion (~30%). Mechanical = friction losses (~10-20%). Volumetric = air mass actually drawn (100% at sea level, lower at altitude).
key_facts: Thermal efficiency: fuel to work conversion
Mechanical efficiency: friction losses
Volumetric efficiency: actual vs theoretical air mass
Thermal typically 30-35%
Mechanical 80-90%
Volumetric drops with altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between typical brake power and power available curves.
concept: A brake power curve plots engine power (vertical axis) against RPM (horizontal axis). At low RPM, power's low. As RPM increases, power increases (more combustion cycles per minute). The curve typically rises steeply initially, then levels off, reaching a peak somewhere around cruise RPM, then might drop slightly or plateau at maximum RPM. A power-available curve (often used in aircraft performance analysis) shows how much power the propeller can absorb at various airspeeds and altitudes. At low airspeed, the propeller demands little power; as airspeed increases (denser air loading the propeller), power required increases dramatically. The intersection of available power (what the engine delivers) and power required (what the propeller needs at that airspeed) determines performance. Below critical altitude, maximum brake power's available at wide-open throttle. Above critical altitude, power drops despite full throttle. In level flight, the aircraft climbs when power available exceeds power required, descends when power required exceeds available, and flies level when they balance. Understanding both curves helps predict aircraft performance across the speed envelope and altitude.
exam_tip: Brake power curve: engine capability vs RPM. Power available: what propeller absorbs vs airspeed. Intersection determines performance balance.
key_facts: Brake power increases with RPM
Peaks near cruise RPM
Power available: propeller demand at each speed
Climb: available exceeds required
Level flight: balanced
Descent: required exceeds available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the general conditions for the most efficient engine operation.
concept: Most efficient operation occurs at cruise power and altitude, where the engine's running at moderate settings (not maximum power, not low power), mixture's properly leaned, and ignition's optimally timed. Specific conditions: manifold pressure set to the value specified for cruise (typically 60-75% power), RPM set for the desired speed-efficiency trade-off (lower RPM = less fuel burn but slower; higher RPM = more speed but more fuel), mixture leaned to the smooth setting just before the power peak (around 17:1 to 18:1 ratio), engine temperature in the green band (not too hot, not too cold), and smooth flying (no excess maneuvring or attitude changes that waste energy). Avoid high-power climbs and descents that stress the engine and waste fuel. Smooth power transitions are more efficient than abrupt throttle changes. Flying at altitude (thinner air but cooler) often provides better efficiency than sea level (denser but hotter). Every engine's different; the POH specifies cruise power settings for best economy or best range. Following the POH exactly gives the best efficiency. Essentially: steady-state moderate-power flight with properly-leaned mixture.
exam_tip: Efficient operation: cruise power, altitude, leaned mixture, smooth flying. Avoid high power and abrupt changes.
key_facts: Cruise power (not max, not low)
Proper altitude (cooler, thinner air)
Mixture leaned to optimum
Smooth flying, minimal maneuvring
Engine temps in green band
POH-specified cruise settings
Steady-state operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of changing altitude on manifold pressure and power output of normally aspirated engines.
concept: A normally-aspirated engine (no supercharger or turbo) draws intake air directly from the atmosphere. As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases, so air density decreases. The engine draws the same volume of air per stroke, but that volume contains fewer oxygen molecules. Manifold pressure (measured on the aircraft's manifold pressure gauge, which shows intake pressure) decreases with altitude. At sea level, manifold pressure might read 28-30 inches of mercury at full throttle. At 10,000 feet, it might read only 18 inches even with throttle wide open. Less air means less combustion force, so brake power decreases. The power decrease is roughly proportional to air density—at 10,000 feet (about 70% sea-level density), you'd have roughly 70% of sea-level power. This is why aircraft can't climb indefinitely; at some critical altitude, power available equals power required (no excess for further climb), and the aircraft reaches its service ceiling. Pilots manage this by descending power settings at altitude (e.g., 'at cruise, reduce to 65% power') to match what the atmosphere allows. Above critical altitude, the manifold pressure gauge becomes a power limiter rather than a throttle—you can't exceed the power available regardless of throttle position.
exam_tip: Higher altitude = lower air density = lower manifold pressure = lower power. Effect: power drop roughly matches density drop.
key_facts: Manifold pressure decreases with altitude
Air density decreases with altitude
Power output decreases proportionally
Roughly 70% power at 10,000 ft
Above critical altitude, power's density-limited
Climb performance worsens higher
Service ceiling: power available = power required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of a simple float-type carburettor.
concept: A float carb's old-school but brilliant. Fuel enters a float chamber (a reservoir) where a floating cork or foam ball sits on the fuel surface. As fuel level rises, the float rises with it, mechanically closing a needle valve that shuts off fuel inflow. Fuel level stays constant around a set level. Below the float chamber is a venturi—a narrowed section in the airflow—where the airspeed increases and pressure drops. This low pressure sucks fuel from the float chamber through a jet (small hole) into the airstream, where it mixes with air. The faster the airflow, the more fuel gets sucked in. Throttle controls the overall airflow; you open it for more power. Simple, mechanical, no electrics needed. You'll still see these on older trainers, especially singles at Ardmore or Christchurch. Fuel injection's replaced them on modern planes.
exam_tip: Float controls fuel level. Venturi creates low pressure. Jet sprays fuel. Throttle controls airflow. Understand the three-way control: float holds level, venturi sucks fuel, throttle sets power.
key_facts: Float chamber maintains fuel level
Venturi creates low pressure zone
Pressure drop sucks fuel through jet
Airspeed controls fuel quantity
Throttle controls total airflow
Mechanical, no electrical components
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: (a) rich and lean; (b) normal workable mixture ratio limits; (c) the chemically correct or stoichiometric ratio; (d) the approximate ratios for maximum power output and best economy.
concept: Mixture ratio is the weight of fuel to weight of air. Rich means more fuel, lean means less fuel (more air relative to fuel). The stoichiometric ratio is the 'perfect' chemical blend: about 15 kilos of air to 1 kilo of fuel, where every fuel molecule has exactly enough oxygen to burn completely. In practice, you operate somewhere between about 8:1 (very rich) and 18:1 (very lean). For maximum power, you want about 12:1—richer than stoichiometric because excess fuel cools the combustion, preventing detonation and allowing more power without knock. For best economy (saving fuel), you want closer to 18:1—very lean—because there's plenty of oxygen and less fuel burned for the same power. The catch: too lean and you lose power and risk detonation; too rich and the mixture won't burn completely, wastes fuel and fouls spark plugs. At takeoff, you typically enrich to prevent knock. At cruise, you lean to save fuel. Every altitude and power setting has an optimal mixture.
exam_tip: Stoichiometric = perfect chemical burn (rarely used in practice). Max power = rich (~12:1). Best economy = lean (~18:1). Understand the tradeoffs.
key_facts: Ratio = fuel weight : air weight
Stoichiometric ≈ 15:1 (theoretical perfect)
Max power ≈ 12:1 (rich of stoichiometric)
Best economy ≈ 18:1 (lean)
Operating range ~8:1 to 18:1
Too rich = waste, fouling; too lean = power loss, detonation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical carburettor mixture setting curve and the operating area where detonation will occur.
concept: A carb mixture curve shows how engine power varies with mixture ratio at a given RPM and altitude. If you plot it, you get a peak—maximum power at a particular mixture ratio (usually around 12:1, rich of stoichiometric). As you lean from that peak, power drops gradually on the economy side. As you enrich past the peak, power also drops because excess fuel cools combustion and increases weight without burning. The detonation zone is the lean side of the power peak—as you lean out, you're running hotter and risking knock. If conditions are hot (high ambient temperature, high power), the detonation zone starts earlier (richer). This is why at high altitude where it's cold, you can lean further without knock. A typical operating curve shows a safe envelope where you can run without detonation; outside that, knock risk rises sharply.
exam_tip: Power peak at rich side of stoichiometric. Lean side = economy. Detonation zone = lean side of power peak. Know why temperature and altitude shift the zone.
key_facts: Power peaks around 12:1 (rich of stoichiometric)
Leaning past peak = economy but losing power
Detonation zone on lean side of peak
High temperature pushes detonation zone richer
High altitude (cold) allows leaner mixture
Safe operating envelope defined by detonation margin
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (a) a venturi; (b) atomization and diffusion; (c) an accelerating system; (d) an idling system; (e) a power enrichment (economizer) system; (f) a mixture control; (g) a cut-off system.
concept: Venturi creates the pressure drop to suck fuel. Atomization breaks fuel into fine droplets for better mixing with air; diffusion spreads those droplets evenly through the airstream. An accelerating system (accelerator pump or valve) squirts extra fuel when you suddenly open the throttle—without it, the engine'd hesitate because air accelerates faster than fuel can be sucked up, creating a momentary lean spike. Idling system provides fuel at low RPM when the venturi effect's too weak to draw enough fuel; it's a separate circuit bypassing the main venturi. A power enrichment or economizer system adds extra fuel at high power (high manifold pressure) to prevent detonation; it's triggered by a diaphragm that senses manifold pressure. Mixture control (manual on older birds, automatic now) lets you adjust fuel quantity as altitude changes—higher you go, less air density, so you manually lean to maintain proper ratio. A cut-off system (fuel selector or mixture full-lean) shuts off fuel flow for engine shutdown. Each system addresses a specific operating condition.
exam_tip: Each system solves a problem: venturi (pressure), atomization (mixing), acceleration (throttle lag), idling (low RPM), enrichment (high power), mixture control (altitude), cut-off (shutdown). Understand why each exists.
key_facts: Venturi: creates suction
Atomization: fine droplet fuel
Diffusion: even distribution
Accelerator: extra fuel on throttle application
Idle system: separate low-RPM fuel circuit
Power enrichment: extra fuel at high power
Mixture control: adjust fuel for altitude
Cut-off: shuts down fuel supply
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the disadvantages of a float type carburettor in an aero-engine.
concept: Float carbs struggle with the variable g-forces in aircraft. A hard turn, rough turbulence, or inverted flight can slosh the fuel around in the float chamber, causing the level to rise or fall erratically. The fuel supply to the engine becomes inconsistent. Carburettor ice is another killer—as fuel evaporates in the venturi, the pressure drop cools the fuel. In humid conditions around freezing, ice can form and block the venturi, starving the engine. Modern carb heaters help, but it's still a threat. Float carbs also suffer from poor distribution of fuel across multiple cylinders—some cylinders get richer mixture than others, leading to uneven running. They're altitude-sensitive because the fuel flow's driven by air pressure drop; at higher altitude where air's thinner, the venturi effect weakens and fuel flow reduces (mixture leans) even without you touching anything. They're slow to respond to sudden power changes—that accelerator pump does its best, but lag is noticeable. Fuel injection solved most of these problems, which is why it's now standard on modern aircraft.
exam_tip: Float carbs have: slosh issues in manoeuvres, carb ice risk, poor fuel distribution, altitude sensitivity, slow response. Know why fuel injection's better.
key_facts: G-forces cause fuel slosh and inconsistency
Carburettor ice risk in humid cold
Poor fuel distribution between cylinders
Altitude sensitivity (mixture leans automatically)
Slow throttle response
Why industry moved to fuel injection
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct use of the mixture control.
concept: The mixture control adjusts how much fuel flows relative to air, allowing you to maintain proper ratio at different altitudes. At sea level (Ardmore), the air's dense and you typically fly at full rich—plenty of fuel because there's plenty of air. As you climb and air gets thinner, the venturi effect weakens and the mixture leans. If you didn't adjust, you'd end up too lean at altitude. So you lean out the mixture control—reducing fuel flow—to maintain the proper ratio. The rule of thumb: climb to altitude, reduce power, lean gradually by pulling the mixture knob while watching the engine response (rough slightly, then smooth again). At cruise you lean for economy. If you're on final approach and descending into denser air, you should enrich the mixture again or the engine might run rough. Never go full lean in flight except to shut down at the end. Proper leaning extends range, prevents plug fouling, and keeps the engine happy. Improper use (too rich or too lean) wastes fuel or risks detonation.
exam_tip: Altitude changes = mixture adjustment. Climb = lean for altitude. Descend = enrich as you enter denser air. Full rich for takeoff. Never abuse mixture.
key_facts: Adjusts fuel-to-air ratio for altitude
Full rich at takeoff (sea level)
Lean gradually during climb
Proper cruise leaning extends range
Enrich when descending into denser air
Prevents fouling and detonation if used correctly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the consequences of operating with over-rich and over-lean mixture settings.
concept: Over-rich (too much fuel): engine runs rough and black-smoky, power drops, fuel consumption skyrockets, and spark plugs foul with excess carbon and soot. You'll see black smoke from the exhaust. The mixture doesn't burn completely, wasting fuel and building up deposits that eventually foul plugs so badly the engine barely runs. Fuel also dilutes the engine oil (raw fuel running down cylinder walls), thinning it and reducing its protective properties. Over-lean (too little fuel): the mixture burns hotter, risking detonation. Engine runs rough and hot, power drops, and sustained lean running can damage pistons and valves. Some cylinders may run so lean they misfire. The engine sounds stressed, RPM becomes inconsistent. Very lean mixture can cause backfiring (fuel igniting in the intake manifold) or popping on deceleration. Both extremes are bad; you want to maintain proper ratio. A slightly rich mixture is usually safer operationally (though wasteful) than too lean.
exam_tip: Over-rich: black smoke, fouling, wasted fuel, rough. Over-lean: hot, detonation risk, rough, potential damage. Know the dangers of both.
key_facts: Over-rich: rough, black smoke, fouling, wasted fuel
Deposits foul spark plugs
Fuel thins engine oil
Over-lean: hot, detonation risk, rough
Can damage pistons and valves
Lean mixture can cause backfire
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor ice, explain the process and atmospheric conditions for the formation of: (a) refrigeration (or fuel) ice; (b) throttle ice; (c) impact ice.
concept: Refrigeration or fuel ice forms because fuel evaporates in the venturi (low pressure zone), cooling the entire carb assembly. In humid conditions near freezing (typically around 0 to -10 degrees C), this cooling causes any moisture in the fuel or air to freeze as ice crystals inside the venturi and fuel passages. Ice blocks airflow or fuel flow, starving the engine. It can happen on seemingly warm days if humidity's high and outside air temp's low enough. Throttle ice forms on the throttle butterfly valve itself (downstream of the venturi, where less evaporative cooling occurs) and can freeze it solid. Impact ice forms in the air intake when ice crystals in the air freeze directly onto the intake surfaces from ram air impact. All three are hazards in high-humidity, cool-to-cold conditions. Carburettor heat (pulling the carb heat lever) draws hot air from around the exhaust manifold into the intake, warming the carb and preventing ice. If you're experiencing symptoms of carb ice (rough running, loss of power, engine fluctuations), apply carb heat immediately.
exam_tip: Fuel ice: evaporative cooling in venturi. Throttle ice: on butterfly valve. Impact ice: from ram air crystals. All need: humidity + cold. Prevention: carb heat.
key_facts: Fuel/refrigeration ice: venturi cooling in humidity + cold
Throttle ice: freezes butterfly valve
Impact ice: atmospheric ice crystals freeze on intake
Typical conditions: 0 to -10°C with humidity
Carburettor heat prevents/fixes icing
Symptoms: rough run, power loss, fluctuations
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the: (a) normal symptoms of carburettor ice formation; (b) the correct use of the carburettor heat control.
concept: Carb ice symptoms start subtle—rough running (missing or uneven), slight power loss, RPM fluctuation if you're holding a constant power setting. As ice accumulates, symptoms worsen: power drops noticeably, roughness increases, engine might even quit in severe cases. You might notice the tachometer fluctuating or the aircraft struggling to maintain altitude. Some pilots notice a slight surge or hesitation that's different from normal engine behaviour. The correct response: apply carburettor heat by pulling the carb heat lever to 'on' or 'heat.' Hot air from the exhaust mani warm the carb, melting the ice. You should see immediate improvement in rough running and power. However, hot air's less dense than cold air, so you might see a momentary drop in power just as you apply heat (this is normal). Continue with carb heat on in icing conditions. In very cold, humid conditions like Christchurch winters, you might run with carb heat on continuously as a precaution. Once you're back in warmer or less humid air, you can turn carb heat off. Never wait for serious symptoms—apply carb heat as soon as you suspect icing.
exam_tip: Symptoms: rough run, power loss, RPM fluctuation. Response: apply carb heat. Know it takes a few seconds to work. In icing conditions, keep it on.
key_facts: Early signs: rough, slight power loss, RPM fluctuation
Worsens: significant power loss, worse roughness
Treatment: apply carb heat lever to 'on'
Hot air melts ice, but less dense (temporary power loss)
May need continuous carb heat in icing conditions
Apply preventatively in suspicious conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe how carburettor icing can prevent the normal operation of an aero-engine.
concept: Carb ice blocks the flow path—either in the venturi where fuel's drawn, or in the throttle valve itself. If the venturi ices, fuel can't be sucked effectively into the airstream, so the mixture leans dramatically and the engine loses power. If throttle ice forms and freezes the butterfly solid, you can't control power with the throttle—it's stuck at whatever position it was at. Either way, you lose smooth power control and the engine runs rough. In extreme cases, if the ice completely blocks the venturi, airflow stops, and the engine quits entirely. This is why carb ice is a real threat—it doesn't just make the engine run poorly; it can actually prevent operation entirely. You can't diagnose the exact icing location by feel, so your response is always the same: apply carb heat and reduce power to let it melt. If ice is severe, you might need to descend into warmer air and keep carb heat on until it clears. A completely blocked carb means engine failure; that's why prevention (staying out of icing conditions or using carb heat prophylactically) is critical.
exam_tip: Ice blocks flow paths. Venturi ice = fuel starvation. Throttle ice = no power control. Either can cause total engine failure. Prevention critical.
key_facts: Ice blocks venturi or throttle passage
Fuel starvation from venturi ice
Loss of power control from throttle ice
Rough running, power loss, or complete failure
May need descent into warmer air
Prevention: carb heat, avoid icing conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of excessive carburettor heat in high output engines.
concept: Excessive carb heat is hot air drawn from around the exhaust manifold, and in high-power engines (or at high power settings), that air can be extremely hot—potentially 300°C or more. If you leave carb heat on continuously at high power, you're running an abnormally hot mixture. This increases combustion temperature significantly, raising detonation risk. You also lose air density benefit (hot air's less dense, less oxygen per volume), so power actually decreases. Additionally, excessive heat can warp or damage carb components, potentially causing fuel vaporization in the lines (vapor lock) where fuel boils before reaching the carb, interrupting supply. Most engines are designed to use carb heat only when needed for ice prevention, not continuously. The rule: use carb heat only in icing conditions or immediately after landing where carb ice risk is highest. Continuous carb heat on high-power flight is a recipe for detonation problems and potential component damage. Some high-performance engines even have carb heat limiting devices to prevent misuse.
exam_tip: Excessive carb heat in high power = detonation risk, power loss, potential vapor lock. Use carb heat only when needed for ice, not routinely in cruise.
key_facts: Hot air from exhaust can exceed 300°C
Raises combustion temperature
Increases detonation risk at high power
Reduces air density (power loss)
Can cause vapor lock in fuel system
Use only for ice prevention, not routinely
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to carburettor air intakes, explain the correct use of: (a) ram air; (b) filtered air; (c) carburettor heat.
concept: Ram air is the air forced into the carb by forward motion; at speed, this ram pressure actually increases air density entering the carb, giving you more oxygen and better power. Most aircraft have a ram air intake that uses this ram effect. Filtered air is the normal intake route, passing through an air filter to remove dust, sand, and other contaminants before the carb. You'd use filtered air when flying in dust storms or sandy conditions, though filtered air introduces a small pressure drop (resistance). Carburettor heat is hot air from the exhaust, used only for icing prevention. The logic: normally you want ram air (extra oxygen at speed) through a filter (cleanliness). In icing conditions, you switch to carb heat to warm the carb and prevent ice. You can't have both simultaneously—it's either ram air or carb heat at any given moment. At Queenstown, if you're flying low and slow on approach, you might lose the ram effect anyway; that's when carb heat becomes essential for ice prevention. During takeoff and climb at high power, you want ram air through filter. During descent or in known icing conditions, you might apply carb heat.
exam_tip: Ram air = ram pressure boost, better power. Filtered air = protection from contaminants. Carb heat = ice prevention. Choose based on conditions.
key_facts: Ram air: forward motion increases pressure/density
Provides power boost at speed
Filtered air: passes through intake filter
Protects engine from contamination
Carburettor heat: for ice prevention
Can't use ram air and carb heat simultaneously
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effects of the partial blockage of the engine air intake filter.
concept: An air intake filter collects dust, sand, pollen, and other particles to protect the engine. As it fills up with contaminants, the filter material becomes increasingly restricted—partial blockage. This restriction increases intake manifold vacuum (suction) below the throttle, which affects the carburettor's metering jets and creates several problems. The mixture leans (less fuel relative to air) because the metering system senses different pressure conditions. You lose power and the engine runs rough. Fuel consumption might drop momentarily (lighter load), but efficiency drops too. If blockage is severe enough, you might not be able to generate enough airflow to draw fuel properly, and the engine might even stall. On some aircraft, a clogged filter will trigger an annunciator light or indicator. The fix: replace the filter. You should check filters regularly, especially if you're operating in dusty conditions (arid areas around the New Zealand interior). Partial blockage might not cause immediate failure, but it degrades performance. A badly blocked filter will prevent engine operation entirely.
exam_tip: Blockage = pressure drop = intake vacuum increases = mixture leans. Result: rough, power loss, fouling potential. Know that partial blockage is insidious.
key_facts: Partial blockage restricts airflow
Increases intake manifold vacuum
Mixture leans (unexpected)
Power loss and rough running
Fuel consumption effects
Complete blockage can stall engine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for a reduction in power when carburettor heat is operated.
concept: When you apply carburettor heat, hot air from the exhaust manifold is drawn into the intake. This hot air is less dense than the cold ambient air you were using before. With lower air density comes fewer oxygen molecules per unit volume. Even though you've got the same volume of air flowing, there's less oxygen in it. The engine responds to this by producing less power—oxygen availability is a limiting factor in combustion. The magnitude of power loss depends on how hot the carb heat air is and how much of it you're using. At sea level on a cool day, the loss might be 5-8%. At altitude or on a hot day when the temperature difference is less pronounced, the loss might be minimal. This is normal and expected behaviour, not a malfunction. Pilots often apply carb heat on approach or in icing conditions and notice the RPM drop; that's exactly what should happen. You accept this power loss as the price of ice prevention. On a high-power flight, you'd avoid continuous carb heat to prevent unnecessarily sacrificing power.
exam_tip: Carb heat = hot air = less dense = fewer oxygen molecules = less power. Normal trade-off for ice prevention. Know it's expected and temporary.
key_facts: Hot air is less dense than cold air
Lower density = fewer oxygen molecules
Results in measurable power loss
5-8% loss typical at sea level
Acceptable trade for ice prevention
Not a malfunction or concern
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical source of heat for the carburettor hot air.
concept: Carburettor heat comes from the engine exhaust manifold, which is scorching hot after combustion—typically 400-600°C. The heat source is a shroud or ducting wrapped around the exhaust manifold (or sometimes around the engine cowling near the hot areas). A thermostatically controlled valve or simple lever-operated valve directs ram air (fresh cold air from the intake) through this hot shroud before sending it to the carburettor. The air passes through the hot metal, warming significantly, and enters the carb as hot air. Some systems use a thermostatic valve that automatically blends hot and cold air to maintain a target temperature; others are manual controls where you simply select 'heat on' or 'off.' The system is simple—no pumps, no electricity required (in most cases)—just physics: ram air passes near hot surfaces and picks up heat. This is why carb heat is most effective at speed (good ram air pressure) and why landing and low-speed approaches create risk (reduced ram air, less heating available if you need it).
exam_tip: Heat source: exhaust manifold shroud. Air passes through, picks up heat. System is mechanical/thermal, usually simple. That's why ram air helps carb heat effectiveness.
key_facts: Exhaust manifold is the heat source
Temperature 400-600°C
Air passes through shroud around manifold
Heats air before carb inlet
Thermostatic or manual control valve
No electricity or pumps typically
Effectiveness varies with ram air available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With regard to exhaust driven turbo charging and mechanically driven supercharging, explain the: (a) advantages; (b) disadvantages.
concept: Superchargers are mechanical compressors belt-driven by the engine (same as an alternator); turbos are driven by exhaust gas flow through a small turbine. Both compress intake air, increasing density and therefore oxygen available for combustion, boosting power. Supercharger advantages: instant response (no lag, directly driven), simpler mechanically, no temperature complexity. Disadvantages: it's power loss (the belt drive consumes engine power to drive the compressor), so you're only recovering some of that power as boost. Turbo advantages: uses waste exhaust energy (minimal power loss to operate), more efficient overall, can produce higher boost levels. Disadvantages: turbo lag (a moment's delay from throttle application to turbine spin-up), heating issues (exhaust is very hot, can damage components, requiring intercoolers), and complexity (more sophisticated). In training aircraft, superchargers are more common (simpler, more predictable). High-altitude or high-performance aircraft often use turbos for efficiency and power recovery.
exam_tip: Supercharger: mechanical, instant, parasitic loss. Turbo: exhaust-driven, lag, efficient. Know trade-offs.
key_facts: Supercharger: belt-driven, instant response, power loss
Turbo: exhaust-driven, lag, recovers waste energy
Both increase air density and power
Supercharger simpler, turbo more efficient
Turbo requires intercooler for heat management
Different training applications
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an exhaust driven turbocharger.
concept: Exhaust gases exiting the cylinders have significant thermal and kinetic energy. A turbocharger captures that energy. The hot exhaust passes through a turbine (similar to a small jet engine turbine) causing it to spin at very high RPM—up to 150,000+ RPM. This turbine is mechanically linked on the same shaft to a compressor that draws intake air and compresses it before sending it to the engine. The compressed air (boost) has higher density and pressure, so more oxygen's available per unit volume for combustion. Higher oxygen means the engine can burn more fuel and produce more power. Unlike a supercharger, the turbo doesn't draw power from the engine; it uses what would otherwise be wasted exhaust energy. The system's quite efficient. The downside: turbo lag from throttle application until the turbine spins up to speed, and high exhaust temperatures requiring cooling.
exam_tip: Exhaust spins turbine → turbine spins compressor → compressor pressurises intake air → more power. Simple concept, elegant system.
key_facts: Exhaust drives turbine at very high RPM
Turbine shaft drives compressor
Compressor pressurises intake air
Higher density air = more combustion power
Uses waste exhaust energy
Minimal parasitic power loss
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the differences between aviation gasoline (AVGAS) and motor gasoline (MOGAS).
concept: AVGAS (aviation gasoline, typically AVGAS 100LL for training and light aircraft) is specifically formulated for aircraft engines. It contains lead as an anti-knock additive (tetraethyl lead), which boosts octane rating. The 'LL' means 'low-lead'—newer formulation with reduced lead (about 0.56 grams per litre) compared to older formulations. AVGAS is manufactured to strict aviation standards (ASTM D910), with controlled volatility, purity, and octane. Motor gasoline (MOGAS) is fuel for cars—designed for automotive engines with different characteristics. MOGAS has much higher volatility (more prone to vapour lock in aircraft at altitude), typically lower octane (premium MOGAS might be 95 octane vs AVGAS 100), and contains ethanol (typically 5-10%) which can corrode fuel system components and separate from water in tanks. MOGAS's cheaper, but it's not approved for most aircraft engines unless specifically authorised by the manufacturer. Using MOGAS in an aircraft engine that's not approved for it can cause detonation, fouling, and fuel system damage. Always use the fuel grade specified in your aircraft's POH.
exam_tip: AVGAS: leads-containing (except unleaded variants), strict aviation spec, 100LL standard. MOGAS: automotive, cheaper, ethanol, not approved for most aircraft. Know the differences and why AVGAS is required.
key_facts: AVGAS 100LL: tetraethyl lead anti-knock, low-lead formula
ASTM D910 spec, controlled volatility
MOGAS: automotive fuel, higher volatility
MOGAS contains ethanol (5-10%)
MOGAS lower octane and cheaper
MOGAS not approved unless specified in POH
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain fuel octane ratings and performance numbers.
concept: Octane rating measures a fuel's resistance to detonation. The scale is based on two reference fuels: iso-octane (which resists detonation well, rated 100) and n-heptane (which detonates easily, rated 0). A fuel with a 100 octane rating behaves like iso-octane. In simple terms, higher octane = higher detonation resistance. AVGAS 100 means the fuel matches 100 octane iso-octane in anti-knock performance. Some high-octane fuels use a performance number system (PN), especially older military specifications, where the number can exceed 100 (like 115/145 PN). The first number is the lean mixture rating; the second is the rich mixture rating. Higher PN means better knock resistance, allowing higher power settings or compression ratios. Your aircraft's POH specifies the minimum octane required—use that grade or higher. Using lower octane risks detonation; using excessively higher octane (whilst not harmful) is wasteful. At places like Ardmore, you'll always use AVGAS 100LL unless your aircraft's approved for something different.
exam_tip: Octane rating = detonation resistance. 100 = iso-octane reference. Higher is better for high-compression engines. Know minimum spec for your aircraft.
key_facts: Octane scale based on iso-octane (100) and n-heptane (0)
Higher octane = more detonation resistance
Performance number: PN-115/145 format
Use minimum octane specified in POH
Higher octane than required is wasteful, not harmful
AVGAS 100LL standard for most training aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the likely result of using a higher grade, or a lower grade, of fuel than that recommended for a given aircraft.
concept: Using higher octane than recommended (e.g., 130/135 in an engine spec'd for 100/130): generally harmless. The engine will run slightly cooler because higher-octane fuel burns more slowly, which is fine. You might see marginally better performance at very high power or altitude, but it's minimal. The downside is cost—premium fuel's expensive, and you're wasting money for negligible benefit. Using lower octane than recommended (e.g., 80/87 in an engine spec'd for 100/130): dangerous. The fuel's more prone to detonation, and at the power settings your engine's designed for, it will knock. Sustained detonation damages pistons, valves, bearings, and rings. You lose power (knock forces you to reduce power), and the engine develops problems. Progressive damage from chronic low-octane usage eventually causes complete failure. If you're at Hamilton and only 80-octane's available, your only option is to operate at reduced power (climb more slowly, etc.) to stay below the detonation threshold. Better to not fly than to risk detonation damage.
exam_tip: Higher octane: safe, unnecessary cost. Lower octane: detonation risk, engine damage. Always use specified grade.
key_facts: Higher octane: safe, marginal benefit, unnecessary cost
Lower octane: detonation risk, progressive damage
Use minimum octane specified in POH
Reduced power operation if lower octane only available
Don't fly if proper fuel unavailable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the caution against using automobile fuel (MOGAS) in an aircraft engine, unless specifically authorised.
concept: MOGAS (automobile petrol) seems logical—it's cheaper and available everywhere. But there are critical issues. First, ethanol content (typically 5-10% in NZ pump fuel) causes corrosion in some aircraft fuel system components (rubber, seals, coatings) and can separate from water in tanks, creating a water layer that gums up fuel system. Second, volatility is too high—MOGAS boils at lower temperatures, creating vapour lock in aircraft fuel systems at altitude (fuel boils to vapour, blocks lines). Third, AVGAS is formulated for aviation use with strict quality controls; MOGAS is automotive and subject to different (less stringent) specs. Fourth, most aircraft engines are approved only for AVGAS; the fuel system and engine aren't validated for MOGAS. Insurance becomes invalid if you use unauthorised fuel and damage results. Some experimental or light-sport aircraft are specifically approved for MOGAS, and there's a whole STCed system for certain models, but that requires STC and POH approval. Unless your aircraft specifically says 'MOGAS approved' in writing, use AVGAS. The cost saving isn't worth the risk to the engine and systems.
exam_tip: MOGAS tempting but dangerous: ethanol corrosion, vapour lock, fuel system damage, engine damage. Caution: don't use unless explicitly approved in POH and STC.
key_facts: MOGAS contains ethanol (corrosion risk)
Higher volatility (vapour lock at altitude)
Not validated for aircraft engines
Insurance invalid if unauthorised fuel used
Some aircraft STC'd for MOGAS (rare)
Use AVGAS unless POH specifically allows MOGAS
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the exhaust manifold.
concept: The exhaust manifold's a system of pipes that collects hot exhaust gases from individual cylinders and combines them into a single flow. Each cylinder has an exhaust port leading into the manifold. As each cylinder completes its power stroke, the exhaust valve opens and burnt gases are expelled into the manifold, which channels them toward the tail pipe (and eventually out the engine exhaust). The manifold's designed to accommodate the pressure pulses from each cylinder's exhaust stroke, minimizing back-pressure. Modern manifolds often have runners of different lengths to optimise scavenging (the removal of exhaust from one cylinder doesn't interfere with another). The manifold gets extremely hot—400-600°C—and in some aircraft, it's used as the heat source for carburettor heat. The manifold must be well-sealed; any leaks allow hot exhaust to escape into the engine compartment, creating fire risk and heat damage.
exam_tip: Manifold collects exhaust from cylinders, combines into single flow, very hot. Seal integrity critical for safety.
key_facts: Collects exhaust from all cylinders
Channels to tail pipe/engine exhaust
Extremely hot (400-600°C)
Used for carb heat source
Must be properly sealed
Back-pressure affects scavenging
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of proper sealing of the exhaust manifold.
concept: The exhaust manifold operates at very high temperatures and pressure. If it leaks, hot gases escape into the engine compartment, creating several hazards. First, fire risk: 400-600°C exhaust in contact with fuel lines, hydraulic fluid, or electrical insulation causes fires. Second, asphyxiation risk: carbon monoxide (CO) can leak into the cabin if the manifold-to-cabin seal fails; CO's odourless and deadly. Third, corrosion: hot exhaust exposure degrades nearby components. Fourth, performance loss: any leak reduces back-pressure, which affects cylinder scavenging and power output. A cracked or loose manifold is a serious maintenance issue. You'd check for discolouration (heat damage) around manifold joints, listen for leaks during run-up, and look for any visible cracks during inspection. Poor seal integrity isn't something you patch; it requires repair or replacement. At pre-flight, if you notice discolouration or smell anything weird (burnt metal, unidentified odours), investigate the manifold.
exam_tip: Proper sealing prevents: fire, CO ingestion, corrosion, performance loss. Cracked manifold is maintenance item, not something to ignore.
key_facts: Prevents hot exhaust escape and fire
Prevents carbon monoxide ingress
Preserves system back-pressure
Protects nearby components from heat
Cracks require repair/replacement
Not a field-fix item
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) tank filler caps and drains; (b) expansion spaces; (c) tank vents; (d) baffles; (e) sumps and drains; (f) fuel quantity detectors; (g) fuel strainers and filters; (h) tank selector valves; (i) cross feed valves; (j) fuel flow meters.
concept: Tank filler caps seal the system and prevent contamination during refuelling—use the correct cap for each tank and check the O-ring seals. Tank drains are at the lowest point; you'll open them during pre-flight to sample fuel and check for water and sediment. Expansion spaces let fuel grow as temperature rises without rupturing the tank—that's why you don't fill tanks completely on a hot day. Tank vents allow air in as fuel is consumed so you don't create a vacuum that collapses the tank or starves the engine. Baffles are internal dividers that prevent fuel sloshing around in rough air and keep the fuel pickup submerged even in a dive or steep turn. Sumps are collection points at the tank bottom where sediment and water settle; you drain these during pre-flight. Fuel quantity detectors (usually floats) tell you how much fuel's in each tank—check them on the ground and monitor them in flight. Tank selectors let you choose which tank feeds the engine; cross-feed valves let you transfer fuel between left and right tanks if one's running low or if an engine fails. Fuel flow meters show fuel consumption rate—essential for managing endurance and detecting abnormal consumption.
exam_tip: Know each component's function and when to check it (pre-flight sampling, tank selection during flight, monitoring quantity). Understand why expansion space matters and what happens if vents are blocked.
key_facts: Filler caps: sealed, O-ring seals checked, correct cap per tank
Tank drains: lowest point, sampled pre-flight for water and sediment
Expansion space: allows fuel thermal expansion without tank rupture
Tank vents: prevent vacuum and maintain pressure as fuel is used
Baffles: prevent fuel sloshing, keep pickup submerged in manoeuvres
Sumps: collection points for sediment and water, drained pre-flight
Quantity detectors: float types, check ground and monitor in flight
Tank selector: choose fuel source; cross-feed: transfer between tanks
Fuel flow meter: shows consumption rate for endurance planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the general rules for refuelling; (b) the correct use of fuel tank dipsticks; (c) the correct use of magna-sticks.
concept: Refuelling rules: aircraft nose into wind to prevent fuel spray, engine off (obviously), master switch off to prevent sparks, ground the refuelling cart and aeroplane to prevent static discharge. Fill on a level surface and don't overfill—leave the expansion space. Check the fuel type matches your engine (Mogas vs avgas, octane rating, etc.). Dipsticks are old-school but still used: you insert them into the filler neck until they bottom, then pull them out and read the fuel level against the graduated marks. They're accurate but they're a physical inspection, not electronic—you've got to do it. Magna-sticks (or fuel strut sticks) are plastic dipsticks with floating magnets inside. You fill the stick with a sample of fuel from the tank drain, and magnets at different levels show you the water level. If water's present, it settles at the bottom and the magnet sinks to it. The marks tell you the fuel grade and any water contamination. It's faster than waiting for sediment to settle, and it gives you confidence the fuel's clean. Some FBOs still prefer the traditional jar method—letting fuel sit in a clear container so water settles visibly—but magna-sticks are becoming standard.
exam_tip: Refuelling: level surface, nose into wind, ground the aircraft, no spark sources, correct fuel type. Dipstick: insert fully, read graduated marks. Magna-stick: fill from tank sample, note magnet position for water and fuel grade.
key_facts: Refuelling procedure: level surface, nose into wind, engines off, master off
Ground aircraft and refuelling cart to prevent static discharge
Confirm fuel type and octane rating before refuelling
Don't overfill; leave expansion space
Dipstick: insert to bottom, read graduated scale on withdrawal
Magna-stick: fill with fuel sample, magnets show water level and fuel grade
Fuel should be clear and bright; contamination requires tank draining
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct procedures to be used for carrying out fuel quality checks.
concept: Fuel quality checks are simple but non-negotiable. At refuelling, you've already verified the grade. On pre-flight, you'll drain a sample from the lowest sump point of each tank into a clear container (usually a fuel sampler cup at the refuelling point). Let it sit for a few seconds; look for water settling at the bottom—it'll be cloudy or a distinct layer. Check for sediment (particles, rust, debris) which should be absent. The fuel should be clear and bright, with the correct colour for the grade: Avgas 100LL is typically light blue, Avgas 100 is green, Jet A is clear to pale straw. Any discolouration, cloudiness, or sediment means the tank needs flushing or fuel dumping. On a multi-tank aircraft like a twin, check every tank—you might find contamination in one wing tank but not the other. If you find water, don't fly; declare the aircraft unserviceable and contact maintenance. This isn't a shortcut—it's the difference between a normal flight and an engine failure at low level.
exam_tip: Sample every tank pre-flight. Look for water (cloudiness at bottom), sediment (particles), and correct colour. Cloudy fuel = no-go. Know the colour standards for fuel grades used in NZ.
key_facts: Drain fuel sample from lowest sump point of each tank
Sample into clear container; let settle briefly
Look for water (cloudiness), sediment (particles), and correct colour
Avgas 100LL: light blue; Avgas 100: green; Jet A: clear to straw
Any cloudiness or sediment means tank requires maintenance
Check every tank on multi-tank aircraft
Fuel contamination is a no-go condition
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of water in a fuel sample.
concept: Water in fuel is the enemy. It'll corrode fuel system components, it'll plug injectors and filters, and it can freeze in the fuel lines at altitude, blocking fuel flow mid-flight. When you drain a fuel sample and water's present, you'll see a distinct layer at the bottom of the container—it looks cloudy or milky if it's emulsified (mixed with fuel), or it might be a clear layer below the fuel. Tiny droplets suspended throughout the fuel make it look hazy or turbid. On a hot day, you might see no water initially, but as the sample cools in your hand, water that was vapour condenses out and becomes visible. If you let the sample sit for a minute, water will settle to the bottom and you'll see it clearly. The colour changes too: normal fuel is bright and clear; fuel with water is dull, cloudy, or has a distinct separation. In serious contamination, you might even smell the water—it has that distinct odour. Don't take any chances: if you see water, the aircraft doesn't fly, and you need to get maintenance involved to find the source (usually a leaking fuel cap or a tank vent failure in damp conditions).
exam_tip: Water indicators: cloudiness, haze, distinct bottom layer, colour change (dull instead of bright). Let sample sit to let water settle. Any visible water = no-go.
key_facts: Water appears as cloudiness, haze, or distinct layer at bottom of sample
Water may be emulsified (mixed throughout) or separated (bottom layer)
Cool sample in hand; water vapour condenses as temperature drops
Fuel with water looks dull or turbid instead of bright and clear
Serious contamination has visible water column at bottom
Water in fuel risks filter blockage, injector damage, and fuel line freeze
Any visible water is a no-go condition
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of a blocked fuel tank vent in a gravity feed fuel system.
concept: A blocked vent in a gravity-feed system creates a vacuum in the tank as fuel is consumed. The engine will run fine at first, but as fuel level drops, the vacuum gets stronger and eventually strangles the fuel flow. You'll notice the engine running rough, losing power, and if it's bad enough, cutting out completely. The problem is it's intermittent—climb or descend slightly, maybe the siphoning effect changes, and fuel flows again. You might chalk it up to carburettor ice or fuel starvation from leaning the mixture. But the real giveaway is that fuel quantity gauge starts fluctuating wildly because the float is bouncing around as the vacuum sucks it. If you suspect a blocked vent mid-flight, you might try a gentle climb or descent to change the pressure differential, or you might open a cabin air vent to let air into the cabin, which could relieve some back-pressure. But really, it's a land-and-sort-it situation. On the ground, check that the vent isn't clogged with dirt, ice, or a blocked valve.
exam_tip: Blocked vent symptoms: engine rough or cutting out as fuel level drops, fluctuating fuel gauge, progressive power loss. On the ground, check vent for blockage or ice.
key_facts: Blocked vent creates vacuum as fuel is consumed
Engine roughness and power loss as vacuum builds
Fuel quantity gauge fluctuates erratically
Problem worsens as fuel level drops (more vacuum)
Changing altitude may temporarily relieve symptom
Vents can block with dirt, ice, or internal failure
Land immediately if blocked vent suspected
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the importance of correct management of fuel selection.
concept: On a twin-engine aeroplane, you've got multiple fuel tanks—typically left wing, right wing, and maybe a centre tank. You've got a selector valve on the flight deck that lets you choose which tank feeds each engine. Get this wrong and you'll have an engine failure, or worse, an asymmetric thrust situation where one engine's running and the other's starved. Proper fuel management means: know your tank capacities, monitor which tank is supplying each engine, understand your fuel consumption rates, and plan your takeoff sequence. Some aircraft require you to burn from specific tanks in a specific order to maintain centre of gravity. If you're flying out of Hamilton and you've got an asymmetric load (say, full right tank and half-full left), you need to be deliberate about which tank feeds which engine so you don't end up with both engines trying to feed from the depleted side. On some operations, you'll cross-feed in flight to balance consumption. And critically, if one engine dies, you need to immediately switch that engine to an alternate tank (if available) or declare the situation and plan your approach. Sloppy fuel selection has killed a lot of pilots—it's not a details-to-ignore kind of thing.
exam_tip: Know your tank layout and capacities. Plan takeoff fuel sequence. Monitor consumption. Maintain CG balance. If one engine dies, switch to alternate fuel source immediately. Document fuel selection in your pre-flight checklist.
key_facts: Multi-tank aircraft require deliberate tank selection
Wrong selection causes single-engine failure or dual failure
Tank selector valves must be properly identified and used
Fuel consumption must be monitored to maintain aircraft balance
Asymmetric fuel loads require cross-feed planning
Engine failure due to fuel selection requires immediate alternate tank selection
Fuel management is part of pre-takeoff and in-flight checklist
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions recommended in the case of loss of power through faulty fuel selection.
concept: You've selected the wrong tank, one engine's lost power, and suddenly you're flying an asymmetric twin—not a fun situation. First thing: don't panic. Immediately check your fuel selector: it's probably in the wrong position. Switch it to the correct tank, and usually the engine will pick up within 5–10 seconds as fuel pressure builds. If the engine doesn't respond, try the opposite tank if you've got alternatives, or try your crossfeed valve if available. If you've got an engine failure that you can't recover—the selector's already in the right position and fuel's available elsewhere—then you're declaring a single-engine emergency with ATC. You've lost asymmetric thrust, so trim carefully, reduce power on the good engine to maintain control, and plan your descent to the nearest suitable aerodrome. Call it early so ATC can vector you and prepare the airfield. Don't try to climb or maintain altitude on one engine in a twin with a significant loading—you'll just burn fuel inefficiently and stress the airframe. Get down safely.
exam_tip: Wrong fuel selection = engine loss. Immediately check and correct selector position. Verify fuel's actually in that tank (gauge might show empty). If engine doesn't recover, declare emergency and plan landing at nearest suitable aerodrome. Manage asymmetric thrust carefully.
key_facts: Check fuel selector immediately when engine power is lost
Switch to correct tank; engine usually responds within 5–10 seconds
Confirm fuel exists in the selected tank (check gauge)
Try crossfeed if available
If unrecovered, declare single-engine emergency
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Manage asymmetric thrust: reduce good engine power, trim carefully
Don't attempt to climb on one engine unnecessarily
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical methods of cross-feeding fuel.
concept: Cross-feeding is transferring fuel from one tank to another, usually from a wing tank to the opposite wing tank. On many twins, you've got a crossfeed selector valve that lets you feed a single engine from the opposite wing tank. Why would you do this? If one wing tank's running low and the other's still full, you can cross-feed to balance consumption and maintain centre of gravity. If an engine's on the verge of failure and you've got fuel in the opposite tank, you can try cross-feeding to see if the problem's a blocked feed line instead of total fuel starvation. Some aircraft have manual crossfeed valves; others have electric selectors on the panel. The procedure is simple: move the crossfeed selector to 'on' or to the desired position, monitor that the engine stays running, and watch your fuel gauges to ensure you're actually consuming from the opposite tank. Cross-feeding during cruise helps balance aircraft loading on long flights. Some operations require specific cross-feed sequences—check your flight manual. And remember: you can only cross-feed if you've got fuel in both tanks and the crossfeed system is serviceable. You can't pull fuel from an empty tank.
exam_tip: Crossfeed transfers fuel between left and right tanks. Used to balance consumption and maintain CG. Operate selector, monitor engine and fuel gauges. Confirm fuel actually transfers. Know your aircraft's crossfeed valve location and operation.
key_facts: Crossfeed transfers fuel between wing tanks to maintain balance
Valve is either manual or electric selector on flight deck
Monitor fuel gauges to confirm crossfeed operation
Engine should stay running; observe no fluctuation
Used during cruise for extended flights to manage CG
Can help if one feed line is blocked (try crossfeed to opposite tank)
Requires fuel in both tanks; can't transfer from empty tank
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: AVTUR vs AVGAS identification and fuel contamination precautions
concept: Distinguishing between AVTUR and AVGAS and identifying fuel contamination hazards. AVGAS comes dyed (100LL is blue, 100 is green) and carries red lettering on white decals; AVTUR (jet fuel) is clear to straw-coloured with white lettering on black decals. You'll never put AVTUR in a piston engine—it causes detonation and catastrophic failure. AVGAS can go in the helicopter, but you've got to get it right.
exam_tip: Know the colours and decals; questions often ask what happens if you use the wrong fuel type.
key_facts: AVGAS (100LL) is dyed blue; 100 is dyed green
AVTUR is clear to straw-coloured
AVGAS decals: red lettering on white background
AVTUR decals: white lettering on black background
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Fuel contamination detection and water indication methods
concept: Water and contamination in fuel tanks is one of your biggest headaches. You detect it visually (cloudy or bubbly samples), with water-detection paste, or by letting a fuel sample settle. The paste turns colours when water's present—it's a quick preflight check at places like Ardmore or Hamilton. Contamination can block fuel filters and starve your engine, so check before every first flight of the day.
exam_tip: Examiners love asking how you'd detect water. Remember: visual inspection, water paste, settling.
key_facts: Water detected visually: cloudy or bubbly sample appearance
Water-detection paste changes colour in presence of water
Settling method: allow sample to sit and observe separation
Always sample fuel before first flight and after refuelling
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Drum stock fuelling procedures and precautions
concept: When you're fuelling from drum stock at a remote site, you've got to be careful. Filter the fuel as you pour it, let it settle in the tank for 72 hours before using it (water settles to the bottom), check for water sediment, and use a chamois filter for an extra layer of protection. It's tedious, but drummed fuel can pick up all sorts of contamination during storage and handling.
exam_tip: Remember the 72-hour settling period and why—it lets water drop out of suspension.
key_facts: Always filter drum fuel during transfer
Drum fuel must settle for 72 hours before first use
Check drum for water sediment after settling period
