Use chamois filter as final filtration stage
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Fuel sampling and checking procedures
concept: Fuel checks are non-negotiable. Sample from the lowest point of each tank using a transparent container so you can see any cloudiness or contamination. Do it before your first flight of the day and after every refuelling. Hold the sample up to the light, shake it to see if bubbles form (air contamination), and watch for any sediment settling. It takes two minutes and could save your engine.
exam_tip: Questions ask what you'd do if you found water in the sample—you'd drain and flush that tank.
key_facts: Sample from lowest point of each fuel tank
Use transparent container for visual inspection
Sample before first flight and after refuelling
Look for cloudiness, bubbles (air), or sediment
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Refuelling rules and dipstick procedures
concept: When you're refuelling, follow the rules: engine off, eliminate static electricity (bonding cables), use proper filter equipment, and top up tanks as required. Dipsticks tell you how much fuel you've got by marking the tank level—check the markings carefully because different aircraft have different calibrations. Some helicopters like the R22 have sight glasses instead, which are quicker to read.
exam_tip: Know the difference between sight glasses and dipsticks; examiners ask which is more accurate.
key_facts: Engine must be off during refuelling
Static electricity hazard: use bonding cables
Use filtered fuel supply equipment
Dipsticks vary by aircraft; check markings carefully
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Blocked fuel vent symptoms and effects in gravity-feed systems
concept: In a gravity-feed fuel system, the vent has to breathe, otherwise fuel can't flow properly. If the vent gets blocked—by ice, debris, or contamination—a vacuum builds up in the tank and fuel flow just stops. Your engine starves and dies, typically during climb or manoeuvring when you need power most. You'd hear it getting rough before it quits, and the only fix is to get down and clear the blockage. That's why you check vents during preflight.
exam_tip: Examiners love asking what happens if the vent blocks. Answer: vacuum builds, fuel flow stops, engine starves.
key_facts: Blocked vent prevents air entering tank as fuel leaves
Vacuum builds in tank and restricts fuel flow
Fuel starvation occurs during power demands (climb, manoeuvre)
Typically noticed as engine roughness before complete stoppage
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Crossfeed fuel system operation and procedures
concept: Crossfeed systems let you transfer fuel between tanks or feed the engine from a tank that normally wouldn't feed it. You'd use this in cruise to manage your centre of gravity—shifting fuel from one tank to another as you burn fuel keeps the CG in limits. You check crossfeed operation by turning on the crossfeed valve, monitoring the fuel gauges to confirm the transfer, and ensuring the engine stays running without fluctuation. You need fuel in both tanks for it to work.
exam_tip: Don't forget: crossfeed requires fuel in both tanks. If one tank is empty, it won't help.
key_facts: Crossfeed valve allows transfer between tanks
Used in cruise to manage centre of gravity during flight
Check crossfeed by monitoring fuel gauges for flow confirmation
Engine must remain stable during crossfeed operation
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Fuel system management and emergency procedures
concept: Managing fuel isn't just about having enough—it's about balance, contamination prevention, and knowing what to do when something goes wrong. You're constantly checking gauges, calculating remaining time, and thinking ahead about alternates. At Christchurch or Queenstown, you've got distance to think about. If fuel's contaminated, you declare an emergency and divert to the nearest suitable aerodrome. If a tank won't feed, you use crossfeed or shut that tank off. Fuel management is a continuous task from engine start to parking.
exam_tip: Questions test your thinking about fuel management decisions. What would you do if one gauge reads wrong?
key_facts: Continuously monitor fuel quantity and quality
Calculate time remaining based on consumption rate
Fuel contamination is grounds for precautionary landing
Know your aerodrome alternates and fuel reserves required
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for fitting independent dual ignition systems to engines.
concept: Aircraft engines use two independent magnetos (not one big one) for several critical reasons. First, redundancy: if one magneto fails, the other continues firing the plugs, so you've still got partial power and can keep flying (though degraded). Second, safety: losing ignition means losing the engine; dual ignition significantly reduces that risk. Third, reliability: magnetos are mechanical and wear; two independent systems spread the risk. Fourth, better engine scavenging and power: each magneto fires its own set of spark plugs, so you get two sparks per power stroke (one from each magneto), creating a broader burn and more complete combustion. This improves power output and efficiency. Fifth, improved starting: with two magnetos firing, starting's easier. The trade-off: complexity and cost, but the reliability and safety benefits are worth it. Every piston trainer you fly has dual mags. The ignition switch typically has positions: off, left mag only, right mag only, and both. During magneto checks before flight, you test each mag independently to ensure both are functioning.
exam_tip: Dual mags: redundancy if one fails, better combustion (two sparks), improved starting. Why aircraft don't use single large magneto.
key_facts: Redundancy: one fails, one continues
Better combustion from dual sparks
Improved starting capability
Increases safety and reliability
Each mag fires its own plugs
Tested independently during run-up
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: List the essential components of an ignition system.
concept: An ignition system starts with the magneto (generates electrical power and timing). The magneto produces high-tension (HT) output that goes to a distributor (routes the spark to the right cylinder at the right time). From there, ignition harness (high-tension leads, aka spark plug wires) carries the spark to each spark plug. The spark plugs (in each cylinder) create the actual spark gap where ignition occurs. An impulse coupling (mechanical device) on the magneto ensures a hot spark during starting by retarding spark timing momentarily. Some systems also include a starting vibrator that helps establish initial oscillation. Capacitors in the magneto and wiring help sharpen the spark. The magneto itself contains a rotating magnet, coils, and electrical components that generate and time the spark. All these components work together; failure of any single component means loss of ignition and loss of that magneto (hence the need for dual systems).
exam_tip: Magneto, distributor, ignition harness, spark plugs, impulse coupling. Know each component's role.
key_facts: Magneto: power generation and timing
Distributor: routes spark to cylinders
Ignition harness: spark plug wires
Spark plugs: create ignition spark
Impulse coupling: aids starting
Capacitors: sharpen spark
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (a) an aircraft magneto; (b) the distributor; (c) ignition harness (high tension leads); (d) spark plugs; (e) impulse couplings.
concept: Magneto: A rotating magnet (rotor) inside the magneto generates magnetic field. This rotating field induces voltage in stationary coils (pickup coils) inside the magneto. The voltage is low initially, then passes through a capacitor circuit that builds voltage, creating a high-voltage pulse (thousands of volts). This pulse is timed to occur just before each cylinder's top dead centre. Distributor: Rotates in sync with the engine and has contact points for each cylinder. As the distributor rotor spins, it sequentially connects the magneto's high-voltage output to the correct spark plug wires in firing order. Ignition harness (spark plug wires): These are high-voltage cables connecting the distributor output to each spark plug. They're shielded to prevent electrical interference and are designed to withstand thousands of volts. Spark plugs: Inside each cylinder, two electrodes (centre and ground) are separated by a small gap. The high-voltage pulse jumps the gap, creating the spark that ignites the mixture. The gap spacing's critical—too small and spark's weak, too large and won't fire. Impulse coupling: A mechanical device on the magneto that temporarily retards spark timing during engine start (slow crank). Once the engine fires and speeds up, the impulse coupling disengages and normal advance applies. This ensures a hot spark even at low crank speeds.
exam_tip: Magneto generates high-voltage pulse. Distributor routes to correct plug. Wires carry pulse. Plugs create spark gap. Impulse coupling aids starting.
key_facts: Magneto: rotating magnet induces voltage
Capacitor circuit boosts to high voltage
Distributor: sequentially routes to each plug
Ignition harness: shielded HV cables
Spark plugs: gap where spark jumps
Impulse coupling: retards timing for starting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of fitting an impulse coupling to a magneto.
concept: During engine start, the starter motor cranks the engine very slowly (perhaps 50-100 RPM). At that speed, the magneto's rotating magnet spins slowly, generating very weak voltage—barely enough to fire the spark plugs reliably. You'd get a weak, late spark, making starting difficult or impossible. An impulse coupling solves this by mechanically retarding spark timing during start. As the engine cranks slowly, the impulse coupling holds back the magneto's rotor (delays its rotation slightly), allowing magnetic field to build up more before the spark fires. When the spark finally does occur, it's much hotter and more reliable. Once the engine fires and speed increases above about 1000 RPM, centrifugal weights inside the impulse coupling overcome the retard mechanism, and the coupling disengages. Normal spark advance then applies automatically. So the impulse coupling's purpose: ensure hot, reliable spark during the slow cranking of engine start, then get out of the way once the engine's running. Without it, cold starting would be much harder.
exam_tip: Impulse coupling retards timing during slow crank, ensuring hot spark for starting. Disengages once engine speeds up. Why starting's reliable.
key_facts: Retards spark timing during start
Allows field buildup for hotter spark
Engages at slow crank speed
Disengages around 1000 RPM
Ensures reliable cold start
Automatically managed, pilot doesn't control it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason ignition is timed to occur before top dead centre (TDC).
concept: Combustion doesn't happen instantaneously. When the spark plug fires, it ignites the fuel-air mixture, but the flame front takes several milliseconds to propagate through the combustion chamber. If you waited until the piston reached top dead centre (TDC) to fire the spark, by the time combustion developed, the piston would already be starting downward, and you'd waste the combustion energy. Instead, spark fires before TDC (typically 20-30 degrees before), so by the time the piston reaches TDC and starts its power stroke downward, combustion is fully developed and creates maximum pressure right when you need it—pushing the piston hard from the very start of the downstroke. This is called 'spark advance.' The amount of advance varies with engine speed and operating conditions; faster engines need more advance (longer lead time for combustion), slower engines need less. Modern high-altitude engines might have 35+ degrees of advance at high RPM. Getting spark timing right is critical for efficiency and power.
exam_tip: Spark before TDC because combustion takes time. Peak pressure needed right at start of power stroke = maximum force = most power.
key_facts: Spark before TDC = spark advance
Allows time for combustion development
Peak pressure at right moment = max power
Advance increases with engine speed
Typical 20-35 degrees before TDC
Critical for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the ignition switch(es) in the cockpit.
concept: The ignition switch is typically a 4-position rotary switch (sometimes 5 on complex aircraft): OFF, LEFT MAG, RIGHT MAG, and BOTH. OFF: both magnetos are grounded and disabled; no ignition. LEFT MAG: left magneto only is active; right magneto is grounded. RIGHT MAG: right magneto only is active; left magneto is grounded. BOTH: both magnetos active—normal flight operation. (Some aircraft have a START position where the magnetos and starter motor are energised together.) During start, you'd typically place the switch in BOTH, and the starter motor cranks the engine while both magnetos provide spark. Once running, you stay in BOTH. During magneto checks, you momentarily switch to LEFT or RIGHT individually to test each one independently. The spark plug wires and distributor are wired so that each magneto's spark always goes to the correct plugs; the switch just controls power to each magneto. A failed magneto or switch would leave one magneto active (the other grounded), giving you asymmetrical ignition—rough running but still flight-capable.
exam_tip: OFF, LEFT, RIGHT, BOTH positions. BOTH for normal flight. LEFT/RIGHT for testing. OFF for security. Know the switch function.
key_facts: OFF: both grounded, no ignition
LEFT: left mag active, right grounded
RIGHT: right mag active, left grounded
BOTH: both active (normal flight)
START: adds starter motor (some aircraft)
Used for mag checking and starting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Outline the correct procedures to be followed during magneto checks.
concept: Before every flight, you perform magneto checks during the run-up to ensure both magnetos are functioning correctly. Procedure: first, run the engine at a safe RPM (typically 1000-1500 RPM, as specified in the POH). Ensure the aircraft is in a stable configuration (trim set, flight surfaces free). With the engine running smoothly at BOTH, note the RPM. Slowly move the ignition switch to LEFT MAG and note the RPM drop. The drop should be modest—typically 50-150 RPM depending on the aircraft. Check that the drop is smooth and the engine doesn't surge or miss badly. Return to BOTH and wait for the engine to stabilise. Repeat with RIGHT MAG. The right-side RPM drop might be slightly different from the left (that's normal, as magnetos aren't perfectly matched). Critical checks: the RPM drop on each side shouldn't exceed the aircraft's limits (typically 150 RPM, but check the POH). The difference between left and right drops shouldn't exceed about 50 RPM. If one mag is significantly worse than the other, or if there's rough running, surging, or backfiring, there's a problem—don't fly. Also check that returning to BOTH smoothly increases RPM back to the original reading. Never test magnetos with a passenger unfamiliar with the procedure; they might get alarmed at the RPM drop.
exam_tip: Mag checks: smooth RPM drop on each side (50-150 RPM typical). Right-left difference max ~50 RPM. No surging. Back to BOTH should restore RPM smoothly.
key_facts: Perform at 1000-1500 RPM stable setting
Test LEFT, then RIGHT individually
Note RPM drop on each side
Drop should be smooth and modest
Right-left difference max ~50 RPM
Return to BOTH should restore RPM
Rough or excessive drop = maintenance issue
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of various problems manifested during a magneto check.
concept: During mag checks, you observe several potential problems. Excessive RPM drop (more than 150 RPM on either side, or asymmetric—right much worse than left): indicates magneto degradation, worn brushes, or carbon buildup. Rough running or missing when on one mag: spark plugs might be fouled, or the magneto's misfiring. Surging (RPM rises and falls irregularly): might indicate a bad capacitor or intermittent contact in the magneto. Backfiring (popping sound from the exhaust): timing issue or magneto delivering spark at wrong time. Inability to return to smooth operation after mag check: again suggests magneto or plug issues. A dead mag (no RPM drop on one side, or massive drop close to shut-down): magneto's completely failed. Any of these warrant maintenance investigation. Some issues (fouled spark plugs from over-rich running) can be cleared by leaning and running at higher power for a bit; others (internal magneto failure) require replacement. Never ignore mag check problems—they indicate a safety issue that needs addressing before flight.
exam_tip: Excessive drop, asymmetry, rough, surge, backfire, dead mag = problems. Know which are critical (mag failure) vs potentially clearable (fouled plugs).
key_facts: Excessive RPM drop: magneto wear
Asymmetry: one mag worse than other
Rough/missing: fouled plugs or misfire
Surging: capacitor or contact issues
Backfiring: timing problem
Dead mag: complete failure
Some clearable, some need maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of spark plug fouling and the methods of clearing such fouling.
concept: Spark plug fouling happens when the spark gap becomes blocked by deposits, preventing the spark from jumping. Causes: running too rich (excess fuel creates carbon deposits and wets the plug), operating at low power for extended periods (plug doesn't get hot enough to self-clean), cold-starting (rich mixture to aid starting leaves deposits), or aged spark plugs (gap widens, deposits accumulate). Fouled plugs create a no-spark condition and misfire, rough running, and power loss. Clearing methods: lean the mixture (reduce fuel flow to burn off deposits and let plugs heat up), increase power (climb to cruise power where plugs self-clean—the hotter the operating temperature, the better plugs clean), or rough-running procedures (some POHs specify running at higher RPM to blow out fouling). If fouling persists, the plugs need actual cleaning or replacement by maintenance. At Hamilton or Christchurch, if you start cold and run too rich too long, you might foul plugs before even taking off; the fix is to lean and run at higher power to self-clean. Regular proper mixture management prevents fouling.
exam_tip: Over-rich = fouling. Fix: lean mixture, climb to cruise power for self-cleaning. Know why proper mixture management's important.
key_facts: Over-rich running = primary cause
Low-power operation = deposits accumulate
Fouling blocks spark gap
Symptoms: misfire, rough, power loss
Fix: lean mixture, increase power
Plugs self-clean at higher operating temp
Persistent fouling needs maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Spark plug fouling causes and clearing methods
concept: Spark plug fouling happens when you're running too rich, burning leaded AVGAS with deposits building up, or your engine's worn rings are letting oil into the combustion chamber. You clear fouled plugs by leaning the mixture, running at higher RPM on the ground for a few minutes to burn off the deposits, or physically pulling and cleaning the plugs if it's bad. It's a maintenance item, but you can nurse it until you get back to the field.
exam_tip: Know what causes fouling (rich mixture, lead deposits, oil fouling) and your clearing options.
key_facts: Rich mixture operation allows lead and carbon deposits to accumulate
Worn piston rings cause oil fouling in combustion chamber
Clear fouling by leaning mixture gradually
High-RPM ground run burns off accumulated deposits
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the main functions of an engine oil system.
concept: Engine oil does three main jobs: it lubricates all the moving parts—bearings, pistons, crankshaft, camshaft—so they slide smoothly without excessive friction and heat. Second, it cools components by carrying heat away from the hottest parts (like the piston underside) back to the cooler sump and then the oil cooler. Third, it cleans: the oil circulates through the engine, suspending dirt and contaminants, carrying them to the filter where they're trapped. As the oil ages and accumulates sludge, it loses all these properties, which is why regular oil changes are essential. On a piston twin sitting at Ardmore in summer heat, the oil's working hard—if you let it get too hot or too thick with gunk, you'll see rising oil temperature and falling pressure, and eventually you'll have bearing damage. That's unrecoverable.
exam_tip: Oil lubricates, cools, and cleans. Pressure indicates flow; temperature indicates heat load. Monitor both continuously. Low pressure or high temperature during flight means land soon.
key_facts: Oil lubricates engine bearings, pistons, and rotating parts
Oil transfers heat from hot engine components to cooler sump
Oil suspends contaminants and carries them to the filter
Regular oil changes remove accumulated sludge and contaminants
Oil viscosity changes with temperature; thicker when cold, thinner when hot
Low oil pressure indicates poor lubrication; high temperature indicates overload
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of temperature on the viscosity and lubrication qualities of oil.
concept: Oil viscosity is its thickness, and it changes dramatically with temperature. Cold oil is thick and sluggish—on a winter morning at Christchurch, you might have to wait longer before the oil pressure stabilises because thick oil doesn't flow easily through the bearings. Hot oil is thin and watery—high altitude or a long descent in summer heat makes the oil fluid, which is good for flow but bad for the film between bearing surfaces. The viscosity needs to be in a sweet spot: thick enough to maintain a protective film between bearings (low viscosity = poor lubrication = bearing wear), thin enough to flow and circulate (high viscosity = poor flow = pressure drop and cooling problems). That's why you use specific oil grades: a SAE 40 oil stays relatively consistent across temperature ranges, while a SAE 10 oil is thin and flows better in winter but might be too thin in summer. Modern ashless dispersant oils (used in aviation piston engines) maintain viscosity better across temperature swings than straight mineral oil, which is why you use them.
exam_tip: Cold oil: thick, high pressure initially, slow to warm. Hot oil: thin, low pressure, poor bearing film. Know your oil grade and acceptable pressure/temperature ranges. Monitor both gauges.
key_facts: Oil thickens when cold; thins when hot
Cold engine: high oil pressure, long warm-up needed
Hot engine: low oil pressure, risk of bearing damage
Oil viscosity must balance flow (thin) and bearing protection (thick)
SAE ratings (10, 30, 40, etc.) define oil weight
Multi-grade oils (e.g., 15W-50) adapt to temperature changes
Ashless dispersant oils maintain viscosity better than mineral oils
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Compare the features of the ashless dispersant (AD) oils used in piston aero-engines with straight mineral oil and detergent oils.
concept: Ashless dispersant oils are the gold standard for piston aero-engines. They don't leave ash residue when they burn (minerals and detergents do), which means cleaner combustion, less sludge buildup, and longer engine life. The 'dispersant' part means they hold contaminants in suspension so they don't settle and form gunk—they stay suspended until the filter catches them. Straight mineral oil is basic: it lubricates but it leaves ash, it doesn't hold contaminants well, and it breaks down faster. It's outdated. Detergent oils (used in cars) have additives that clean engine deposits, which sounds good, but in aircraft piston engines, they can cause issues: they leave ash residue, they react with aviation fuel additives in ways we don't want, and they're not certified for use in aircraft. Your flight manual will specify ashless dispersant (usually Tempus, Aeroshell, or equivalent). Don't deviate—mixing oils or using the wrong grade can void your engine warranty and potentially damage the engine. The AD oils are more expensive, but they're designed specifically for our engines and they extend time between overhauls.
exam_tip: Use only ashless dispersant oils in piston aircraft engines. Never mix grades or use automotive detergent oils. AD oils: no ash, hold contaminants, longer intervals. Know the brand and grade specified in your POH.
key_facts: Ashless dispersant (AD) oils are approved for piston aero-engines
AD oils don't leave ash residue; combustion is cleaner
Dispersant additive suspends contaminants for filter removal
Straight mineral oils leave ash and break down faster (obsolete)
Detergent oils (automotive) are not approved for aircraft use
Mixing oil grades or types is not permitted
AD oils extend time between engine overhauls
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to oil grades, differentiate between the commercial aviation numbers and SAE ratings.
concept: You'll see two numbering systems on oil bottles. Commercial aviation numbers like 15, 30, 40, 50 are what the aero industry uses—they're based on viscosity at specific temperatures. SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) numbers—SAE 10, 20, 30, etc.—are similar but they're automotive standards. They roughly correspond: aviation 30 ≈ SAE 20–30W, aviation 40 ≈ SAE 40, etc. But here's the key: don't assume they're identical. Your aircraft POH will specify aviation numbers, and that's what you use. If it says 'aero oil 50', you buy aviation oil 50, not SAE 50W. The 'W' in automotive oils (15W, 20W) means winter grade—it's a multi-viscosity oil that's thin when cold and thick when hot. Aviation oils sometimes use similar naming but the specs are different. The short version: follow your POH exactly. It'll say something like 'Tempus aero oil 40' or 'Aeroshell 15W-50'—use exactly that. Don't improvise.
exam_tip: Aviation oil numbers (15, 30, 40, 50) ≠ exact SAE equivalents. Follow your POH specification exactly. Don't mix grades or substitute automotive oils. Know what's written in your aircraft manual.
key_facts: Aviation oil grades: 15, 30, 40, 50 based on aero specs
SAE grades: 10, 20, 30, 40, etc. based on automotive standards
Roughly correspond but are not identical
Multi-grade oils: SAE 15W-50, for example, adapt to temperature
Always use the exact grade specified in the POH
Never substitute or mix grades without explicit approval
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between a wet sump and a dry sump oil system.
concept: A wet sump system is simple: all the oil sits in a pan bolted to the bottom of the engine. The engine-driven pump pulls oil from this pan, circulates it through the engine, and it drains back into the same pan. It's straightforward and low-maintenance, but it's limited by aircraft attitude—if you dive steeply or fly inverted, the oil sloshes around and you risk oil starvation because the pickup tube loses suction. Most piston twins use wet sump because they're not aerobatic. A dry sump system is more complex: there's a small oil tank mounted separately, usually in a wing or fuselage location. The engine pump draws from this tank and circulates through the engine, then a scavenge pump pulls the used oil back out of the engine sump into a separate return tank. This setup means oil stays isolated, independent of aircraft attitude, so you can fly inverted, barrel roll, whatever. Aerobatic aircraft and some high-performance types use dry sump. It's heavier, more complex, and needs more maintenance. For CPL exams in NZ, know the difference and understand why your aircraft has the one it has.
exam_tip: Wet sump: simple, oil pan bolted to engine, altitude-sensitive. Dry sump: separate tank, scavenge pump, altitude-independent. Most twins = wet sump. Aerobatic = dry sump.
key_facts: Wet sump: oil pan at engine bottom, pump pulls and drains to same pan
Wet sump limited to shallow manoeuvres (no sustained inverted flight)
Dry sump: separate oil tank and return system with scavenge pump
Dry sump allows any aircraft attitude including inverted flight
Dry sump heavier and more complex than wet sump
Most piston twins use wet sump systems
Aerobatic and high-performance aircraft use dry sump
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (a) the engine-driven oil pump and pressure relief valve; (b) oil lines, passages and galleries; (c) oil sumps, and scavenge pumps; (d) oil cooler; (e) oil tank and filter.
concept: The engine-driven pump is a gear pump or rotary pump that's mechanically driven by the engine—as engine speed increases, pump output increases, delivering more oil pressure. The pressure relief valve prevents over-pressure: if pressure climbs too high (usually set around 60–80 psi), the relief opens and dumps excess oil back to the sump. Oil lines carry oil from the pump to galleries in the engine block. Galleries are internal passages casting through the engine, distributing oil to every bearing and critical surface. The sump is the oil pan at the engine bottom where used oil drains and collects for recirculation. In a dry sump system, a scavenge pump pulls used oil from the engine sump into a separate return tank. The oil cooler is a heat exchanger (like a small radiator) where oil passes through cooling fins and sheds heat to airflow or ram air. The oil tank holds the oil supply and provides residence time for contaminants to settle before recirculation. The filter traps debris—you'll change it at specified intervals (usually every 50–100 hours on a piston twin) and check it during pre-flight.
exam_tip: Pump: mechanical drive, delivers pressure. Relief: prevents over-pressure. Galleries: internal passages. Cooler: sheds heat. Filter: traps debris. Monitor pressure and temperature continuously.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump: gear or rotary, output increases with RPM
Pressure relief valve opens at ~60–80 psi
Oil galleries: internal passages distributing oil through engine block
Oil sump: collection pan at engine bottom
Scavenge pump (dry sump only): returns used oil to tank
Oil cooler: heat exchanger shedding heat to airflow
Oil filter: captures debris, replaced every 50–100 hours
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for periodic oil changes.
concept: Oil degrades with use. The ashless dispersant additive gets depleted, the oil picks up moisture and contaminants, carbon buildup increases, and eventually the oil's no longer effective at lubrication and cooling. Typically, you'll change piston engine oil every 50–100 hours (your POH specifies exactly) or annually, whichever comes first. On a twin that's being flown regularly (50+ hours a month), you'll hit the hour limit first. If the aircraft is flown infrequently, oil degrades anyway and you'll hit the annual limit. The oil filter gets changed at every oil change—it's collecting debris, and once it's full of gunk, it restricts flow. Your pre-flight oil check is a visual inspection of quantity only; the contamination and degradation you can't see until you have oil analysed in a lab (which some operators do). Flying with old, degraded oil is like flying with a leaking seal bearing—you're running on borrowed time waiting for bearing failure.
exam_tip: Oil changes at 50–100 hour intervals or annually. Filter changed at every oil change. Old oil = poor lubrication and cooling. Stick to your POH change schedule.
key_facts: Oil degrades with time and use
Ashless dispersant additive depletes gradually
Typical change interval: 50–100 hours or annually
Whichever interval comes first is the limit
Oil filter changed at every oil change
Regular oil sampling can detect contamination early
Flying with expired oil risks bearing damage and engine failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely results of operating an engine with: (a) incorrect oil type; (b) incorrect oil quantity.
concept: Using the wrong oil type—say, using automotive detergent oil or a different aviation grade than specified—causes several problems. Detergent oils leave ash residue in the combustion chambers, fouling plugs and reducing ignition efficiency. The additive package might not be compatible with avgas, causing sludge or varnish buildup. Wrong aviation grades (too thin or too thick) mean either poor bearing lubrication (thin oil) or clogged filters and pressure problems (thick oil). Operating with insufficient oil (low quantity) means less heat dissipation, higher oil temperature, lower pressure, and risk of bearing starvation. You'll see rising temps and dropping pressure on the gauges. Excess oil is also bad: it foams in the sump, reducing its viscosity, and the extra heat generated by friction and aeration will spike temperature. Your daily oil level check (cold, on a level surface) should show the oil within the green band on the dipstick. Too much or too little is a problem.
exam_tip: Wrong oil type: deposits, sludge, poor lubrication. Too little oil: high temp, low pressure, bearing risk. Too much oil: foaming, high temp. Always use specified grade and maintain level in green band.
key_facts: Wrong oil type causes deposits, sludge, additive incompatibility
Detergent (automotive) oils leave ash residue (not approved)
Wrong aviation grade: poor flow or poor lubrication
Low oil quantity: high temperature, low pressure, bearing starvation
Excess oil: foaming, increased friction, high temperature
Monitor oil level before each flight (cold, level surface)
Maintain level within green band on dipstick
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely causes of: (a) low oil pressure; (b) high oil pressure; (c) high oil temperature.
concept: Low oil pressure mid-flight is usually a bearing wear issue: loose bearings have bigger gaps, oil leaks past instead of staying in the bearing film, pressure drops. It can also be a failed pressure relief valve (stuck open), a blocked oil filter restricting return flow, a faulty pump, or actual oil leakage from a line. High oil pressure is less common but usually means a stuck pressure relief valve (stays closed when it should open) or a clogged oil filter causing backpressure. High oil temperature usually comes from high oil load (big power settings, hot day, lots of electrical load or systems drawing power), inadequate cooling (fouled oil cooler, low airflow at low speed), or high friction from low oil quantity or viscosity too thick. Combining low pressure with high temperature is a red flag: it often means bearing wear—the bearing's loose, friction is high, oil's getting hot, pressure's dropping because the bearing isn't holding oil. That's a get-down-now situation. Your oil cooler can get fouled (dust, insects, salt spray if you're operating near the coast at places like Auckland), so check it visually during pre-flight.
exam_tip: Low pressure: bearing wear, pump failure, filter clog, relief stuck open. High pressure: relief stuck closed, filter clog. High temp: overload, poor cooling, low quantity. Low pressure + high temp = bearing wear = land urgently.
key_facts: Low oil pressure: bearing wear, pump failure, filter blockage, relief stuck open
High oil pressure: relief valve stuck closed, filter blockage
High oil temperature: high power setting, hot day, fouled cooler, insufficient quantity
Low pressure + high temperature: bearing wear (critical situation)
Monitor pressure and temperature continuously in flight
Oil cooler can foul from dust, insects, salt spray
Combination of low pressure and high temperature requires immediate landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between a fluctuating or low oil pressure reading accompanied by a rise in oil temperature.
concept: This combination is a red flag that points to bearing wear. Here's the chain of events: as a bearing wears, the clearance between the rotating shaft and the bearing surface increases. Oil that should stay in a thin film between the surfaces starts leaking past, increasing oil flow but reducing the protective film. With less film pressure, the bearing surfaces make closer contact, friction rises, heat generation spikes. You see the oil temperature climbing. Meanwhile, the pressure reading drops because the worn bearing isn't holding pressure—the oil's just flowing through and leaking out. The fluctuation you're seeing is the pressure bouncing around as the bearing rocks or the oil flow becomes intermittent. This is a bearing on the way out. You're not going to recover from this in flight, and continuing to run the engine makes it worse. Declare a precautionary landing immediately and get the aircraft on the ground and into maintenance. Don't power back up until a mechanic has inspected and cleared it.
exam_tip: Low pressure + high temperature + fluctuation = bearing wear. Immediate landing required. Don't continue running. Each minute of flight accelerates bearing damage.
key_facts: Bearing wear increases clearance between shaft and bearing surface
Increased clearance = oil leakage from bearing film
Oil leakage = lower pressure, higher friction, higher temperature
Fluctuating pressure indicates bearing movement or intermittent contact
This combination is a critical failure in progress
Immediate landing required; do not continue flying
Aircraft grounded until maintenance inspection and repair
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons why excessively high engine temperatures and rapid cooling must be avoided.
concept: Your engine's temperature management is all about keeping things in that sweet spot. If the cylinders get too hot, you're looking at detonation, pre-ignition, and your piston rings seizing. Cool things down too fast though—say, a rapid descent after climb—and you've got thermal shock cracking your cylinders. You want smooth, gradual temperature changes.
exam_tip: They'll test whether you understand detonation risk at high temps and thermal shock at low temps. Know that slow cooling is better than rapid cooling.
key_facts: Excessive heat causes detonation, pre-ignition, and component failure
Rapid cooling causes thermal shock and cylinder cracking
Maintain steady power settings to avoid temperature swings
Cowl flaps and airflow management control temperature rise and descent rate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the ways in which heat from the combustion process is dispersed from a typical aero-engine.
concept: Most of the heat energy from combustion gets wasted anyway—only about 20-30% actually goes into useful power. The rest goes three ways: through the exhaust gases (takes away heaps of heat), through the engine oil (which circulates and picks up heat), and through the cylinder cooling fins into the airstream. That's why we have oil coolers and baffles directing air across those fins.
exam_tip: Remember the three main heat dispersal paths: exhaust, oil circulation, and direct cooling fins. The oil's your main cooling agent inside the engine.
key_facts: Only 20-30% of combustion heat converts to useful power
Heat dispersed via exhaust gases, engine oil circulation, and cylinder cooling fins
Oil cooler removes heat from oil before it returns to sump
Cowling baffles direct air flow over cooling fins
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Lycoming/Textron technical data

syllabus_desc: In a typical aircraft engine, explain the function of: (a) cowling ducts and baffles; (b) cooling fins; (c) pilot-operated cowl flaps; (d) oil cooler; (e) radiator; (f) intercooler.
concept: Think of your cooling system like directing a river of air. The cowling ducts and baffles are your channels—they force cold air to flow where you need it. Cooling fins are just extra surface area bolted to each cylinder; more surface means more heat transfer. Cowl flaps are your throttle for cooling; you open them if you're hot, close them if you need to climb and temperature's good. The oil cooler's like a radiator but for oil—cooling it before it recirculates. Radiators cool fuel and other systems. Intercoolers cool supercharged air between stages before it goes to the cylinders, which lets you run higher boost pressure.
exam_tip: Cowl flaps are pilot-operated—you control them. Oil cooler keeps oil in the sweet spot. Intercoolers let you squeeze more boost pressure safely.
key_facts: Cowling ducts and baffles direct ram air over cooling fins
Cooling fins increase surface area for heat dissipation
Cowl flaps (pilot-operated) control airflow and engine temperature
Oil cooler removes heat from engine oil circulation
Radiator cools fuel and secondary systems
Intercooler cools compressed air from supercharger to allow higher boost pressure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the general engine handling techniques for maintaining engine temperatures within the proper range, for aircraft: (a) without CHT gauge or cowl flaps; (b) fitted with CHT gauge and cowl flaps.
concept: If you've got an older airframe without a CHT gauge, you're managing temperature by controlling power, descent rate, and airspeed. Keep power reasonable, descend slowly, and use cruise descent techniques rather than diving. If you've got a modern twin with CHT and cowl flaps, you've got precision tools. Monitor your CHTs, open cowl flaps if any cylinder starts creeping high, and close them once you've got good airflow and climb performance back. Before takeoff, exercise those cowl flaps to make sure they move freely—you don't want them stuck.
exam_tip: Without CHT: manage via power and descent rate. With CHT and cowl flaps: monitor continuously and adjust cowl flaps as needed. Always exercise cowl flaps before flight.
key_facts: Without CHT/cowl flaps: manage via power settings, descent rate, and airspeed
With CHT and cowl flaps: monitor CHT continuously and adjust flap position
Cowl flaps should be exercised on ground before takeoff
Descend at cruise descent rate (200 fpm rule) to avoid rapid cooling
Close cowl flaps for takeoff to build airflow and climb performance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, AFM/POH procedures

syllabus_desc: Reducing cylinder head temperature in piston-engine helicopters
concept: If your CHT's creeping up, especially in the R22 during a hot-day climb into the Waikato, you've got several moves. Enrichen the mixture slightly—counter-intuitive, but the extra fuel cools the combustion chamber. Reduce power if you can. Increase airspeed—the extra cooling air helps. Reduce climb angle and come down in altitude where it's cooler. Helicopter cowling design matters too; some designs force more air over the engine, which is why manufacturers are picky about cowl flaps.
exam_tip: Examiners ask what you'd do to lower CHT. Answer with the practical options: mixture, power, speed, altitude, climb angle.
key_facts: Enrichening mixture increases fuel cooling effect in combustion chamber
Reducing power decreases heat generation
Increased airspeed improves engine cooling airflow
Reducing climb angle decreases engine demand and heat
source: Wagtendonk

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic characteristics of an electrical circuit.
concept: An electrical circuit's basically a complete path where current flows from the power source, through the load (your equipment), and back. You've got three key bits: the source (like your battery), the conductors (wiring), and the load (whatever uses the power). Current only flows when you've got a complete path—break it anywhere and nothing happens. Think of it like water flowing through pipes; if you block it, flow stops.
exam_tip: Remember: circuit must be complete for current to flow. Examiners love asking what happens when you open a switch.
key_facts: Complete conducting path required
Current flows from positive to negative terminal
Open circuit = no current flow
Series vs parallel affects total resistance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: State Ohm's law.
concept: Ohm's law is dead simple: voltage equals current times resistance, written as V = I × R. This relationship's at the heart of understanding how your electrical system works. If you increase voltage with resistance constant, current goes up. If you increase resistance with voltage constant, current drops. It's how we size wiring, fuses, and circuit protectors in aircraft.
exam_tip: Know the formula V = I × R and be able to rearrange it for any variable. They might give you two values and ask for the third.
key_facts: V = I × R (voltage = current × resistance)
Measured in volts, amperes, and ohms
Direct proportional relationship
Foundation for all circuit analysis
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (a) electromotive force (volts); (b) current (amperes); (c) resistance (ohms).
concept: Electromotive force, or EMF, is the voltage your source provides—like your aircraft battery pushing 28 volts. Current's the actual flow of electrons through your circuit, measured in amperes; think of it as how much 'stuff' is moving. Resistance is opposition to that flow, measured in ohms—thicker wires have less resistance, thin ones have more. Your engine starter draws massive current because it's low resistance; your navigation lights draw far less.
exam_tip: EMF is the source voltage, current is the flow, resistance opposes it. You'll get scenarios asking which increases and which decreases with circuit changes.
key_facts: EMF = voltage provided by source (volts)
Current = flow of electrons (amperes)
Resistance = opposition to flow (ohms)
EMF stays constant regardless of load
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the structure of a: (a) two-wire circuit; (b) grounded 'earth return' electrical circuit.
concept: A two-wire circuit's got a positive feed wire and a separate return (negative) wire running the full length—both wires carry the current. In an earth return system, which you'll find on most aircraft, the positive feed runs out but the aircraft structure itself (frame, fuselage) acts as the return path to the battery negative. This saves weight since you only need one wire. Most light twins at Ardmore or Hamilton use earth return systems because it's simpler and lighter.
exam_tip: Two-wire = both paths are wires. Earth return = structure is the negative return. Be ready to explain why aircraft prefer earth return (weight saving, simplicity).
key_facts: Two-wire: separate positive and negative conductors
Earth return: structure acts as return path
Earth return saves weight in aircraft
All modern aircraft use earth return systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between direct and alternating current.
concept: Direct current, or DC, flows one way only—from positive to negative—which is what your battery provides. Alternating current, or AC, constantly reverses direction, changing polarity back and forth. Your aircraft runs on DC from the battery and alternator, but some older systems or test equipment might produce AC. AC's actually generated more easily in alternators, then we convert it to DC with a rectifier because that's what the aircraft electrical system needs.
exam_tip: DC = one direction always (battery/alternator output). AC = constantly reversing (found in alternators before rectification). Know why aircraft prefer DC.
key_facts: DC = unidirectional flow (positive to negative)
AC = bidirectional, constantly reversing
Aircraft electrical systems are DC-based
Alternators generate AC, then it's rectified to DC
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms: (a) frequency (Hertz); (b) rectification.
concept: Frequency's how many times per second AC reverses direction, measured in Hertz—your aircraft alternator typically produces around 400 Hz. Rectification's the process of converting that AC to DC, done by a rectifier inside the alternator. Think of it like a one-way valve; it lets current flow one direction but blocks the reverse, so out comes DC current you can actually use for charging the battery and running systems.
exam_tip: Frequency = cycles per second (Hz). Rectification = converting AC to DC using a rectifier. You might get asked about typical aircraft alternator frequency.
key_facts: Frequency measured in Hertz (cycles per second)
Aircraft alternators produce ~400 Hz
Rectification = AC to DC conversion
Rectifier acts as electronic one-way valve
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: (a) polarity; (b) attraction; (c) repulsion.
concept: Every magnet's got a north and south pole—that's polarity. Opposite poles attract each other (north pulls south), same poles repel (north repels north). This is the foundation of how your alternator, starter motor, and relays work. The magnetic field around a magnet is strongest at the poles and weakest at the centre. You'll see this in action with electromagnetic switches and the magneto's ignition system.
exam_tip: Opposite poles attract, same poles repel. Remember this when explaining how electromagnets create switching action in relays.
key_facts: Polarity: north and south poles
Opposite poles attract
Same poles repel
Magnetic field strongest at poles
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between: (a) temporary and permanent magnets; (b) the properties of 'soft iron' and 'hard iron' magnets.
concept: A permanent magnet keeps its magnetism indefinitely—that's your bar magnet. A temporary magnet only works while current flows through it, like an electromagnet; when you switch off, it loses magnetism. Soft iron becomes magnetic easily but loses it quickly when the field's removed—perfect for relay cores where you want rapid on/off. Hard iron stays magnetised longer—that's what permanent magnets are made of. Your starter motor solenoid uses soft iron in its core.
exam_tip: Permanent = always magnetic. Temporary = only when powered. Soft iron = quick response. Hard iron = retains magnetism. Link this to relay operation.
key_facts: Permanent magnets retain magnetism
Temporary magnets lose magnetism when field removed
Soft iron magnetises/demagnetises quickly
Hard iron retains magnetisation longer
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (a) magnetic field; (b) magnetic flux; (c) permeability.
concept: The magnetic field is the region around a magnet where magnetic force acts—you can visualise it with field lines. Magnetic flux is the total amount of magnetic field, the sum of all those lines of force flowing through a given area. Permeability's how easily a material lets magnetic field pass through it—soft iron's highly permeable, air isn't. In your alternator, the iron core has high permeability, so the magnetic field concentrates efficiently there rather than spreading into the air.
exam_tip: Field = region of force. Flux = total field strength through an area. Permeability = how easily material lets field pass through. You'll explain how alternator cores use high permeability materials.
key_facts: Magnetic field = region of magnetic force
Magnetic flux = total field through area
Permeability = ease of magnetic field passage
Soft iron has high permeability
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain electromagnetism.
concept: When current flows through a conductor, it creates a magnetic field around it—that's electromagnetism. You can boost this field by wrapping the conductor into a coil; more turns mean stronger field. Add a soft iron core inside and you've got an electromagnet that's switchable; electricity on, you get magnetism; switch off, magnetism's gone. This principle powers your starter motor solenoid, relays, and ignition systems. It's the bridge between electricity and magnetism that makes modern aircraft electrical systems work.
exam_tip: Current through wire = magnetic field. Coil = stronger field. Iron core = even stronger. Switchable = that's why it's useful. Know how this enables relays and solenoids.
key_facts: Current produces magnetic field around conductor
Coil strengthens magnetic field
Iron core further increases field strength
Switchable nature enables relays and solenoids
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the lines of magnetic force around a: (a) straight conductor; (b) current carrying coil.
concept: Around a straight current-carrying wire, magnetic field lines form concentric circles perpendicular to the wire. Use the right-hand rule: thumb points along current direction, fingers curl showing field direction. A current-carrying coil strengthens this enormously—field lines bunch up through the centre, creating a pattern like a bar magnet with distinct north and south poles. That concentrated field is what makes solenoids and relay coils effective.
exam_tip: Right-hand rule for straight wire. Coil field = stronger and more concentrated. You might draw field patterns or explain directional effects.
key_facts: Straight wire: concentric circular field lines
Right-hand rule determines field direction
Coil: field lines concentrate through centre
Coil creates poles like a bar magnet
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of an electromagnetic switch (or relay).
concept: An electromagnetic switch or relay's essentially a remote control for electrical circuits. Current flows through a coil, creating a magnetic field that attracts an iron armature, which moves and operates contacts—opening or closing circuits without anyone touching anything. You've got a low-current control circuit that operates a high-current load circuit, which is brilliant for aircraft where you can't run heavy cables to every switch. Your starter motor uses a relay; so does your landing gear system.
exam_tip: Low current through coil creates magnetic field, attracts armature, operates contacts. Know why relays are essential in aircraft electrical systems (remote operation, circuit isolation).
key_facts: Coil carries control current
Magnetic field attracts armature
Armature movement operates contacts
Separates control from load circuit
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe typical examples of the use of electromagnetic switches in aircraft electrical circuits.
concept: You'll find electromagnetic switches throughout your aircraft. The starter solenoid's the obvious one—single switch in the cockpit controls a heavy-duty starter motor. Landing gear relays use electromagnets to divert power to retract or extend the gear. Landing light circuits often have relays so a small cockpit switch doesn't have to handle the full current. Fuel pump circuits, flap motors, heater fans—they all typically use relays. The beauty is you've got one small switch doing remote control work.
exam_tip: Be ready with specific examples: starter solenoid, landing gear relay, fuel pump relay. Explain why we don't just run heavy cables from cockpit switches.
key_facts: Starter motor solenoid
Landing gear relays
Landing light relays
Fuel pump control relays
Flap motor relays
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIC NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of electromagnetic induction.
concept: When you move a conductor through a magnetic field, or change the field around a conductor, you generate an electrical voltage. That's electromagnetic induction—Faraday's principle. The faster the field changes or the more field lines cut, the bigger the voltage generated. This is exactly how your alternator works: rotating magnets create a changing field through stationary coils, inducing voltage. It's also how your ignition magneto generates the high voltage for spark plugs, and how instrument pickups sense engine speed.
exam_tip: Movement or field change = induced voltage. Faster change = higher voltage. Know this is the basis for alternators and magnetos.
key_facts: Conductor moving through field generates voltage
Changing magnetic field induces voltage
Rate of change determines voltage magnitude
Foundation of alternator and magneto operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a simple alternator.
concept: A simple alternator's got a rotating magnetic field (rotor) inside stationary coils (stator). As the rotor spins, its magnetic field cuts through the stator coils, inducing AC voltage. The faster it spins, the higher the frequency. Your engine drives the alternator directly, usually through a belt. The frequency depends entirely on rotational speed and pole count. Modern aircraft alternators generate around 400 Hz at cruise RPM, which is then rectified to DC for the electrical system.
exam_tip: Rotating magnet, stationary coils, induced voltage. Higher RPM = higher frequency. This AC gets rectified to DC before reaching your battery.
key_facts: Rotor (magnet) rotates, stator (coil) stationary
Magnetic field change induces AC
Frequency increases with RPM
Output is AC before rectification
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the features of a practical alternator; (b) how the AC output is normally rectified to provide DC.
concept: A practical alternator's more sophisticated than the simple version. It's got a three-phase output instead of single-phase, which gives smoother power. The rotor's an electromagnet (needs DC to excite it), the stator's typically delta-connected for efficiency. A voltage regulator controls excitation current to maintain constant output voltage regardless of load or RPM—critical for your electrical system. The AC output runs through a rectifier pack containing diodes; each diode lets current flow one direction only, converting AC to DC. Six diodes in a three-phase system gives near-constant DC output.
exam_tip: Practical alternators are three-phase, electromagnet rotor, voltage regulator. Rectifier uses diodes—know six diodes for three-phase. This is why your alternator output is smooth DC.
key_facts: Three-phase output for smoothness
Electromagnet rotor needs excitation
Voltage regulator maintains constant output
Rectifier pack uses diodes (typically 6)
Output is smooth DC for battery charging
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a simple generator.
concept: A simple DC generator's similar to an alternator, but instead of rectifying AC, it uses a commutator—a split ring on the rotor that switches connections as it rotates. This switching action reverses which coil half's connected to the output at exactly the right moment, so output's always DC. You get pulsing DC rather than the smooth output of an alternator, but it's DC nonetheless. The faster you spin it, the higher the voltage. Older aircraft and some backup systems used generators; most modern ones switched to alternators for better output characteristics.
exam_tip: Generator uses commutator to switch coil connections, producing DC. Alternator + rectifier vs. generator + commutator—both end with DC but different methods.
key_facts: Rotating coil in magnetic field
Commutator switches connections
Output is DC (pulsing)
Voltage increases with rotation speed
Less efficient than modern alternators
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the features of a practical generator; (b) how the output is passed through a commutator to provide DC.
concept: A practical DC generator's got a field coil system (electromagnets, not permanent magnets) for stronger output, and a commutator that's not just a simple split ring but a multi-segment ring with brushes making contact. Each segment connects to a different coil section; as the rotor turns, the brush arrangement ensures coil halves always connect to the external circuit at the moment their voltage's pointing the right direction. This gives relatively smooth DC, though still pulsing compared to modern alternators. The field coils need their own excitation circuit, usually from the generator's own output (self-excited).
exam_tip: Multi-segment commutator with brushes provides DC. Field coils generate magnetic field. Why did we move to alternators? Generators need field excitation, give pulsing output.
key_facts: Multi-segment commutator with brushes
Field coils provide magnetic field
Self-excited (output excites field)
Output is pulsing DC
Commutator ensures correct polarity switching
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain: (a) the need for voltage regulation for both alternators and generators; (b) why a generator also requires a current regulator and a reverse current relay.
concept: Both alternators and generators need voltage regulation because their output varies with RPM—cruise power's different from takeoff. You can't let battery voltage fluctuate all over the place; you need it stable around 28 volts. A voltage regulator senses output voltage and adjusts field excitation current to maintain constant voltage. Generators additionally need a current regulator because output current also varies with load, and you need to limit charge rate to protect the battery. They also need a reverse current relay that disconnects the generator when RPM drops below flight idle—prevents the battery back-driving the generator, which would burn out the brushes. Alternators, with their rectifier design, inherently prevent reverse current, so they don't need that relay.
exam_tip: Both need voltage regulation for constant output. Generators need current limiter and reverse current relay. Alternators don't—rectifier prevents reverse current. This is why alternators are superior.
key_facts: Voltage regulator needed (alternators and generators)
Maintains ~28 V regardless of load/RPM
Generators need current regulator
Generators need reverse current relay
Alternators prevent reverse current inherently
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a battery.
concept: A battery works through chemical reaction. Inside, you've got two different metals (terminals) separated by chemical electrolyte. Chemical action causes electrons to build up at one terminal (negative) and leave the other (positive), creating voltage. When you connect an external circuit, electrons flow from negative to positive, powering your equipment. The chemical reaction continues supplying this electron imbalance until the chemicals are spent. In a secondary cell (rechargeable), applying external current reverses the chemical reaction, restoring the battery. Your aircraft's lead-acid battery stores energy chemically; the alternator, via the regulator, chemically recharges it.
exam_tip: Chemical reaction = voltage difference. Electrons flow negative to positive. Chemical depleted = dead battery. Recharge = reverse the chemistry. Simple concept, understand it deeply.
key_facts: Chemical reaction creates voltage
Two different terminals (positive/negative)
Electrolyte enables reaction
Electrons flow negative to positive externally
Rechargeable batteries reverse chemical reaction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: (a) primary and secondary cells; (b) wet cells and dry cells; (c) lead-acid and nickel-cadmium (and similar) types.
concept: Primary cells are one-shot; once discharged, you bin them (think torch batteries). Secondary cells recharge—your aircraft battery's secondary. Wet cells have liquid electrolyte (lead-acid: sulphuric acid) where you can actually see and sometimes top up the liquid; dry cells use paste electrolyte (NiCad: sealed unit). Lead-acid batteries are cheap, reliable, but heavier and need maintenance (checking water levels at Christchurch or Queenstown). NiCad batteries are lighter, don't spill, handle charge/discharge cycles better, but costlier and need special charging equipment. Most modern training aircraft still use lead-acid; some higher-spec birds use NiCad.
exam_tip: Primary = disposable. Secondary = rechargeable. Wet = liquid you see. Dry = sealed. Lead-acid = heavier, needs maintenance. NiCad = lighter, sealed, costlier. Know both types used in NZ aircraft.
key_facts: Primary cells: non-rechargeable
Secondary cells: rechargeable
Wet cells: liquid electrolyte (lead-acid)
Dry cells: paste electrolyte (NiCad)
Lead-acid cheaper, NiCad lighter and sealed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the systems which typically require DC power in an aircraft.
concept: Your DC electrical system is the backbone of the aeroplane. Everything that needs power before you get the engines running—and quite a bit after—relies on that battery and alternator. Think about what happens when you're doing your walk-around at Ardmore or anywhere else: you need lights, you need the radio to talk to ATC, the fuel pump needs juice to get fuel to the engines. Once you're running, systems like landing lights, navigation lights, instrument panel lighting, the autopilot, and the entire avionics suite all depend on DC power. Even your flaps and landing gear on many twins use electrically-driven pumps or motors. The point is, without DC power, you're not flying very far.
exam_tip: List the major consumers: battery charging/starting, lighting (cockpit, nav, landing), radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, autopilot, landing gear/flaps, and avionics. Focus on what's essential for flight ops.
key_facts: DC power comes from the battery and alternator/generator
Critical systems: radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, lighting
Landing gear and flaps often use electric motors or pump drives
Autopilot and modern avionics require steady DC supply
Loss of electrical power means loss of radio comms and engine instrumentation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (a) the battery; (b) a ground power source; (c) the alternator or generator; (d) bus bars; (e) over voltage protection.
concept: Your battery is your electrical starter motor and emergency backup—it cranks the engine and provides power until the alternator takes over. Once you're running, the alternator (or generator on older twins) generates AC current, which gets rectified to DC to charge the battery and power the system. Bus bars are just distribution points—think of them as electrical junctions where power splits off to different circuits. A ground power unit (GPU) does the same job as your battery for cold starts, especially useful in winter at places like Christchurch. Over-voltage protection is critical because if your alternator fails in a way that causes over-charging, you'll cook your battery and damage sensitive avionics. That's why you've got a voltage regulator watching the output.
exam_tip: Remember: battery starts and backs up; alternator powers and charges; bus bars distribute; GPU is external battery; voltage regulator prevents over-charge. Be ready to explain why over-voltage protection matters (equipment damage, battery boil-off).
key_facts: Battery provides starting power and emergency backup
Alternator/generator produces main electrical power in flight
Bus bars are distribution centres for electrical loads
Ground power unit (GPU) provides external power for starting
Voltage regulator prevents over-voltage damage to equipment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Given appropriate data, determine the duration of battery operation following a generator failure.
concept: If your alternator fails mid-flight, you're running on battery power alone—and that battery's capacity is finite. You've got your battery amp-hour rating (typically 25-35 Ah on a light twin) and the aircraft's electrical load in amps. Simple division: capacity divided by load equals remaining time. So if you've got a 30 Ah battery and your electrical load is pulling 40 amps (lights, radio, instruments), you've got roughly 45 minutes before that battery's flat. But here's the reality: you'll start dropping non-essential loads, shed the landing light, maybe dim the panel—so you can stretch it. The exam will give you specific numbers, so the maths is straightforward, but the practical lesson is urgent: get the aircraft on the ground at the nearest suitable airfield.
exam_tip: Use the formula: time (hours) = battery capacity (Ah) ÷ electrical load (amps). In the real world, you'll reduce non-essential loads immediately after an alternator failure and declare an emergency with ATC.
key_facts: Battery capacity is rated in amp-hours (Ah)
Calculate remaining time: capacity ÷ load = hours
Typical light twin battery: 25–35 Ah
Cockpit load in normal flight: 20–50 amps depending on configuration
Reduce non-essential loads immediately (landing lights, autopilot, non-essential avionics)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Split bus bar system and essential vs non-essential electrical bus classification
concept: Modern helicopters like the R44 use a split bus bar system. You've got essential and non-essential buses—if you lose alternator output, the battery backs up the essential bus (engine systems, fire warning, basic instruments) while non-essential stuff (cabin lights, entertainment) gets dropped. The split keeps critical systems alive when the alternator fails. It's all about redundancy and keeping you flying safely.
exam_tip: Understand why split buses matter: battery prioritises essential systems.
key_facts: Essential bus carries engine systems, fire warning, basic instruments
Non-essential bus powers convenience and entertainment systems
Battery backs essential bus if alternator fails
Split design maintains critical flight systems on battery power
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Electrical circuit protection: fuses, circuit breakers, and resettable vs non-resettable types
concept: Circuit protection prevents electrical fires and damage. Fuses blow once and must be replaced; circuit breakers pop and can be reset. Resettable breakers let you try again if something trips (pilot discretion), but non-resettable ones protect critical circuits and must be replaced by maintenance. You'd carry spare fuses of the right ratings, and if a circuit breaker pops repeatedly, you don't keep resetting it—there's a fault, and you need to diagnose it or get on the ground.
exam_tip: Questions test the difference between fuses and breakers, and when you'd reset vs replace.
key_facts: Fuses are one-time protection; must be replaced when blown
Circuit breakers can be reset manually (if resettable)
Non-resettable breakers protect critical circuits, require maintenance replacement
Repeated breaker trips indicate a fault, not a marginal circuit
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Ammeter and voltmeter: function and normal readings
concept: Your ammeter shows you charging and discharging current. Positive (right) means the alternator's generating more than the system needs—you're charging the battery. Negative (left) means you're drawing from the battery because the alternator's not keeping up. Your voltmeter should sit around 28V (helicopter system voltage). If it drops below 26V or climbs above 30V, something's wrong—bad alternator, loose connection, or a failed regulator. Check these gauges during engine start and throughout flight.
exam_tip: Know normal readings: ammeter should show a charge initially, then near-zero in cruise. Voltmeter 28V nominal.
key_facts: Ammeter shows positive current when alternator is charging
Ammeter shows negative current when drawing from battery
Voltmeter normal reading: approximately 28 volts DC
Voltage below 26V or above 30V indicates alternator or regulator fault
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Battery care and preflight checks including electrolyte level verification
concept: Your helicopter battery's a workhorse. During preflight, visually inspect the case for cracks or leaks—a damaged battery's a fire risk. Check electrolyte level if it's a vented cell type (some newer helicopters have sealed batteries). The level should be just below the filler cap. If it's low, top it up with distilled water only. Keep the battery terminals clean and tight; a loose terminal can cause electrical gremlins. During engine start, monitor the ammeter—if it spikes too high, the battery's struggling or there's a short.
exam_tip: Examiners ask about battery maintenance. Answer: visual inspection, electrolyte level, terminal cleanliness.
key_facts: Visual inspection of battery case for cracks, leaks, corrosion
Electrolyte level just below filler cap (vented batteries)
Top up with distilled water only, never tap water
Keep terminals clean and tight to prevent electrical faults
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Electrical system malfunctions: diagnosis and response procedures
concept: Electrical failures fall into a few categories. Blown fuse: the circuit's open, affected system's dead. Excessive charge rate (ammeter buried in positive): voltage regulator failure, alternator overcharging—shut off non-essential electrical load and get back to the field before it overheats. Alternator failure: ammeter swings negative, voltmeter drops, battery's powering the essential bus only—non-essential systems die, and you've got limited battery time. Total electrical failure: nothing works—you're flying on fumes and instincts, declare emergency, get down immediately. Know your instrument priorities when the electrical system fails.
exam_tip: Questions ask 'what would you do if...' Practise your decision tree for each failure type.
key_facts: Blown fuse means that circuit is open and non-functional
Excessive charge rate indicates voltage regulator malfunction
Alternator failure causes ammeter swing negative and voltmeter drop
Total electrical failure requires immediate descent and emergency landing
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (a) tachometers (centrifugal, drag cup, electrical and electronic types); (b) manifold pressure and boost gauges; (c) oil pressure gauges (direct reading, remote indicating and ratiometer types); (d) fuel pressure gauges; (e) vacuum gauges; (f) cylinder head and exhaust gas temperature gauges (thermocouples); (g) oil temperature gauges; (h) outside air temperature gauges; (i) fuel quantity gauges; (j) fuel flow gauges.
concept: Your engine instruments are your window into what's happening inside the powerplants. A tachometer (rev counter) reads RPM: centrifugal types use weighted balls that fling outward as speed increases, pulling a needle; drag cup types use a spinning magnet creating drag proportional to RPM; electrical types use an alternator on the engine generating frequency proportional to RPM; electronic ones count pulses from the magnetos. Manifold pressure gauge shows intake air pressure in the intake manifold—at full throttle sea level you might see 30 inHg, at altitude it drops. Boost gauge is similar but measures turbocharger output in psi, warning you if boost's excessive (over-pressure risk). Oil pressure gauge (direct reading uses a Bourdon tube connected by a small pipe to engine oil; remote indicating uses a transducer on the engine sending an electrical signal to the gauge; ratiometer compares two resistances to indicate pressure on a moving needle). Fuel pressure gauge tells you fuel pump output—normal range is 4–6 psi on carburetted engines, higher on fuel-injected ones. Vacuum gauge (or suction gauge) measures negative pressure created by the vacuum pump—typical range is 4–5.5 inHg—and tells you whether your gyro-powered instruments are getting good suction. Cylinder head temperature (CHT) and exhaust gas temperature (EGT) use thermocouples (two dissimilar metals creating a tiny voltage in response to heat); the gauge measures this voltage and converts it to temperature. CHT typically 350–400°F max; EGT varies by engine but tells you if a cylinder's running lean or rich. Oil temperature gauge (similar thermocouple sensor in the oil sump) shows oil heat—normal range is 165–220°F. Outside air temperature gauge (OAT) uses a small Bourdon tube that expands/contracts with air temperature, or an electronic thermistor—gives you true air temperature for calculations and icing awareness. Fuel quantity gauge uses a float-operated rheostat in the tank (like a car fuel gauge) sending resistance to the gauge, which converts resistance to fuel quantity. Fuel flow gauge shows fuel consumption rate (pounds per hour or gallons per hour) based on a turbine or gear pump in the fuel line that spins faster with higher flow, generating frequency that the gauge displays.
exam_tip: Know each instrument's principle and what normal values look like. Understand which are direct-reading (oil pressure Bourdon tube) versus remote-indicating (fuel quantity). Thermocouple-based gauges (CHT, EGT, OAT) measure voltage from heat.
key_facts: Tachometer: centrifugal, drag cup, electrical, or electronic; measures RPM
Manifold pressure: intake air pressure, normal 30 inHg at sea level full throttle
Boost gauge: turbocharger outlet pressure, measures in psi
Oil pressure: direct reading (Bourdon tube), remote (transducer), ratiometer
Fuel pressure: 4–6 psi carburetted, higher fuel-injected
Vacuum/suction: 4–5.5 inHg required for gyro instruments
CHT (thermocouple): cylinder head temp, max typically 400°F
EGT (thermocouple): exhaust gas temp, indicates lean/rich condition
Oil temperature (thermocouple): 165–220°F normal range
OAT: outside air temperature, Bourdon or thermistor
Fuel quantity: float rheostat in tank, converts to gauge reading
Fuel flow: turbine or gear sensor measures consumption rate (pph/gph)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Engine instruments: types, function, and interpretation
concept: You've got a suite of engine instruments telling you what your powerplant's doing. Tachometer shows RPM (centrifugal, drag cup, or electronic designs all work the same—three needles on older birds, digital readouts on R44s). Manifold pressure gauges (piston helicopters) show induction pressure. Oil pressure—direct gauges, remote transmitters, or ratiometers—tells you lubrication's working. Fuel pressure confirms fuel's reaching the engine. Vacuum gauges back up your gyro instruments. CHT (cylinder head temperature) and EGT (exhaust gas temperature) let you monitor combustion.
