Watch all of them—they tell the story of what's happening in your engine.
exam_tip: Know which instrument does what and what's normal. Questions test interpretation and action.
key_facts: Tachometer shows RPM; centrifugal, drag cup, or electronic designs
Manifold pressure indicates fuel-air mixture pressure (piston engines)
Oil pressure shows lubrication system effectiveness
Fuel pressure confirms fuel delivery to engine
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Define: (a) static air pressure; (b) dynamic air pressure; (c) total (pitot) pressure.
concept: Static air pressure is the pressure of the air around you at rest—it's the ambient pressure at your altitude. It decreases as you climb (lower density, less pressure). Dynamic air pressure is the pressure created by the motion of the aircraft through the air—it's zero if you're stationary and increases with airspeed. Total pressure (pitot pressure) is the sum of static and dynamic: it's the pressure measured by a pitot tube that faces directly into the airflow and stagnates (stops) the air, creating maximum pressure. Your airspeed indicator uses the difference between total and static pressure (dynamic pressure = total - static) to calculate and display airspeed. Your altimeter uses static pressure alone; the higher you climb, the lower the static pressure, and the altimeter displays that as altitude. Your vertical speed indicator also uses static pressure but monitors the rate of change. So your three basic pressure instruments need two inputs: static pressure (for altitude and VSI) and total/pitot pressure (for airspeed). The pitot-static system provides both.
exam_tip: Static: ambient pressure at your altitude. Dynamic: pressure from motion, zero at rest. Total (pitot): static + dynamic. ASI uses both; ALT and VSI use static only.
key_facts: Static air pressure: ambient atmospheric pressure
Static pressure decreases with altitude
Dynamic air pressure: pressure caused by aircraft motion
Dynamic pressure = 0 when stationary, increases with speed
Total (pitot) pressure = static + dynamic
Pitot tube: faces into airflow, measures total pressure
Static vent: measures ambient static pressure
ASI requires both static and dynamic pressures
ALT and VSI require static pressure only
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the pressure requirements of the three basic pressure instruments.
concept: Your airspeed indicator (ASI) needs two pressures: total pressure (from the pitot tube) and static pressure (from the static vent). The ASI's internal mechanism measures the difference (dynamic pressure) and converts it to airspeed. Your altimeter needs only static pressure: as static pressure decreases with altitude, the altimeter mechanism expands a bellows and the needle climbs. Your vertical speed indicator (VSI) needs static pressure but in a specific way: it has a sensitive capsule inside that connects to the static system and a reference chamber also fed by static, but with a delayed connection through a metering restriction. When static pressure changes, the capsule responds faster than the reference chamber, creating a pressure difference that moves the needle to show the rate of change (climb or descent). So in summary: ASI = total + static. Altimeter = static only. VSI = static with internal delay mechanism. If you lose static pressure (blocked static vent), all three instruments fail or become unreliable. If you lose pitot pressure (pitot tube iced over), only the ASI fails; altitude and vertical speed still work. That's why understanding what each instrument depends on is critical for troubleshooting.
exam_tip: ASI: pitot (total) + static. Altimeter: static only. VSI: static only (internal delay for rate sensing). Loss of static = all three fail. Loss of pitot = ASI only.
key_facts: ASI requires: pitot pressure (total) and static pressure
Altimeter requires: static pressure only
VSI requires: static pressure with metering delay
ASI failure if pitot ices or blocked
ALT/VSI failure if static vent ices or blocked
Loss of both pitot and static = complete pressure instrument failure
Alternate static system: if main static blocked, use cabin pressure or alternate vent
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical pitot-static system.
concept: A typical system has a pitot tube (a small intake facing directly into the airflow), a static vent (or vents) usually on the fuselage side where the airflow runs parallel and static pressure predominates), and connecting tubing from both to the flight deck instruments. The pitot tube is exposed on the nose or wing leading edge and has a small heater (pitot heat) to prevent ice accumulation. The static vent is usually a pair of openings (left and right sides of the fuselage) to measure average static pressure and cancel out lateral pressure variations. Lines from the pitot run to the ASI and any digital airspeed system. Lines from the static vent branch to the altimeter, VSI, and any other pressure-referenced systems. The lines should be clear tubing so you can see for blockages (dust, insects, water, ice). Moisture can condense inside the lines and freeze, blocking them. That's why some systems include a drain valve at the lowest point to bleed condensation. The system is checked during pre-flight: you look at the pitot tube (make sure it's uncovered and clean), you check that the alternate static valve (if equipped) is in the correct position, and you verify instruments read sensibly (altimeter matches field elevation, VSI reads zero on the ground, ASI reads zero).
exam_tip: Pitot tube: nose or wing, faces into airflow. Static vent: fuselage side, parallel airflow. Lines connect to instruments. Pitot heat prevents ice. Check pre-flight: pitot clear, static vent uncovered, instruments reading correctly.
key_facts: Pitot tube: intake facing airflow, measures total (pitot) pressure
Pitot tube location: nose or wing leading edge
Pitot heat: electric heater prevents ice accumulation
Static vent: fuselage side, measures static pressure
Dual static vents (left and right): average pressure, cancel lateral variations
Tubing: clear plastic, check for blockages or condensation
Drain valve: at lowest point, drains condensed moisture
Alternate static system: cabin air or manual vent if main static blocked
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between a single pitot (pressure) head and separate pitot tube and static vent system.
concept: A single pitot head (also called a combined pitot-static head or probe) is a single intake that measures both pitot and static pressures from internal ports. It's compact and you only have one thing to worry about keeping clear. The internal design routes air to either the pitot chamber (centre, high pressure) or the static chamber (outer annulus, low pressure). It's elegant if it works. A separate pitot tube (forward-facing intake) and static vent (side-facing openings) system is simpler and more reliable. Each measures its specific pressure without trying to do double duty. If one gets blocked, the other still works. Separate systems are preferred on modern aircraft because they're transparent: you can see and clear the pitot easily, and the static vent is usually accessible. Combined heads were popular in older designs but they can confuse ice formation or water ingestion—a combined head that ices over loses both pressures at once. Most modern general aviation aircraft have separate systems. On a piston twin, you'll almost certainly have a separate pitot tube and static vent. The combined head shows up on older singles or experimental aircraft.
exam_tip: Single pitot head: compact, both pressures in one probe, more blockage risk if it ices or plugs. Separate: pitot tube and static vent, transparent, fail independently. Separate is more reliable.
key_facts: Single pitot head: one probe measures pitot and static through internal passages
Single head: compact but higher blockage risk from ice or water
Separate pitot tube: forward-facing, measures total (pitot) pressure only
Separate static vent: side-facing, measures static pressure only
Separate systems: more reliable, each fails independently
If single head ices over: both ASI and ALT/VSI fail simultaneously
If separate pitot ices: ASI fails, ALT/VSI work
If separate static ices: ASI unreliable, ALT/VSI fail
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of pitot heat.
concept: Pitot heat is an electrical heating element wrapped around the pitot tube, powered by the aircraft electrical system. Its sole function is to prevent ice from forming on the pitot tube. You turn it on before entering known icing, or proactively if you're climbing through a layer where icing is expected. The heater warms the tube to 40–50°C, well above the freezing point of water. Without pitot heat, even light icing will plug the pitot opening, and your airspeed indicator becomes unreliable or stops functioning. A blocked pitot initially reads low (because you've got static pressure leaking in through a partial blockage), then as the blockage gets worse, the reading becomes completely unreliable. If the pitot becomes completely plugged, the ASI reading might freeze or fluctuate wildly. Pitot heat is essential for any operation in known or suspected icing. On the ground, you leave it off (no need, and it draws electrical power). In cruise in clear conditions, it's off. Approaching a cloud layer with temperature and moisture conducive to icing, you switch it on. Once clear of icing (lower altitude or warmer air), you turn it off. Pitot heat power draw is small but constant while on, so it's not something you'll forget you've activated (unlike landing gear, which you select once).
exam_tip: Pitot heat: prevents ice accumulation on pitot tube. ON before entering known icing, OFF after exiting. Power draw small but continuous. Loss of pitot heat = risk of ASI failure in icing.
key_facts: Pitot heat: electrical heater element on pitot tube
Heats tube to 40–50°C, prevents ice formation
Activated before known or suspected icing
Deactivated when exiting icing conditions
Blocked pitot: ASI reads low initially, then unreliable
Completely plugged pitot: ASI frozen or fluctuates
Pitot heat power draw: continuous while on, small amperage
Essential for any icing operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting an ASI: (a) density error (IAS/TAS relationship); (b) position (pressure) error; (c) compressibility error.
concept: Your ASI reads indicated airspeed (IAS) based on the dynamic pressure (pitot - static). At sea level on a standard day, IAS equals true airspeed (TAS). But IAS is always lower than TAS at altitude because the air is thinner; there's less dynamic pressure for a given true speed. Density error is the difference between IAS and TAS as altitude increases. You calculate TAS from IAS using temperature and altitude: a rough rule is TAS ≈ IAS + (2% per 1000 feet). So at 10,000 feet, TAS might be 20% higher than IAS. Position error (pressure error) happens because the static vent location isn't perfectly in free static air. Near the fuselage, air accelerates and pressure varies slightly depending on aircraft attitude, power setting, and configuration. If you're climbing, the airflow around the static vent changes, and the vent reads slightly different pressure than true static. This error is usually small (2–5 knots) and documented for your aircraft in the POH. Compressibility error occurs at higher speeds where the air near the pitot tube is compressed, raising pressure slightly above the true dynamic pressure. At 200 IAS, it's negligible. At 400+ IAS (rare on piston twins), it becomes noticeable. The result is IAS reads slightly high. For CPL operations on piston twins in NZ, density error is the main correction: always remember that your indicated airspeed is not your true airspeed at altitude.
exam_tip: Density error: main one. IAS lower than TAS at altitude. Rule of thumb: TAS ≈ IAS + 2% per 1000 feet. Position error: static vent location effect, small. Compressibility: high speed only, negligible on twins.
key_facts: Density error: IAS < TAS at altitude due to thinner air
TAS ≈ IAS + (2% × altitude in 1000 ft)
At 10,000 ft: TAS ≈ IAS × 1.20 (roughly)
Position (pressure) error: static vent location effect, 2–5 knots typical
Position error varies with aircraft attitude, power, configuration
Compressibility error: IAS reads high at high speed (rare on twins)
Compressibility significant above 250 IAS approx
Instrument error: ASI calibration tolerance ±5 knots
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the pre-flight and in-flight serviceability checks for an ASI.
concept: Pre-flight checks: on the ground with the aircraft stationary and the engine off, your ASI should read zero. If it reads anything else, it's got an internal fault or pressure leak. Check that the pitot tube is uncovered and clear (no dust caps left on, no insects blocking it). Check the pitot heat switch is in the correct position (usually OFF on the ground). Verify the static vent is unobstructed. If you've got an alternate static valve, check it's in the normal (or correct) position. In flight, monitor the ASI: if it suddenly drops below zero (which shouldn't happen), suspect a pitot blockage. If it fluctuates erratically, suspect a partial blockage or a pressure leak. If you're climbing and the ASI drops when you'd expect it to stabilise or climb slightly, suspect position error or actual slowdown. Compare the ASI to your other senses: if the noise and feel suggest a reasonable airspeed but the ASI is low, suspect pitot icing (turn on pitot heat if you haven't already). If you're approaching an airfield and the ASI suddenly goes very high (reading 50+ knots higher than expected), suspect pitot static separation (the pitot became un-iced or freed up). Landing with a reliable ASI is non-negotiable, so if there's any doubt, declare and request a GPS-based speed reference or another aircraft to check your groundspeed.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: ASI zero on ground, pitot uncovered, static vent clear. In-flight: monitor for erratic movement, sudden changes, blockage indications. Pitot heat ON in icing; OFF when clear.
key_facts: Pre-flight: ASI should read zero on ground, engines off
Pitot tube: check uncovered, no dust caps, no debris
Static vent: check unobstructed
Pitot heat: OFF on ground (unless pre-start check specified)
In-flight: monitor for sudden drops (blockage), fluctuation (partial block)
Blocked pitot: ASI unreliable or frozen
Pitot icing: ASI drops then may freeze; apply pitot heat
Position error: ASI varies with climb/descent; documented in POH
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of an altimeter.
concept: Your altimeter is a sensitive barometer that measures static pressure and converts it to altitude. Inside the altimeter is a stack of evacuated (partially vacuum) bellows. As static pressure decreases (you climb), the bellows expand (the internal vacuum pressure stays constant, but external pressure is lower, so the bellows push outward). As static pressure increases (you descend), the bellows contract. The mechanical movement of the bellows is geared to the altitude needles: a 1000-foot needle (thick needle, moves fast) and a 100-foot needle (thin needle, moves slower) and sometimes a 10-foot pointer (small triangular indicator). The scale is calibrated assuming a standard atmosphere: 29.92 inches of mercury (Hg) at sea level, decreasing at a standard rate with altitude. But real atmosphere varies: on a hot day, air expands and is less dense, so static pressure is lower than standard, and your altimeter reads higher than true altitude (called density altitude). On a cold day, the opposite. That's why you set the altimeter to a known altitude (field elevation on the ground) using the QNH or QFE setting knob. QNH is the barometric pressure adjusted to sea level (what you set on the ground, matching the field elevation). QFE is the actual barometric pressure at the field (rarely used in NZ). Once set correctly on the ground, your altimeter should display the field elevation. In flight, you leave the setting alone and trust the altimeter, understanding that altitude error is possible if temperature varies significantly from standard.
exam_tip: Altimeter: bellows expand/contract with pressure change. Needles show thousands and hundreds of feet. Set to QNH before flight (should read field elevation). Density altitude error: hot = high reading, cold = low reading.
key_facts: Altimeter: evacuated bellows respond to static pressure change
Bellows expand when climbing (lower external pressure)
Bellows contract when descending (higher external pressure)
Two needles: 1000-ft (thick) and 100-ft (thin)
Calibrated for standard atmosphere: 29.92 inHg at sea level
QNH: barometric pressure adjusted to sea level; set before flight
QFE: actual barometric pressure at field (rare in NZ)
Error: altimeter reads higher on hot days (density altitude effect)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the meanings of the subscale settings QNH and QFE.
concept: QNH is the barometric pressure at the airfield elevation adjusted (reduced) to sea level. It's the standard setting used in aviation worldwide. You set QNH on your altimeter's subscale knob before flight. Once set, the altimeter displays altitude above mean sea level (MSL) throughout the flight. At the airfield, the altimeter should read the field's published elevation. QNH changes with weather: on a high-pressure day, QNH is high (e.g., 1030 mb); on a low-pressure day, it's low (e.g., 990 mb). You get QNH from ATIS, ATC, or the flight service station. QFE is the actual barometric pressure at the field itself (not adjusted to sea level). If you set QFE on your altimeter, it reads zero when you're on the airfield and shows altitude above that field (field elevation = 0, 5000 feet above field = 5000 feet). QFE is useful for circuit work: you're always thinking in terms of height above the field. But in cruise or cross-country, it's confusing because you'd be thinking in MSL terms. In NZ, QNH is standard for all flight operations. QFE is rarely used. Some countries (historically the UK) preferred QFE for visual circuits, but modern practice globally is QNH. You'll set QNH before every flight and leave it set unless specifically instructed otherwise (e.g., military low-level flying might use QFE for operational reasons).
exam_tip: QNH: pressure at field adjusted to sea level. Altimeter reads MSL altitude. Standard setting everywhere. Set before flight, verify reads field elevation. QFE: actual field pressure; reads zero at field. Rarely used in NZ.
key_facts: QNH: barometric pressure adjusted to sea level, standard setting
QNH varies with weather: high pressure = high QNH, low = low QNH
Altimeter set to QNH: reads altitude above mean sea level (MSL)
At field with QNH set: altimeter should read field elevation
QFE: actual barometric pressure at field (not adjusted)
Altimeter set to QFE: reads zero at field, height above field in flight
QFE useful for circuit flying, confusing for cruise
NZ standard: always use QNH unless specifically instructed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of pressure setting error on altimeter indication.
concept: If you set the wrong QNH (say, you set 1020 mb when the correct QNH is 1030 mb), your altimeter will be off by the difference. With an incorrect setting, you're essentially telling the altimeter a wrong sea-level pressure, so it calculates altitude incorrectly relative to that false reference. Here's the relationship: every 1 mb difference in QNH setting results in roughly 27 feet of altitude error. If you're 10 mb off, you'll be off by about 270 feet. If you accidentally set 1020 instead of 1030, you're 10 mb low, so your altimeter reads 270 feet higher than true altitude. You think you're at 5000 feet, but you're actually at 4730 feet. On approach, this is serious: you might descend into terrain thinking you're higher than you are. On the ground before flight, you always verify the altimeter is set correctly by checking it against the known field elevation. If it reads more than ±75 feet from field elevation (allowing for instrument tolerance), the altimeter's unserviceable or your QNH is wrong. In flight, if you receive a new QNH (pressure changes as weather moves), you adjust the subscale. ATC will give you new QNH at transitions or handoffs: 'New QNH 1024 mb'. You set it immediately. Forgetting to update QNH when transitioning from one airfield to another with different pressure is a common error and a potential accident cause.
exam_tip: Pressure setting error: 1 mb difference = ~27 feet altitude error. Set QNH before flight (altimeter reads field elevation ±75 ft). Update QNH when given new setting by ATC. Wrong QNH = altitude error = terrain risk.
key_facts: QNH error: 1 mb difference ≈ 27 feet altitude error
Set QNH before flight; altimeter should read field elevation ±75 ft
If error >75 ft, either QNH is wrong or altimeter is unserviceable
Receiving new QNH from ATC: set immediately
High-pressure system: QNH higher than standard (1030+ mb)
Low-pressure system: QNH lower than standard (990-1000 mb)
In climb, QNH decreases (weather depression moves) → update periodically
Wrong QNH in descent = risk of descending below safe altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of air mass temperature, at constant QNH, on altimeter indication.
concept: Temperature affects air density, which affects how static pressure changes with altitude. In a standard atmosphere (assumed to be 15°C at sea level, decreasing at 1.98°C per 1000 feet), the pressure gradient is predictable. But if you're flying in a warmer air mass at constant QNH, air is less dense, and the pressure decreases more slowly with altitude. Your altimeter, which assumes the standard temperature lapse rate, will read higher than true altitude. On a hot day, the actual air mass is warmer than standard, so you're higher than the altimeter says. On a cold day, the air mass is colder than standard, so you're lower than the altimeter says. This is called 'temperature error' or the 'lapse rate error'. The rule of thumb: for every 10°C deviation from the standard temperature, you'll have about 4% altitude error. If it's 35°C (20°C hotter than standard 15°C), you'll read about 8% low. At 5000 feet, that's 400 feet—a significant error. This is why altimeter accuracy decreases in extreme temperatures. In NZ summer heat at Ardmore or Christchurch, a hot day can introduce noticeable temperature error. The only way to correct for this accurately is to know the actual temperature aloft and calculate true altitude, or to use a more sophisticated instrument. For practical CPL flying, you're aware of the error and you add a safety margin (fly higher than minimums if it's hot, or use GPS altitude as a cross-check).
exam_tip: Temperature error: hot air = altimeter reads high (you're lower than indicated). Cold air = altimeter reads low (you're higher than indicated). Every 10°C variation ≈ 4% altitude error. On hot days, add safety margin to minimums.
key_facts: Standard atmosphere: 15°C at sea level, -1.98°C per 1000 feet
Warmer than standard: air less dense, pressure decreases slowly, altimeter reads high
Colder than standard: air more dense, pressure decreases fast, altimeter reads low
Error magnitude: ~4% per 10°C deviation from standard
At 5000 feet, 20°C hot day: potential 400-foot error (reads high)
At 5000 feet, 20°C cold day: potential 400-foot error (reads low)
Extreme heat (e.g., Christchurch summer): significant temperature error
Mitigation: use GPS altitude, add safety margin to minimums
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting an altimeter: (a) instrument error; (b) position error; (c) lag.
concept: Instrument error (mechanical tolerance) is the calibration accuracy of the altimeter itself. Factory-built altimeters are tested and calibrated, but they have a tolerance: typically ±100 feet over the operating range (some are better, some worse). This is documented in your aircraft's calibration certificate. You check it on the ground: if the altimeter reads within ±75 feet of the known field elevation with correct QNH, it's serviceable. Position error (sometimes called 'installation error') is due to the location of the static vent on the fuselage. In climb or descent, or with changes in power and configuration, the airflow around the static vent changes, and the vent reads slightly different pressure than true static. This causes the altimeter to read slightly high or low depending on the aircraft's attitude and power setting. It's usually small (±50–100 feet) and documented in the POH. Lag is the time delay in the altimeter's response to pressure changes. The bellows and needle mechanism are mechanical and have mass and friction; they don't respond instantly. If you're climbing and the pressure is dropping, the altimeter might lag 10–20 seconds before the needle catches up. This is rarely a problem in normal flying, but in a steep or rapid climb/descent, you might be 100+ feet off from the lag. Lag is more noticeable in the VSI, which is designed to detect rate but which lags several seconds initially.
exam_tip: Instrument error: ±100 feet typical tolerance, checked on ground. Position error: ±50–100 feet depending on configuration, documented in POH. Lag: mechanical delay, significant in rapid climbs/descents.
key_facts: Instrument error: factory calibration tolerance ±100 feet
Checked on ground: should read field elevation ±75 ft with correct QNH
If error >75 ft on ground, altimeter unserviceable
Position error: static vent location effect, ±50–100 feet
Position error varies with climb/descent, power, configuration
Position error documented in POH as 'installation error'
Lag: mechanical response delay, 10–20 seconds typical
Lag more significant in rapid climbs/descents (rare in GA)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the serviceability checks and the accepted indication tolerances of an altimeter.
concept: Serviceability checks are done on the ground before flight. Set QNH correctly (get it from ATIS, ATC, or the ground station). The altimeter should read the field's known elevation. Acceptable tolerance is ±75 feet. If it's off by more than 75 feet, the altimeter is unserviceable and the aircraft is not airworthy until it's fixed. The tolerance accounts for instrument error (±100 feet possible) and position error. You're accepting a small error as inevitable but within limits. In some jurisdictions, stricter rules apply: some operators require ±50 feet tolerance. Check your POH and local regulations. If the altimeter reads more than 75 feet off, note it in the technical log and ground the aircraft. A technician will need to troubleshoot (check the static system for leaks, inspect the altimeter itself, and possibly replace it). Once the aircraft is airborne, you can't easily verify the altimeter short of checking it against known ground elevation (passing over an airfield, for example) or comparing it to GPS altitude (though GPS altitude itself has limitations). In NZ, CAANZ allows ±75 feet on the ground for serviceable altimeters. If your altimeter reads 100 feet high on the ground, it's no-go.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: set QNH, altimeter should read field elevation ±75 ft. If >75 ft off, altimeter is unserviceable. Document in technical log. No-go until repaired.
key_facts: Pre-flight serviceability check: set QNH, compare to field elevation
Acceptable tolerance: ±75 feet (some operators ±50 feet)
If error >75 feet on ground, altimeter unserviceable
Error source: instrument tolerance, position error, or system leak
If unserviceable: ground aircraft, document technical log, arrange repair
In-flight: monitor altimeter for sudden changes or inconsistencies
Periodic checks: every 24 months (subject to local regulation)
Altimeter test and certification required for airworthiness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a vertical speed indicator (VSI).
concept: Your VSI (also called a rate of descent/ascent indicator or variometer) shows how fast you're climbing or descending in feet per minute. It measures the rate of change of static pressure, not the absolute pressure. Here's how it works: internally, the VSI has a capsule connected directly to the static pressure line (fast connection, responds quickly) and a reference chamber also connected to static but through a metering restriction (slow connection, responds slowly). When you start climbing, static pressure drops. The capsule responds immediately and pressure inside drops, but the reference chamber drops slowly because of the restriction. This pressure difference between the two is what moves the needle: climb = capsule pressure lower than reference = needle up. As you settle into steady climb, both equalize and the needle settles to show the actual climb rate. The metering restriction (orifice or capillary tube) is calibrated to give a delay of about 6 seconds, so the VSI shows you a smoothed, average climb rate. If you suddenly pitch up, the static pressure drops immediately, the capsule reacts, and the needle jumps up—but then it damps back down as the reference chamber equalizes. This damping smooths out turbulence and momentary pitch changes, showing you the sustained climb rate. On the ground with static pressure constant, the reference and capsule are equal, and the needle should centre on zero. It usually has a ±50-foot-per-minute calibration tolerance.
exam_tip: VSI: capsule responds to instant pressure change, reference chamber lags through metering restriction. Difference creates climb rate indication. On ground, should read ±50 fpm of zero.
key_facts: VSI: direct connection capsule (fast) and metering-restricted reference (slow)
Pressure difference between capsule and reference = climb rate signal
Climb: capsule pressure drops faster than reference = needle up
Descent: capsule pressure rises faster than reference = needle down
Metering restriction: orifice or capillary, calibrated 6-second lag
Damping effect: turbulence smoothed, sustained rate shown
On ground: should read ±50 fpm zero (or very close to zero)
Lag: initial response delayed 6 seconds; then accurate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors affecting a VSI: (a) position error; (b) lag.
concept: Position error in the VSI arises from the same source as in the altimeter: the static vent location. If the vent is affected by airflow variations (climb/descent, power, configuration), the pressure it measures isn't true static. In a climb, the vent might read slightly lower pressure than it should, making the rate of pressure drop seem steeper, so the VSI reads high (shows too much climb). In descent, the opposite. This error is usually small (±100–200 feet per minute) and varies with aircraft configuration. It's documented in the POH if significant. Lag is the mechanical response delay. The VSI has a 6-second designed lag from the metering restriction. When you first pitch up or down, the VSI doesn't respond for a couple of seconds—it's waiting for the reference chamber to equalize. Then it catches up. This means if you've just started a climb, the VSI might show zero for the first few seconds, then gradually increase. In an autopilot-guided descent, the VSI lags behind, so the autopilot over-corrects: it sees a larger descent than actual (because the VSI lags), so it pitches up more, causing oscillations. This is why autopilots on large aircraft have VSI inputs filtered and damped further. For a pilot hand-flying, the lag is something to be aware of: you're doing a steep approach and the VSI is sluggish, so you rely on pitch attitude and altimeter trend as much as the VSI reading.
exam_tip: Position error: static vent effect, ±100–200 fpm typical. Lag: 6-second designed delay, noticeable in rapid changes. VSI sluggish on initial pitch change; trust pitch attitude and altimeter trend.
key_facts: Position error: static vent location effect, ±100–200 fpm
Position error varies with climb/descent, power, configuration
Climb: position error can make VSI read high
Descent: position error can make VSI read low
Lag: 6-second metering restriction intentional for smoothing
Lag means delayed response to pitch changes
Initially after pitch change: VSI reads zero for first few seconds
Recovery: VSI gradually shows actual rate as delay passes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the serviceability checks for a VSI.
concept: On the ground with static system at ambient pressure and the aircraft stationary, the VSI should read zero (or very close to zero, within ±50 feet per minute). If it reads significantly above or below zero, the instrument has a leak or internal mechanical issue. A sticky needle is common: the damping mechanism (metering orifice) gets plugged with dust or moisture, and the VSI becomes sluggish or freezes. If it's frozen at climb or descent, it's unreliable. Another check: after starting the engine and allowing time for static system pressure to stabilise, the VSI should still read approximately zero (it might show a slight trend during startup as pressures equalize, but it should settle). If it's showing a sustained climb or descent while parked with engines running, something's wrong with the static system or the instrument. In flight, you monitor the VSI: does it agree with your pitch attitude and altimeter trend? If you're in a climb and the VSI shows descent, suspect a static leak or a failed instrument. If the VSI is sluggish (doesn't respond to pitch changes), suspect moisture in the orifice or a sticky mechanism. A completely failed VSI (stuck needle) is a nuisance but not immediately catastrophic—you can hand-fly using pitch attitude and altimeter cross-checks. But it should be repaired at the next maintenance opportunity.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: VSI reads ±50 fpm zero on ground, engines off. If >50 fpm off, unserviceable. In-flight: monitor agreement with pitch and altitude trend. Sluggish or stuck VSI = suspect moisture or mechanical failure.
key_facts: Ground check: VSI reads ±50 fpm zero, engines off
If off by >50 fpm: unserviceable, moisture or mechanical failure suspected
After engine start: VSI should settle to zero once pressures stabilise
Sustained climb/descent while parked: static leak or instrument failure
In-flight: monitor for agreement with pitch attitude and altimeter
Sluggish response: moisture in metering orifice (most common)
Frozen needle: mechanical sticking or orifice blockage
Completely failed VSI: hand-fly using pitch and altimeter cross-checks
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: With regard to blockages and leaks in the pitot-static system of the various pressure instruments, explain the: (a) instrument symptoms; (b) indication errors; (c) possible remedies available.
concept: A blocked pitot tube: ASI drops (shows low airspeed) or becomes unreliable or frozen. ALT and VSI unaffected. If pitot ice is suspected, turning on pitot heat usually recovers the ASI within seconds. If it doesn't recover, the blockage is persistent (debris, not ice) and you'll need to land. A partially blocked pitot shows erratic ASI. A blocked static vent (or vents): ASI becomes unreliable (it reads airspeed based on pitot - static, so without good static, it's wrong). ALT and VSI both fail (they depend on static). The altimeter might read high (if static is partially blocked, pressure is relatively high). The VSI freezes or reads zero (no pressure change sensed). Symptoms: in a climb with a blocked static, the ASI might stay constant or increase when it should decrease, the ALT doesn't climb, the VSI reads zero. A complete static blockage is devastating: you've lost three instruments. An electrical leak in the system (crack in a line or poor connection): creates a small pressure loss. Symptoms are subtle: ASI reads slightly high (dynamic pressure leaks away), ALT reads slightly high (static pressure leaks, so the bellows thinks pressure is lower, altitude appears higher). VSI becomes sluggish (pressure change is slower through the leak). Remedies: for blocked pitot, apply pitot heat, or if that fails, land immediately. For blocked static, switch to the alternate static system (if equipped)—this uses cabin pressure or an alternate vent. If no alternate, you're flying on unreliable instruments. For leaks, check all tubing connections, look for cracks or loose clamps, and apply sealant or replace tubing as needed. Ground-based troubleshooting: use a pressure tester to blow through the pitot and static lines and verify airflow and pressure.
exam_tip: Blocked pitot: ASI fails, ALT/VSI ok. Blocked static: ASI unreliable, ALT/VSI fail, frozen or zero VSI. Leak: all three sluggish or erratic. Remedies: pitot heat for icing, alternate static for blockage, tubing inspection for leaks.
key_facts: Blocked pitot: ASI low or frozen, ALT/VSI normal
Pitot heat recovers ice blockage within seconds
Persistent pitot blockage: land immediately
Blocked static: ASI erratic, ALT high, VSI zero/frozen
Alternate static system: cabin pressure or alternate vent, switch if static blocked
Without alternate static: three-instrument failure
Static leak: all three instruments sluggish or slightly erratic
Leak repairs: tubing inspection, connections, sealant or replacement
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the function and operation of the alternate static system.
concept: An alternate static system is a backup source of static pressure if the main static vent becomes blocked. Most equipped aircraft have a switch or knob labelled 'Alt Static' or 'Static Alternate' somewhere on the flight deck. When selected to 'alternate', the system draws static pressure from inside the cabin or cockpit instead of from the external fuselage vent. The cabin is not at exact static pressure because it's pressurised (or semi-pressurised), but it's close enough for instrument operation in a pinch. Alternatively, some systems route alternate static to a separate external vent on the fuselage (maybe facing slightly aft where blockage is less likely). When you switch to alternate static, the pitot-static instruments suddenly behave differently: the ASI might jump (because cabin pressure is slightly different from external static), and the ALT might shift by 100–200 feet. It's not comfortable, but the instruments become usable again. The procedure if you suspect main static blocked: verify the blockage (ASI erratic, ALT not climbing, VSI frozen), declare the issue to ATC, select alternate static, and check if instruments stabilise. They should. If they still misbehave, you've got a bigger problem (possibly both static sources blocked or a leak). In older or simpler aircraft, there's no alternate static: you're stuck with whatever happens. That's why regular pitot-static maintenance and checking is important. The alternate static system is simple electrically—usually just a selector valve solenoid or manual three-way valve—so failures are rare, but the whole system needs to be checked during maintenance.
exam_tip: Alternate static: switches from external vent to cabin or alternate external vent. Use if main static blocked. Instruments may jump initially; then stabilise. Declare issue to ATC if you select alternate.
key_facts: Alternate static: backup source if main static vent blocked
Sources: cabin pressure or alternate external vent
Selection: switch or knob labelled 'Alt Static' or equivalent
Cabin pressure alternate: convenient, slightly non-standard pressure
External alternate vent: more accurate but less common
Instrument change: ASI and ALT may shift 100–200 feet when selected
Once stabilised: instruments usable on alternate static
Maintenance: alternate system must be tested and certified
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical aircraft vacuum (suction system).
concept: Your vacuum system (also called a suction system) provides negative pressure that drives gyroscopic instruments like the artificial horizon, directional indicator, and turn indicator. The system starts with a vacuum pump, usually engine-driven (mounted on the engine and spun by a pulley or gearbox) or electrically-driven (spinning from the aircraft electrical system). The pump creates negative pressure by drawing air from the system, which lowers pressure below atmospheric. This reduced pressure is fed through tubing to the flight deck instruments. A pressure regulator maintains steady vacuum in the 4–5.5 inHg range (a standard range for aircraft gyro instruments). Without regulation, the pump would create excessive vacuum and damage the instruments or make them unstable. A filter removes dust and debris before the pump inlet. The system exhausts air outside the aircraft (through the fuselage or to ambient). A relief valve prevents excessive vacuum build-up if the regulator fails. Some systems have an isolation solenoid valve (electric) that can shut off vacuum supply for testing or emergency isolation. A vacuum gauge on the flight deck shows the pilot the vacuum state: if it drops below 4.5 inHg, the gyro instruments become sluggish or unreliable. If it's too high (over 5.5 inHg), the gyros might overspeed and become erratic. Checking the vacuum gauge before flight is routine: you want a steady reading in the green band (typically 4.5–5.5 inHg). A vacuum system is simple but essential—without it, your gyro instruments are useless.
exam_tip: Vacuum system: pump driven by engine, creates negative pressure. Regulator holds 4–5 inHg steady. Feeds turn indicator, AI, DI. Gauge shows system pressure. Green band 4.5–5.5 inHg. Low vacuum = instruments sluggish.
key_facts: Vacuum pump: engine-driven (belt/gear) or electric motor
Pump: creates negative pressure by drawing air
Pressure regulator: maintains 4–5.5 inHg (standard range)
Without regulator: pump would overspeed instruments
Filter: removes dust and debris from intake
Relief valve: prevents excessive vacuum if regulator fails
Vacuum gauge: shows system pressure, should read 4.5–5.5 inHg
System exhausts: outside aircraft to ambient
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between suction pump, venturi, and positive pressure systems.
concept: A suction pump system uses a pump (engine-driven or electric) to actively draw air out and create negative pressure. This is the most common and most reliable. A venturi system uses aerodynamic lift: a venturi tube mounted on the exterior of the aircraft creates a pressure difference (low pressure) as air flows through it at speed. This low pressure is fed to the gyro instruments. The advantage is no moving parts and no maintenance. The disadvantage is that venturi vacuum is proportional to airspeed—at low speed (takeoff, landing, holding), venturi pressure is weak and instruments become sluggish. It's suitable for cruise-only aircraft but unreliable for low-speed flying. A positive pressure system (used on some pressurised aircraft and turboprops) uses engine bleed air routed directly to instruments instead of vacuum. The air pressure is controlled by a regulator. The advantage is that pressure is available at all speeds and power settings. The disadvantage is it consumes bleed air (reducing engine efficiency) and is more complex. For most piston twins in NZ, a suction pump system is standard. Venturi systems appear on older light singles. Positive pressure is rare on piston aircraft (more common on jets and turboprops). A vacuum gauge tells you which system is working: on the ground, a suction pump should still show 4–5 inHg even with engines off (if there's an electric pump), but a venturi shows zero on the ground (no airflow). In flight, both show steady pressure if working correctly.
exam_tip: Suction pump: active, reliable, works on ground and flight. Venturi: passive, airspeed-dependent, weak at low speed. Positive pressure: engine bleed, complex, rare on pistons. Most twins: suction pump.
key_facts: Suction pump: engine-driven or electric pump, active vacuum
Suction pump: most reliable, works at all speeds
Venturi: aerodynamic low pressure, airspeed-dependent
Venturi: weak at low speed, unsuitable for takeoff/landing
Positive pressure: engine bleed air routed directly
Positive pressure: complex, bleed air loss, rare on pistons
Ground check: suction pump shows 4–5 inHg, venturi shows zero
In-flight: all systems should show steady pressure if serviceable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the inherent properties of a spinning gyroscope rotor.
concept: A spinning gyroscope has two fundamental properties: rigidity and precession. Rigidity in space means the rotor maintains its plane of spin relative to inertial space (absolute space), not relative to the aircraft. If the rotor is spun up around the vertical axis (yaw axis), it remains stable in that orientation even as the aircraft pitches or rolls. This is what makes gyroscopes useful for navigation and attitude reference: the gyro 'remembers' its orientation independent of the aircraft's motion. Precession is the second property: if you apply a force to a spinning rotor perpendicular to its axis, the rotor doesn't move in the direction of the force but instead precesses (rotates) around an axis perpendicular to both the spin axis and the applied force. This 90-degree lag is crucial to how gyro instruments work. For example, in a turn indicator, the rotor spins around a horizontal axis (fore-aft). When the aircraft yaws (turns), the vertical axis of the aircraft rotates, which applies a deflecting force to the gyro rotor. Instead of the rotor tilting directly, it precesses, and the precession is sensed and displayed as a rate-of-turn needle. Without precession, the turn indicator wouldn't work—it would just tilt without indicating anything useful. These two properties—rigidity and precession—are inherent to all spinning rotors and are the foundation of all gyroscopic aircraft instruments.
exam_tip: Rigidity: rotor maintains plane of spin in inertial space (independent of aircraft motion). Precession: deflecting force causes 90° rotation response. Both properties fundamental to gyro instrument operation.
key_facts: Rigidity in space: rotor maintains orientation relative to inertial space
Rigidity: gyro doesn't follow aircraft motion; stays 'fixed' in space
Precession: deflecting force causes rotor to rotate perpendicular to force
Precession lag: 90-degree offset between force and response
Rigidity: basis of directional indicator (maintains heading)
Precession: basis of turn indicator (senses yaw rate)
Both properties: inherent to all spinning gyroscopes
Combined: enable gyro instruments to measure attitude and rates
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by the term gimbal.
concept: A gimbal is a pivoting ring or support that allows a gyro rotor to maintain freedom of movement while keeping its axis of spin oriented correctly. Think of it as a universal joint. A rotor needs to be supported and spun, but it must be free to precess (rotate in response to aircraft motion) without that precession being resisted by the support structure. A single gimbal is a ring that pivots on one axis, allowing the rotor to tilt on that axis while spinning. A two-gimbal system (used in most aircraft gyros) has two concentric rings, each pivoting on a different axis, allowing the rotor to move in two directions simultaneously. A three-gimbal system (used in some high-performance applications) allows free movement on all three axes. In a typical aircraft artificial horizon, the rotor spins around a horizontal fore-aft axis, and it's mounted in gimbals that allow it to pivot up and down (pitch) and side-to-side (roll) as the aircraft moves. The gimbals transmit the rotor's orientation to the mechanical needle or display, which shows the pilot the aircraft's attitude. Without gimbals, the rotor would be rigidly mounted, the aircraft's motion would twist the rotor out of true, and the instrument would be useless. Gimbals are simple mechanical devices (just pivot bearings and rings), but they're essential to gyro instrument operation. Friction and wear in gimbals can cause erratic instrument behaviour, which is why bearing wear is a maintenance concern.
exam_tip: Gimbal: pivot support allowing rotor to move while spinning. One gimbal: single-axis. Two gimbal: two-axis motion. Three gimbal: three-axis (rare). Gimbals allow precession without resistance.
key_facts: Gimbal: pivoting support allowing rotor freedom of movement
Single gimbal: one pivot axis
Two-gimbal: two pivot axes, typical in aircraft gyros
Three-gimbal: three pivot axes, prevents gimbal lock
Gimbal lock: condition where two axes align, losing one degree of freedom
Gimbals transmit rotor orientation to mechanical output
Friction: gimbal bearing wear causes drift and erratic readings
Maintenance: gimbal condition checked as part of instrument service
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: List the types of gyroscope.
concept: Gyroscopes in aircraft are classified by their axis of spin (the direction the rotor spins). A vertical-axis gyroscope spins around the vertical axis (up-down), maintaining a fixed orientation in the horizontal plane. This is the basis of the directional indicator or heading indicator (DI/HI). A horizontal-axis gyroscope spins around a horizontal axis (fore-aft or left-right), and it's used to sense pitch and roll. The artificial horizon (attitude indicator) uses a horizontal-axis gyro spinning fore-aft; the rotor maintains a level orientation, and aircraft pitch and roll cause the rotor's gimbal orientation to shift, displaying attitude. The turn indicator uses a horizontal-axis gyro spinning left-right; aircraft yaw causes precession, which moves the needle to show rate of turn. Some aircraft have spin-axis variants for specific purposes, but the three main types are vertical-axis (heading reference), horizontal fore-aft axis (attitude), and horizontal left-right axis (turn rate). All three are often powered by the same vacuum system, so a single pump failure can disable all three—which is why cross-checking with the magnetic compass and hand-flying skills are essential.
exam_tip: Three main types: vertical-axis (DI/HI), horizontal fore-aft axis (AI/AH), horizontal left-right axis (TI/TC). All spin types use vacuum. Same pump = all fail together if pump fails.
key_facts: Vertical-axis gyro: spins up-down, maintains heading (DI/HI)
Horizontal fore-aft axis gyro: spins forward-backward, maintains attitude (AI/AH)
Horizontal left-right axis gyro: spins left-right, senses yaw (TI/TC)
All types powered by same vacuum system
Vacuum pump failure: all three instruments fail simultaneously
Backup: magnetic compass for heading, visual horizon for attitude
Hand-flying skills: essential if gyro instruments fail
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a turn indicator.
concept: A turn indicator (TI) is a gyroscopic instrument that shows the rate at which the aircraft is turning (turning to the left or right). The instrument houses a gyro rotor that spins horizontally (left-right axis). When the aircraft yaws (turns), the aircraft's vertical axis rotates, which applies a force perpendicular to the gyro's spin axis. Due to precession (the fundamental property of spinning gyroscopes), this force doesn't tilt the rotor but instead causes it to precess. The precession is mechanised—it moves the indicator needle. A quick turn causes rapid precession and a large needle deflection. A slow turn causes slow precession and a small deflection. The needle is calibrated to show rate of turn directly: typically, one needle width = 3 degrees per second of turn. A standard rate turn (used for instrument flying) is 3 degrees per second, so in a standard rate turn, the needle aligns with a mark on the dial (usually a small aircraft symbol). The beauty of a turn indicator is it responds immediately to yaw rate (no lag, unlike magnetic compass or other heading references), making it perfect for instrument flight in clouds or at night when visual reference is lost. The instrument is powered by vacuum, so loss of vacuum means loss of turn rate information. Most modern turn indicators are electronic (bearing an electric heading sensor and a display) rather than mechanical gyros, but the principle remains the same: measure rate of turn and display it.
exam_tip: Turn indicator: gyro spins left-right. Aircraft yaw causes precession. Needle deflection proportional to turn rate. Standard rate turn = 3°/sec = needle at mark. No vacuum = no turn indication.
key_facts: Turn indicator: horizontal left-right axis gyro
Senses yaw rate, shows turn direction and rate
Gyro precesses in response to aircraft yaw
Needle deflection = rate of turn (calibrated)
Standard rate turn: 3°/sec, needle at mark
Response: immediate, no lag (unlike magnetic compass)
Vacuum-powered: loss of vacuum = loss of indication
Modern versions: electronic, not mechanical gyro
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between a turn indicator (TI) and a turn co-ordinator (TC).
concept: A turn indicator (TI) responds to yaw (turning) only. Its gyro spins horizontally left-right, so it detects only rotation around the vertical axis. In straight-and-level flight, the needle stays centred. If you roll into a turn, the needle doesn't move until the aircraft yaws. If you slip (skidding or slipping condition with uncoordinated control input), the TI might not show anything if there's no yaw component. A turn co-ordinator (TC) is more sophisticated. It has a gyro spinning on an axis tilted 30–45 degrees from horizontal, so it responds to both yaw (turning) and roll (banking). When you bank the aircraft, the TC needle moves immediately to show the bank. As you establish a turn, the needle moves further to show the turn rate. The TC is better for instrument flying because it gives you information about both turning and rolling, helping you fly coordinated manoeuvres. A standard rate turn on a TC still aligns the needle with the standard rate mark. Many modern aircraft now have electronic display systems instead of mechanical TC instruments, but the principle is the same. The TC is more complete for instrument flying: it tells you the aircraft's attitude (pitch/roll) implicitly and the turn rate explicitly, whereas the TI only tells you turn rate.
exam_tip: TI: yaw only, doesn't show roll or bank immediately. TC: yaw and roll, needle moves on bank. TC better for instrument flying. Standard rate same for both: needle at mark = 3°/sec.
key_facts: Turn indicator (TI): senses yaw only, gyro horizontal left-right
TI: needle moves only when aircraft yaws
Turn co-ordinator (TC): senses yaw and roll, gyro tilted 30–45°
TC: needle moves on bank and yaw
TC better instrument-flying reference: shows attitude and rate
TI standard rate: 3°/sec, needle at mark
TC standard rate: same, 3°/sec at mark
Modern versions: electronic display, not mechanical gyro
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the errors which a turn indicator is subject to.
concept: A turn indicator responds to yaw rate only, so it's immune to pitch and roll changes (assuming no yaw component). The main error is instrument lag: when you first yaw, the rotor needs a moment to precess, and the needle takes a second or two to respond. In an aggressive turn entry, the needle might lag the actual turn rate by several seconds. This lag is minor and typically accepted. The second source of error is compass card or dial lag: once the gyro rotor detects yaw and precesses, the mechanical indicator (needle and dial) has inertia and can overshoot or oscillate before settling. This is rarely significant in practice. A third source is precession caused by aircraft acceleration or deceleration that creates forces on the gyro rotor apart from yaw. If the aircraft accelerates forward, inertia pushes the gyro backward, causing unwanted precession and a false turn indication. Similarly, a sudden altitude change or climb/descent can cause the rotor to precess if the gimbal friction isn't perfect. These effects are usually small and brief. The main practical error is understanding that the TI doesn't sense roll: in a coordinated turn, the TI needle will show turn rate, but the needle position doesn't tell you bank angle. You need the attitude indicator for bank information. That's why modern aircraft have turn co-ordinators instead—they sense both yaw and roll. In an old TI-only equipped aircraft, you cross-check with visual reference or attitude estimation.
exam_tip: TI errors: lag (few seconds), doesn't sense roll (need attitude reference), acceleration effects (minor). Understand it's yaw-only; cross-check with visual or attitude indicator.
key_facts: Lag: rotor precession delay, 1–2 seconds typical
Dial overshoot: mechanical inertia, rare in practice
Acceleration effects: forward accel can cause false turn indication
Roll insensitivity: doesn't sense banking directly
No bank angle info: need attitude indicator for bank
Altitude changes: can cause minor precession if gimbal friction high
Practical: accept lag, cross-check with attitude and visual reference
Modern aircraft: turn co-ordinator eliminates roll error
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of the balance ball.
concept: The balance ball (slip/skid indicator) is a simple mechanical device that shows whether your turn is coordinated. It's a ball rolling inside a curved glass tube mounted on the turn indicator or turn co-ordinator instrument. When the aircraft is in a coordinated turn, the ball centres in the tube. When the aircraft is skidding (turning too fast for the bank angle—turning too hard for the bank you've got), centrifugal force pushes the ball outward (away from the turn direction). When the aircraft is slipping (turning too slowly for the bank—banking harder than needed for the turn rate), the ball rolls inward (toward the turn direction). The pilot's job in a coordinated turn is to keep the ball centred, usually using rudder input. The rudder controls yaw; by adjusting rudder, you change the turn rate, which changes the balance of centrifugal force and gravity/lift, moving the ball until it centres. It's a feedback mechanism: if the ball is left of centre in a right turn, you're slipping, so you add right rudder to increase turn rate. If it's right of centre, you're skidding, so you add left rudder to decrease turn rate. The balance ball is simple, requires no power, and is immediately obvious to the pilot. In instrument flight, a coordinated turn keeps the aircraft stable and reduces stress on the airframe. The phrase 'needle, ball, airspeed' is a classic instrument scan for basic flying: check the turn indicator needle, check the ball for coordination, and check airspeed. The ball is part of every turn indicator and turn co-ordinator.
exam_tip: Balance ball: centred = coordinated. Left of centre in right turn = slipping (add inside rudder). Right of centre in right turn = skidding (add outside rudder). Simple, reliable, no power needed.
key_facts: Balance ball: ball in curved tube, no power required
Centred: turn is coordinated
Ball left (toward centre): slipping, add inside rudder
Ball right (toward outside): skidding, add outside rudder
Responds to: imbalance between centrifugal and gravitational forces
Used in: turn indicator, turn co-ordinator
Pilot technique: 'step on the ball' (add rudder in direction ball rolls)
Essential for: instrument flying, coordinated manoeuvres
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Interpret various TI and TC indications.
concept: Turn indicator indications are straightforward: needle pointing left = turning left, needle pointing right = turning right. The further the needle deflects, the faster the turn rate. The needle aligning with the standard rate mark (usually marked on the dial) indicates a standard rate turn of 3 degrees per second. On the ground, the needle should be centred (no turn). In flight during a level straight-and-level segment, the needle should be centred. During a turn, the needle deflects proportionally to yaw rate. Turn co-ordinator indications include both needle position (turn rate) and a small aircraft symbol: the symbol shows bank angle (tilts left or right), and the needle shows turn rate. An aircraft symbol level with wings horizontal indicates level flight. Tilted left or right indicates banking in that direction. The needle deflection superimposed on the aircraft symbol tells you both attitude and rate. Interpreting the ball: ball centred = coordinated, ball left = slipping (need inside rudder), ball right = skidding (need outside rudder). A practical scan on instruments: check the turn indicator/co-ordinator needle and ball together. In a level turn, the needle should show appropriate turn rate (say, 1/3 deflection for a shallow turn, full standard rate mark for a steeper turn) and the ball should be centred. If the ball is off-centre, adjust rudder to centre it while maintaining the desired turn rate. On a TC, also check the aircraft symbol for bank angle: is it tilted appropriately for the turn rate? If turn rate is high but bank is shallow, the aircraft is slipping and losing altitude.
exam_tip: TI needle: left = left turn, right = right turn. Standard rate = mark. Ball: centred = good. TC: aircraft symbol shows bank, needle shows rate. Scan both together.
key_facts: Needle deflection: proportional to yaw rate
Standard rate mark: TI/TC needle aligns when 3°/sec turn
Ground/level flight: needle centred
Ball centred: coordinated
Ball displaced: slipping or skidding, adjust rudder
TC aircraft symbol: level = level flight, tilted = banked
TC symbol tilt: indicates bank angle
Scan: needle, ball, airspeed, attitude together
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the serviceability checks for the TI/TC.
concept: On the ground before flight, the turn indicator or turn co-ordinator needle should be centred (no turn). If it's deflected, the instrument might have a mechanical sticking problem or the gyro might be spinning slower than normal. The vacuum gauge should read 4–5.5 inHg; if lower, the gyro spins slower and the instrument is sluggish or unreliable. After starting the engine and allowing a minute or two for vacuum to build, the needle should still be centred (once vacuum is stabilised). The balance ball should also be centred. During flight, you monitor the instrument: when you turn, the needle should respond immediately and smoothly. A sluggish or delayed response suggests low vacuum or mechanical friction. After completing a turn and rolling level, the needle should return to centre promptly (within a second or two). If it's slow or sticks, suspect vacuum loss or mechanical binding. The ball should move smoothly and return to centre when you centre the rudder. An erratic or sticking ball indicates mechanical damage. If you notice the instrument consistently reads off (e.g., needle slightly deflected during level flight), it might be a mechanical problem with the needle bearing. Modern electronic turn co-ordinators (which use an electronic rate sensor instead of a mechanical gyro) should power up and display a level aircraft symbol with a centred needle. If not, suspect an electrical or sensor fault. Any serviceability issue (sluggish, sticking, erratic, won't centre) means the instrument is unserviceable and must be repaired before the next flight.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: needle centred, ball centred, vacuum 4–5.5 inHg. In-flight: needle responds promptly, ball centred in coordinated flight. Sluggish or sticking = unserviceable.
key_facts: Ground check: needle centred, ball centred, vacuum 4–5.5 inHg
After engine start: vacuum builds, needle should remain centred
In-flight: prompt needle response to yaw, smooth motion
Turn completion: needle returns to centre quickly (1–2 sec)
Ball: smooth movement, centres when turn is coordinated
Sluggish response: low vacuum or mechanical friction
Sticking needle or ball: mechanical binding, unserviceable
Electronic TC: checks electrical power, sensor function
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a direction indicator/heading indicator (DI/HI).
concept: A direction indicator (or heading indicator, DI/HI) is a gyroscopic instrument that displays the aircraft's magnetic heading. Unlike a magnetic compass, the DI doesn't respond to Earth's magnetic field directly; instead, it uses a vertical-axis gyroscope to maintain a fixed heading reference. The gyro rotor spins around the vertical axis (up-down). Because of rigidity in space, the rotor maintains its orientation relative to inertial space. A heading card (disk) is geared to the rotor; as the gyro maintains its orientation, the card maintains a fixed heading display. The pilot aligns the heading card to the magnetic compass heading on the ground before flight (using a knob to adjust the card). Once set, the card doesn't change unless you manually adjust the knob or the gyro precesses (drifts). In flight, the DI displays the set heading even though no external reference is being sensed. This is useful because the DI has no lag (unlike the magnetic compass with its turning errors and acceleration errors) and it's smooth and stable. The disadvantage is the gyro drifts over time due to friction and mechanical imperfections in the gimbals (real drift or real wander), and it can topple if tilted excessively (in a steep dive or climb with unusual attitude, gimbal limits can be exceeded and the gyro flips). To maintain accuracy, you periodically synchronise the DI to the magnetic compass: you glance at the compass, note the difference, and reset the DI card using the knob. Standard practice is to reset every 10–15 minutes in flight.
exam_tip: DI/HI: vertical-axis gyro maintains heading. Rigidity keeps card fixed. Set to compass before flight. Drift requires periodic reset to compass. No lag or compass errors; much smoother for instrument flying.
key_facts: DI/HI: vertical-axis gyroscope, maintains magnetic heading
Rigidity in space: card maintains set heading independent of turns
Heading card: geared to gyro rotor, displays magnetic heading
Set-up: synchronise card to magnetic compass before flight
Adjustment knob: manual synchronisation to compass in flight
Advantages: no lag, no turning errors, smooth and stable
Disadvantages: drifts over time, needs periodic reset
Reset frequency: every 10–15 minutes during flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors which the DI/HI is subject to; (a) gimbal error; (b) real drift (or real wander); (c) apparent drift (or apparent wander); (d) low rotor speed.
concept: Gimbal error occurs when the gyro rotor axis tilts beyond its design limit, causing the gimbals to misalign. If you fly a very steep dive or climb or an unusual attitude, the rotor's physical supports (gimbals) hit their mechanical stops, and the rotor loses its alignment with the vertical axis. When you recover to normal flight, the rotor has toppled and the DI card now shows incorrect heading. This is a catastrophic error in the sense that the DI is now unreliable until reset to the compass. Real drift (or real wander) is gradual precession of the rotor due to friction in the gimbal bearings and other mechanical imperfections. Over time (typically 15–20 minutes depending on gyro quality), the rotor slowly precesses and the heading card drifts. This is why periodic reset to the compass is necessary. Apparent drift (or apparent wander) is drift that appears to occur but is actually caused by the aircraft's latitude change. As you fly north or south, the rate at which magnetic heading changes (due to Earth's curvature and variation change) differs from how your gyro perceives heading. At high latitudes, this effect is noticeable. Low rotor speed (below design RPM) causes reduced rigidity and increased drift. If vacuum pressure drops below 4.5 inHg, the rotor slows, and the DI becomes sluggish and drifts faster. Very low vacuum (near zero) makes the gyro unreliable. A 15-minute reset schedule is the standard practice to manage real drift. In practice, gimbal error is rare if you fly within normal operating limits. Real and apparent drift are managed by periodic synchronisation to the magnetic compass.
exam_tip: Gimbal error: steep attitude breaks gimbal limits, topples rotor. Real drift: friction causes slow precession, reset every 15 min. Apparent drift: latitude effect, managed by reset. Low vacuum: slows rotor, increases drift.
key_facts: Gimbal error: exceeding gimbal limits topples rotor (unusual attitudes)
Rotor topple: card shows incorrect heading after recovery
Real drift: friction in gimbals, slow precession over time
Real drift rate: 1–3°/minute typical (varies by quality)
Apparent drift: latitude-related magnetic heading change
Reset frequency: 15-minute standard schedule
Low rotor speed: reduces rigidity, increases drift rate
Low vacuum: <4.5 inHg, rotor slows, DI unreliable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of a toppled directional gyro (DI/HI).
concept: A toppled DI rotor has lost its alignment with the vertical axis. The rotor is tilted, and the heading card is no longer level. Characteristics of a toppled DI include: the heading card will show erratic or nonsensical headings (it might show multiple headings simultaneously if viewed from different angles), the card might be tilted or off-level, and most obviously, the card will show incorrect heading compared to the magnetic compass. If you topple the DI in a dive (by exceeding gimbal limits), you might recover from the dive and roll level, but the DI card will be tilted and showing wrong heading. You can't rely on the reading. To verify topple, compare the DI heading to your magnetic compass: if they differ by more than a small amount (say, >10 degrees) and the DI card looks tilted, the DI is toppled. Recovery: you can't fix a toppled DI in flight—the gimbal is mechanically locked. You must land, shut down, restart (allowing the rotor to spin up again), and re-synchronise to the compass. Some DIs have a 'cage' knob (a mechanical lock) that locks the rotor in the vertical position for ground use only; caging the DI on the ground after a topple helps re-align it before the next flight. Flying with a toppled DI is dangerous because you're flying blind on heading—you have only the magnetic compass for reference, and the magnetic compass has turning errors and lag. The lesson is to avoid unusual attitudes that exceed gimbal limits.
exam_tip: Toppled DI: rotor tilted, card shows wrong heading, may appear askew. Compare to compass to verify. Can't fix in flight. Land and reset. Avoid unusual attitudes to prevent topple.
key_facts: Toppled DI: rotor has lost vertical alignment
Heading card: shows incorrect heading, may appear tilted
Erratic reading: card might show multiple headings if tilted
Verification: compare DI to magnetic compass; large difference = topple
Gimbal lock: mechanical stops prevent recovery in flight
Fix: land, restart gyro, synchronise to compass
Cage function: locks rotor vertically for ground recovery
Prevention: avoid unusual attitudes exceeding gimbal limits
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the DI/HI typical: (a) operating limitations; (b) serviceability checks.
concept: Operating limitations of a DI: the DI should only be used for heading reference during normal aircraft attitudes. Extreme pitch or roll (dives, loops, unusual attitudes) can cause gimbal lock and toppling. The DI should not be relied upon for heading reference beyond 15 minutes without synchronisation to the magnetic compass. Vacuum must be maintained at 4–5.5 inHg; low vacuum causes the rotor to slow and drift increases. The DI is not suitable for navigation at extreme latitudes (above 70–75 degrees) where apparent drift becomes significant. Serviceability checks on the ground: the DI heading card should be set to match the magnetic compass heading using the adjustment knob. The card should rotate smoothly when you adjust the knob, and the compass rose should rotate without sticking. Vacuum gauge should read 4–5.5 inHg. After engine start and allowing vacuum to stabilise (a minute or so), the heading card should still be aligned with the compass. In flight: periodically (every 10–15 minutes), check the DI against the magnetic compass and reset if drift is noticeable (more than a few degrees). Monitor vacuum; if it drops below 4.5 inHg, the DI becomes unreliable. If the DI shows sudden heading change or toppling is suspected, compare to compass and visual reference. If disagreement persists, the DI is unserviceable and you must rely on magnetic compass and visual heading reference. A DI that's drifting faster than normal (more than 5 degrees per 10 minutes) indicates gimbal bearing wear and needs overhaul.
exam_tip: Limitations: normal attitudes only, 15-min sync, 4–5.5 inHg vacuum needed. Checks: set to compass pre-flight, monitor drift, reset periodic. Fast drift = wear.
key_facts: Operating limit: normal aircraft attitudes, avoid extremes
Synchronisation: reset to compass every 10–15 minutes
Vacuum requirement: 4–5.5 inHg for stable operation
Low vacuum: <4.5 inHg causes unreliable DI
Latitude limit: ~70° where apparent drift significant
Ground check: set to compass, knob rotates smoothly
In-flight: periodic sync to compass, monitor vacuum
Drift rate: >5°/10 min indicates mechanical wear
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of an electrically-driven DI/HI versus an air-driven instrument.
concept: An air-driven (vacuum-driven) DI depends on the vacuum pump operating reliably. If the pump fails, the rotor slows, and the DI becomes unreliable or fails completely. Also, the vacuum system is complex (pump, regulator, filter, relief valve, gauges) and needs maintenance. An electrically-driven DI uses an electric motor to spin the rotor. It requires electrical power (AC or DC depending on design) but doesn't depend on an engine-driven vacuum pump. If the alternator fails but the battery is good, the electric DI might continue working longer than a vacuum-driven one. Electric drive is also more compact and can be designed with lower power draw. The main advantage is redundancy: if one gyro system fails, you still have the other. On modern glass-panel aircraft, the DI is often replaced by an electronic heading display (part of the attitude/heading reference system, AHRS) that uses heading sensors and no mechanical gyro at all. The disadvantages of electric drive: it depends on electrical power, and some electric DIs draw significant current (reducing available electrical capacity). If both the alternator and battery fail, the electric DI fails too. Vacuum-driven systems are mechanical and don't have electrical dependencies, but they're heavier and bulkier. Most modern aircraft transitioning away from mechanical gyro instruments favour electronic displays that use solid-state sensors (magnetometers, accelerometers) instead, providing both advantages: no mechanical complexity and no vacuum/electrical dependencies for the core heading reference.
exam_tip: Electric DI: independent of vacuum, depends on electrical power, compact. Vacuum DI: independent of power, depends on pump. Modern: electronic AHRS, no mechanical gyro needed.
key_facts: Vacuum-driven: depends on engine-driven pump, complex system
Vacuum advantage: independent of electrical power
Vacuum disadvantage: pump failure = DI failure
Electric-driven: uses electric motor to spin rotor
Electric advantage: independent of vacuum system
Electric disadvantage: depends on electrical power supply
Electronic (AHRS): solid-state sensors, no mechanical gyro
Electronic advantage: small, robust, fast response, no maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of an artificial horizon/attitude indicator (AH/AI).
concept: An artificial horizon (attitude indicator, AH/AI) is a gyroscopic instrument that displays the aircraft's pitch and roll attitude. A gyro rotor spins around a horizontal fore-aft axis (parallel to the fuselage). Due to rigidity in space, the rotor maintains a level orientation regardless of how the aircraft moves. A mechanical linkage transmits the rotor's orientation to an attitude indicator display: a ball or model airplane that tilts with the rotor, showing pitch (nose up or down) and roll (wings tilted). The display is set against a horizon line: the ball's upper hemisphere (sky) is dark or blue, the lower hemisphere (ground) is tan or brown. Pitch is indicated by the position of the model aircraft relative to the horizon line: nose above = climbing, nose below = descending. Roll is indicated by the tilt of the model: wings level = 0 degrees bank, tilted left = left bank, tilted right = right bank. The artificial horizon is vacuum-powered (or electrically-powered in modern variants). A vacuum-powered AH is typically the most reliable instrument because the rotor is spinning continuously and maintains its level orientation without requiring external reference. In instrument flight, the artificial horizon is your primary pitch and roll reference—it tells you immediately whether you're climbing, descending, or banking, which is critical for safe instrument flying. The AH doesn't drift like a directional indicator because level (horizontal) is an absolute reference—gravity defines what's level. Modern electronic displays (glass cockpits) replace the mechanical AH with a digital display fed by an AHRS (attitude heading reference system) using solid-state sensors, but the principle is the same: display aircraft pitch and roll attitude.
exam_tip: AH/AI: horizontal fore-aft gyro maintains level. Display shows pitch and roll relative to horizon line. Primary instrument for attitude in instrument flight. Vacuum or electric powered.
key_facts: Artificial horizon (AH): horizontal fore-aft gyro spins
Rigidity: rotor maintains level orientation in inertial space
Display: attitude ball or model aircraft on horizon line
Pitch: ball position relative to horizon (above = climb, below = descent)
Roll: ball tilt relative to horizon (tilted = banked)
Vacuum-powered: engine-driven pump, continuous operation
