Electrically-powered: motor drive, similar operation
Modern: electronic AHRS provides same indication digitally
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on an air-driven AH/AI, including the errors in indication arising from a: (a) straight-line acceleration; (b) turn.
concept: A straight-line acceleration (climbing while accelerating) can cause errors in the AH display. Inertia from the acceleration creates a force that tilts the gyro rotor off its level axis. In a climb with forward acceleration, the forward inertia pushes the gyro rotor backward, tilting it, and the AH display shows a pitch-up error (shows more climb than actual). The error fades as the acceleration ends and the rotor settles back to level. In level flight with acceleration, the AH might show a slight climb indication momentarily. In a descent with deceleration (pulling back while descending), the deceleration force pushes the rotor forward, creating a pitch-down error (shows more descent). During a turn, the gyro rotor experiences a centrifugal-like effect due to Earth's rotation and the aircraft's circular motion. In a coordinated turn at constant altitude and constant turn rate, the AH should show constant bank angle and level pitch. However, the AH has limitations: if the turn is very steep or the aircraft is uncoordinated (slipping or skidding), the AH can show erratic pitch (false climb or descent indications). In a steep turn with rapid roll-in, the AH might momentarily show a pitch change even though the aircraft isn't actually pitching. These errors are usually small and temporary, settling out as the manoeuvre stabilises. The errors are worst in rapid manoeuvres or extreme attitudes. For normal flying, the AH is accurate enough to rely upon. The error is also why cross-checking with airspeed and altitude instruments is important: if the AH shows climb but altitude isn't increasing and airspeed is stable, something's wrong (possible AH error or unusual aircraft response).
exam_tip: Acceleration: AH shows erratic pitch during accel/decel, settles after. Turns: AH shows constant bank, but steep/uncoordinated turns can cause pitch errors. Cross-check with airspeed and altitude.
key_facts: Acceleration error: forward accel tilts rotor, shows pitch-up
Deceleration error: backward accel tilts rotor, shows pitch-down
Error magnitude: usually small (5–10° or less)
Error duration: temporary, settles as manoeuvre ends
Turn at constant altitude: AH shows correct constant bank
Steep/rapid turns: erratic pitch indication possible
Uncoordinated flight: slip/skid causes pitch errors
Cross-check: airspeed and altitude should match AH indication
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the general operating limitations of an: (a) air-driven AH/AI; (b) electrically-driven AH/AI.
concept: Air-driven (vacuum-driven) AH/AI limitations: requires vacuum at 4–5.5 inHg to operate reliably. If vacuum drops below 4.5 inHg, the rotor spins slower, rigidity decreases, and the instrument becomes unreliable. Complete vacuum loss (pump failure) causes the rotor to stop spinning and the AH to tumble and show incorrect attitude. The vacuum system is engine-dependent: if the engine fails, the vacuum pump stops, and the AH fails. Air-driven AH/AI can display a "cage" or "erect" mechanism (a manual knob) that locks the rotor level for ground use and initial spin-up. Electrically-driven AH/AI limitations: requires electrical power (AC or DC). Loss of electrical power means the rotor stops and the AH fails. Some electric AH/AI instruments draw significant current, reducing electrical capacity available for other systems. Electric drive is sometimes used with a standby battery so the AH continues to work briefly after main power loss, providing time to transition to manual flying or compass navigation. Modern integrated systems (glass cockpits with AHRS) depend on electrical power and correct sensor operation. Both air-driven and electric-driven AH/AI have operating limits on aircraft attitude: they're designed for normal flight (up to roughly ±30 degrees of pitch and roll). Extremely unusual attitudes (inverted, vertical dives) can cause gimbal lock or toppling, and the AH would show incorrect attitude until recovery to normal flight. For both types, periodic alignment or synchronisation is not required (unlike the DI) because level is an absolute reference defined by gravity.
exam_tip: Air-driven: needs vacuum 4–5.5 inHg, engine-dependent, pump failure = failure. Electric: needs power, power loss = failure. Both: normal attitudes only (<±30° pitch/roll), gimbal limits at extremes.
key_facts: Air-driven AH: vacuum-powered, requires 4–5.5 inHg
Low vacuum: rotor slows, AH becomes unreliable
Vacuum loss: rotor stops, AH tumbles and fails
Engine dependence: engine failure = pump failure = AH failure
Electric AH: motor-driven, requires electrical power
Power loss: rotor stops, AH fails immediately
Standby power: some electric AH have battery backup
Attitude limits: designed for normal flight, extreme attitudes cause errors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the indications of the TC and AH/AI under various conditions.
concept: Turn co-ordinator (TC) shows both turn rate and bank angle (via the tilted aircraft symbol). The needle indicates rate of turn (standard rate at the mark). The aircraft symbol shows bank angle (tilted left/right for bank, level for level flight). The balance ball shows coordination. Artificial horizon (AH/AI) shows pitch and roll attitude directly. The horizon line stays fixed; the aircraft symbol pitches up/down and rolls left/right relative to the horizon. No turn-rate indication (the AH doesn't explicitly show how fast you're turning). Under level flight: TC shows level aircraft symbol, centred needle, centred ball. AH shows aircraft level with horizon line, level pitch. Under a turn: TC shows tilted aircraft symbol (banking) and deflected needle (turn rate). Ball shows coordination. AH shows rolled aircraft (tilted wings relative to horizon) and possibly pitched (depending on climb/descent during turn). Under a climb: TC shows the aircraft symbol slightly tilted and a slight needle deflection (if there's any yaw component of the climb). AH shows nose-up pitch, wings level if it's a straight climb. Under a descent: TC similar to climb (needle might show slight deflection if yawed). AH shows nose-down pitch. Under a turn while climbing: TC shows both bank and turn rate, ball coordination. AH shows both roll (wings tilted) and pitch (nose up). Distinguishing: TC is primarily for turn rate and bank; AH is primarily for pitch and roll attitude. In instrument flying, you use both together: TC tells you turn rate and helps coordinate, AH tells you pitch and roll attitude. A classic instrument cross-check uses all three (needle, ball, airspeed) plus AH for a complete picture: TC needle = turn rate, ball = coordination, AH = attitude, airspeed = speed being maintained. Together they define the aircraft's flight path and motion.
exam_tip: TC: turn rate (needle) and bank (symbol). AH: pitch and roll. Use together: TC for rate/coordination, AH for attitude. Both needed for complete instrument flying picture.
key_facts: TC needle: turn rate, standard rate at mark
TC aircraft symbol: bank angle (tilted = banked)
TC ball: coordination (centred = coordinated)
AH: pitch attitude (nose up/down relative to horizon)
AH: roll attitude (wing tilt relative to horizon)
TC no pitch indication, AH no explicit turn-rate indication
Level flight: TC level symbol centred needle, AH level attitude
Climbing turn: TC shows bank and turn, AH shows pitch and roll
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the features of the earth's magnetic field (flux).
concept: The Earth acts like a giant bar magnet with a magnetic field emanating from poles near the geographic poles. The magnetic field lines converge at the magnetic poles. The magnetic north pole is currently about 10 degrees west of geographic north (in the Northern Hemisphere; in the Southern Hemisphere, magnetic south is offset from geographic south). This offset is called magnetic variation, and it changes geographically and with time. The magnetic field strength is measured as flux and is stronger at the magnetic poles and weaker at the equator. In the Southern Hemisphere (where NZ is), the magnetic flux has a vertical component that angles downward toward the magnetic south pole. This is called the 'dip' or 'inclination'. At higher southern latitudes, the dip is steeper. The total magnetic flux can be split into two components: the horizontal component (H), which is what a compass needle responds to and points toward magnetic north, and the vertical component (Z), which pulls the compass needle down. The vertical component is stronger at higher latitudes, which is why compasses in New Zealand have a steeper dip than compasses near the equator. Understanding these components is important for understanding compass errors.
exam_tip: Earth's field: magnetic poles offset from geographic. Flux stronger at poles, weaker at equator. Vertical component (Z) steeper at higher latitudes. Horizontal component (H) points to magnetic north. NZ: southern latitude, steeper dip.
key_facts: Earth's magnetic field emanates from magnetic poles
Magnetic north ≠ geographic north (variation)
Flux strength varies: maximum at poles, minimum at equator
Dip (inclination): angle of vertical component
Horizontal component (H): useful part, points to magnetic north
Vertical component (Z): pulls needle down, stronger at high latitudes
Southern Hemisphere: Z points downward toward magnetic south pole
NZ latitude: significant dip angle (~55 degrees)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain magnetic variation.
concept: Magnetic variation is the angle between magnetic north (where the compass needle points) and true north (where the meridians converge on the globe). It varies geographically: in NZ, magnetic variation is roughly 20 degrees west (varies slightly by location and year). West of the International Date Line, variation is west (compass points west of true north). East of the dateline, variation is east (compass points east of true north). Variation also changes with time—it drifts slowly due to changes in the Earth's magnetic field. Charts are updated annually, and your sectional or aviation chart will show isogonic lines (lines of constant variation) with the current variation and the annual change rate. When you're planning a route and calculating magnetic headings, you account for variation: true heading minus variation (if west) equals magnetic heading. If variation is 20 degrees west, and your true heading is 010 degrees, your magnetic heading is 010 + 20 = 030 degrees (you turn more east because the compass points west of true). In NZ airspace, variation is west, so you're always adding to true headings when converting to magnetic. Variation is the same everywhere along an isogonic line, so you can pick one value for a cross-country flight if the route doesn't cross major variation changes. On a chart, you'll see 'VAR 20°W, increasing 8' per year' or similar—that means variation is 20 degrees west and increases about 8 minutes per year. The increase means variation will become more westerly.
exam_tip: Variation: angle between magnetic north and true north. In NZ: 20°W (varies by location). Use chart isogonic lines. True heading ± variation = magnetic heading. West variation adds to true (True, Variation, Magnetic rule: add West, subtract East).
key_facts: Magnetic variation: difference between true and magnetic north
NZ: variation approximately 20°W (location dependent)
Isogonic lines: constant variation lines on charts
Variation changes with time (drifts geographically)
Charts updated annually with current variation
Annual change rate shown on charts (e.g., '8' per year')
Westerly variation: increases when variation becoming more westerly
Easterly variation: decreases when variation becoming less westerly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the angle of dip and components H and Z of the earth's magnetic flux.
concept: The angle of dip (also called inclination) is the angle between the Earth's magnetic field vector and the horizontal plane. At the equator, dip is 0 degrees (the field runs horizontally). At the magnetic poles, dip is 90 degrees (the field points straight down). In the Southern Hemisphere, the field points downward and southward toward the magnetic south pole; the dip angle increases as you move toward higher southern latitudes. In New Zealand (latitude around 40 degrees south), the dip angle is approximately 55–60 degrees—quite steep. The vertical component (Z) of the Earth's flux is the part of the magnetic field pointing toward the magnetic pole (downward in the Southern Hemisphere). The horizontal component (H) is perpendicular to Z and points toward magnetic north (or south in the Southern Hemisphere, but the compass needle follows H). In NZ, Z is much larger than H because of the steep dip. A compass needle only responds to H (it's suspended to pivot freely in the horizontal plane), so the strong Z component pulls the needle down but doesn't affect its direction. However, the pendulous suspension of aircraft magnetic compasses is designed to accommodate the dip by inclining the magnet system to align with the field's natural angle. This is called 'dip compensation'. Without it, the needle would dip excessively in the vertical plane and you couldn't read the compass accurately.
exam_tip: Dip: angle of magnetic field from horizontal. NZ: ~55–60 degrees (steep). H: horizontal component (useful). Z: vertical component (downward in SH, strong at high latitudes). Compass needs dip compensation to read accurately.
key_facts: Dip angle: angle of magnetic field from horizontal
Dip = 0 degrees at equator, 90 degrees at magnetic poles
NZ dip: approximately 55–60 degrees
Vertical component (Z): points toward magnetic south pole (downward)
Horizontal component (H): points toward magnetic north
In NZ: Z >> H due to steep dip
Compass needle: responds to H only
Dip compensation: pendulous suspension aligns with field angle
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the basic features of a typical aircraft direct-reading magnetic compass.
concept: Your direct-reading magnetic compass is a bowl-shaped compass card mounted below the windscreen, usually on the glare shield. The card is printed with heading marks (0, 90, 180, 270 degrees) and has a magnetic bar magnet or magnets attached. As the aircraft turns, the magnet aligns with the Earth's magnetic field, and the card rotates with it, showing the heading. Inside the bowl is damping fluid (usually a mixture of oils) that slows needle movement and damps oscillations caused by turbulence—the compass responds smoothly instead of swinging wildly. The compass is suspended by a float system that's weightless (or nearly so) in the fluid, reducing mechanical friction. The compass is mounted in a bowl oriented so the magnet is pendulously suspended (tilted to align with the local dip). This dip compensation is crucial: without it, the vertical component of Earth's field would tilt the needle excessively. The compass is adjusted during manufacture or installation to align with the aircraft's magnetic axes, and residual deviation is noted on a deviation card posted nearby. The compass is essentially friction-free and floats in damping fluid, so it's extremely reliable—it doesn't need power and it works as long as the magnet is magnetic and the fluid hasn't leaks. It's the 'old reliable' of aircraft navigation.
exam_tip: Compass: magnets in bowl, floats in damping fluid, card rotates with magnetic field. Pendulous suspension aligns with dip. Damping slows movement. No power needed. Reliable and simple.
key_facts: Magnetic compass: bowl-shaped, floats in damping fluid
Card prints heading marks (0, 90, 180, 270)
Magnets attached to card, align with Earth's magnetic field
Damping fluid: reduces oscillation, smooth response
Float system: near-weightless, reduces friction
Pendulous suspension: aligns with local dip angle
No electrical power required; operates independently
Deviation card: posted adjacent, shows compass error by heading
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason(s) for: (a) pendulous suspension of the magnet system; (b) immersing the magnet system in fluid.
concept: Pendulous suspension tilts the magnet system to align with the local dip angle of Earth's magnetic field. Remember, in NZ the field points downward at 55–60 degrees. Without pendulous tilt, the vertical component (Z) of the field pulls the magnet downward, causing the compass card to tilt or oscillate in pitch. By tilting the magnet system to match the dip, the magnet aligns with the net magnetic field, and the compass card stays level and stable. The fluid immersion (damping fluid) serves several functions. First, it provides buoyancy: the float system displaces almost as much fluid as the magnet system weighs, so there's minimal mechanical friction on the pivot bearing. Second, the viscous damping fluid damps oscillations and turbulence effects: if the aircraft hits an air bump and the compass wants to swing, the fluid resists the motion and brings it back to equilibrium smoothly. Without damping, the compass would swing wildly with every bump, making it useless. Third, the fluid protects the magnet from demagnetisation due to vibration and shock. Fourth, the fluid fills expansion space so there's no air bubble that could cause the compass to tilt as the aircraft manoeuvres. A bubble in the compass would allow the card to shift, introducing errors. The combination of pendulous suspension and fluid immersion makes the magnetic compass robust, reliable, and relatively immune to the aircraft's manoeuvres.
exam_tip: Pendulous: aligns magnet system with dip angle, keeps card level. Fluid: buoyancy reduces friction, damping smooths oscillations, prevents bubble, protects magnet.
key_facts: Pendulous suspension: tilts magnet to match local dip
Dip compensation: ensures card stays level despite Z component
Damping fluid: viscous oil mixture reduces oscillation
Buoyancy: float system near-weightless, minimal pivot friction
Damping effect: smooth response to aircraft movement and turbulence
Fluid-fill: no air bubble, prevents tilting on manoeuvres
Magnet protection: fluid cushions vibration, prevents demagnetisation
Result: stable, reliable compass independent of aircraft motion
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: State the basic structure of a helicopter fuselage.
concept: Your helicopter's fuselage is built around a central frame structure that supports the engine, transmission, and flight control systems. It's typically a welded steel tube framework with external skin (often aluminium) for aerodynamic shape and protection. The fuselage must be strong enough to handle the stresses from rotor forces and flight manoeuvres while being as light as possible. Unlike fixed-wing aircraft, helicopter fuselages don't need to be pressurised, which simplifies the design. The cabin area houses the pilot and passengers, while the engine compartment forward of the cabin contains the power plant. The tail boom extends aft to house the tail rotor. This structure must withstand enormous forces—the main rotor system creates continuous lift that puts the entire fuselage under stress.
exam_tip: Know that the helicopter fuselage is a framework structure, not a monocoque like many fixed-wing aircraft. The tail boom's a distinct structural element. Be able to identify the major sections.
key_facts: Fuselage: welded steel tube framework with aluminium skin
Cabin and engine compartment are main structural elements
Tail boom extends aft to support tail rotor
Must withstand continuous lifting forces from main rotor
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction and purpose of the tail boom.
concept: The tail boom is a slender structural member extending aft from the main fuselage that houses the tail rotor assembly. It's typically made of welded aluminium alloy tubes or composites, designed to be strong yet lightweight. The tail boom serves two critical purposes: first, it positions the tail rotor far from the main fuselage, maximising its effectiveness in counteracting the torque from the main rotor. Second, it provides a strong structural connection for the tail rotor shaft and gearbox. The boom must withstand bending forces from tail rotor thrust, torsional forces from the rotor shaft, and the buffeting of flight. In a Robinson R22, the tail boom is a welded structure that's regularly inspected for cracks. Damage here is serious—a failed tail boom means complete loss of yaw control.
exam_tip: The tail boom's job is to mount and position the tail rotor. It's critical to yaw control. Know what happens if it fails (total loss of yaw control). Understand why it extends so far from the fuselage.
key_facts: Extends aft from fuselage to position tail rotor
Welded aluminium alloy or composite construction
Supports tail rotor shaft, gearbox, and control systems
Failure means complete loss of yaw control
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Describe the purpose and construction of landing skids.
concept: Helicopter landing skids are the tubular framework that extends below the fuselage and contacts the ground during landing. They're typically constructed from steel tubes (often with shock absorption built in) that spread the aircraft weight over a large landing area. Unlike aircraft wheels, skids have no rolling friction—they slide across rough terrain, which is why helicopters can land in unprepared areas like hillsides, beaches, and clearings. The skids are attached to the fuselage via elastic or spring elements that absorb landing shock. In an R22, the skids are connected by a cross-bar that helps maintain proper spacing and provides additional structural rigidity. The design allows the helicopter to land on slopes up to about 25 degrees, which is essential for remote operations in NZ's mountainous terrain.
exam_tip: Skids don't have wheels—they slide. This lets helicopters land anywhere with firm ground. Know the shock absorption system and why skids can handle slopes.
key_facts: Tubular steel construction extending below fuselage
Contact ground directly (no wheels)
Shock absorption system for landing impact
Enable landing on slopes up to ~25 degrees
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: State the purpose and construction of a cockpit canopy.
concept: The cockpit canopy is the transparent covering (typically lexan or similar polycarbonate) that encloses the crew compartment and provides visibility for flight operations. In your helicopter, it's constructed as a rigid frame with multiple transparent panels that can be opened or removed for better visibility, ventilation, or emergency egress. The canopy must be strong enough to withstand wind loads and vibration during flight while remaining transparent for good visibility in all directions. It's not pressurised (helicopters don't need cabin pressure at operating altitudes). The canopy design in many training helicopters allows for complete removal of doors, which is essential for certain operations like external load carrying or camera work. The structure typically uses aluminium frames with polycarbonate panels—durable but requiring regular cleaning and care.
exam_tip: The canopy provides visibility and protection, not pressurisation. Know that it can often be removed. Be aware of visibility limitations—look for blind spots.
key_facts: Transparent polycarbonate/lexan construction
Often removable or hinged for operational flexibility
Provides visibility but not pressurisation
Must withstand wind loads and vibration
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: State the function of the main gearbox.
concept: The main gearbox is the crucial mechanical component that takes the high-speed rotation from the engine (typically 2,500–3,000 RPM) and reduces it to the proper main rotor RPM (typically 400–600 RPM depending on aircraft type). It also distributes power to the tail rotor drive shaft, which drives the tail rotor at a ratio that ensures the tail rotor turns at the right speed for yaw control. The main gearbox is filled with special aircraft-grade oil that both lubricates the gears and provides hydraulic pressure for swashplate operations. In your R22, the main gearbox also integrates the bevel gears that transmit power vertically from the engine up through the mast to the rotor hub. The gearbox is one of the most critical systems—failure here means immediate landing required. The gearing system is carefully engineered for specific helicopter types, so you never mix gearbox types between aircraft.
exam_tip: Main gearbox reduces engine RPM to rotor RPM. It also drives the tail rotor at the correct ratio. The oil in it serves dual purposes: lubrication and hydraulic pressure.
key_facts: Reduces engine RPM (~3000) to rotor RPM (~500)
Integrates main rotor drive and tail rotor drive
Gearbox oil provides lubrication and hydraulic pressure
Failure requires immediate landing
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the main rotor mast and its connection to the fuselage.
concept: The main rotor mast is the vertical shaft extending upward from the main gearbox through the helicopter structure to the rotor head. It's typically a hollow steel tube that transmits the rotational power from the gearbox up to the rotor hub and main rotor blades. The mast must be strong enough to transmit the full power of the engine while remaining flexible enough to withstand rotor vibration and flight loads. At the bottom, it's splined to the gearbox output shaft, allowing it to transmit torque without slipping. At the top, it connects to the swashplate mechanism, which controls blade pitch for all flight control inputs. The mast is supported by low-friction bearing surfaces within the fuselage that allow smooth rotation while maintaining alignment. Any imbalance or damage to the mast causes severe vibration that's immediately noticeable in the aircraft.
exam_tip: The mast transmits power from gearbox to rotor. It's also part of the pitch control system via the swashplate. Know that imbalance here causes distinctive vibration.
key_facts: Vertical hollow steel tube from gearbox to rotor head
Transmits full engine power to rotor
Splined to gearbox, supported by bearings
Part of pitch control system (swashplate connection)
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of the swashplate mechanism.
concept: The swashplate is one of the most clever mechanical systems in a helicopter. It's a complex assembly of plates and bearings that converts the pilot's cyclic stick input into individual blade pitch changes, enabling all helicopter flight control. Here's how it works: the inner swashplate is fixed to the mast and rotates with the rotor. The outer swashplate doesn't rotate but can tilt in any direction and move up and down. When the pilot moves the cyclic stick, control linkages push or pull on the outer swashplate, tilting it. As the rotor blades pass over the tilted swashplate, each blade rides up and down on it, changing its pitch angle. A blade passing over the high point of the tilted swashplate increases its pitch, while a blade passing over the low point decreases pitch. This cyclic pitch change every rotation gives you cyclic control—it's why the helicopter can move forward, backward, or sideways. The vertical movement of the outer swashplate controls collective pitch (all blades together), which controls vertical power and lift.
exam_tip: The swashplate converts stick input to blade pitch changes. Cyclic (tilted swashplate) controls direction. Collective (vertical movement) controls lift. It's complex but essential—understand the basic principle.
key_facts: Converts pilot stick input to blade pitch changes
Cyclic tilt controls forward, back, left, right movement
Vertical movement controls collective pitch and lift
Inner plate rotates with rotor, outer plate doesn't
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the control linkages from the swashplate to the rotor blades.
concept: The control linkages from the swashplate to the rotor blades are the mechanical connections that transmit the swashplate motion to each blade's pitch change mechanism. Each rotor blade has a pitch horn (a mechanical arm) attached to its root. This pitch horn is connected to the swashplate via pushrods and control rods that allow frictionless movement while transmitting force. As the rotor turns and each blade passes through different positions relative to the swashplate, the linkage geometry changes the blade pitch proportionally. The system must maintain exact alignment and consistent friction—any wear or looseness degrades control response. In your R22 or similar helicopter, these linkages are carefully adjusted during maintenance to ensure consistent pitch control throughout the rotor disk. A worn linkage creates lag in control response and can be dangerous.
exam_tip: Control linkages transmit swashplate motion to blades. They must be free-moving but tight (no slop). Wear here degrades control response.
key_facts: Pushrods and control rods connect swashplate to pitch horns
Maintain pitch consistency throughout rotor rotation
Must be precisely adjusted—wear creates control lag
Each blade has its own pitch control linkage
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: State the function of the tail rotor drive shaft.
concept: The tail rotor drive shaft is the mechanical connection from the main gearbox output to the tail rotor gearbox. It typically runs through or along the tail boom (depending on design) and transmits power from the main gearbox at a fixed ratio that ensures the tail rotor turns at the correct speed relative to the main rotor. In most helicopters, the tail rotor turns approximately 4–5 times faster than the main rotor to generate sufficient thrust to counteract main rotor torque. The drive shaft usually consists of a series of aluminium tubes with universal joints or flexible couplings that allow for slight misalignment without binding or excessive vibration. The shaft must be well-balanced and supported by bearings—any imbalance here shows up as tail boom vibration. The tail rotor drive ratio is fixed and specific to each aircraft type; you can't change it without major gearbox modification.
exam_tip: Tail rotor drive shaft transmits main gearbox power to tail rotor. It runs at a fixed ratio (typically 4-5:1). It must be balanced—imbalance shows as tail boom vibration.
key_facts: Connects main gearbox to tail rotor gearbox
Fixed gear ratio (typically 4-5:1 speed increase)
Aluminium tubes with universal joints
Imbalance causes tail boom vibration
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the tail rotor gearbox and its function.
concept: The tail rotor gearbox, located near or at the junction of the tail boom and empennage (tail assembly), does the final speed increase and direction change for the tail rotor. It takes the tail rotor drive shaft input and increases the speed further through a set of bevel gears, directing the power horizontally to drive the tail rotor blades. The tail rotor gearbox is much smaller than the main gearbox but operates at much higher speeds. It's also filled with special aircraft lubricant. This gearbox is subjected to significant aerodynamic loading from the tail rotor thrust, so its bearings and structure must be robust. Failure of the tail rotor gearbox is a serious issue—the tail rotor stops, you lose yaw control, and you must land immediately. The gearbox is inspected regularly for signs of wear, oil leakage, or bearing damage.
exam_tip: Tail rotor gearbox does the final speed increase and redirects power to the horizontal tail rotor. Its failure means immediate loss of yaw control.
key_facts: Increases speed further and redirects power horizontally
Bevel gears provide final speed increase
Operates at high speeds under significant aerodynamic load
Failure results in immediate loss of yaw control
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: State the function of rotor brake and autorotation systems.
concept: The rotor brake is a mechanical friction brake that stops the main rotor spin-down after engine shutdown, reducing the time to full stop from several minutes to under 20 seconds. This is important for operational efficiency and safety—you don't want a spinning rotor disc overhead while you're working on the ground. The brake is typically a friction disc mechanism that's engaged by a switch or is automatically engaged when the engine shuts down. In modern helicopters, rotor speed might be controlled by a governor that automatically adjusts engine throttle to maintain constant rotor RPM, giving the pilot less workload. Autorotation is the helicopter's emergency descent mode—if the engine fails, the rotor blades continue spinning from the upward airflow during descent, allowing a controlled landing from any altitude above minimum. This self-regulating aerodynamic principle is what makes helicopter single-engine-failure survival possible. Most helicopter accidents involve failure to recognise an engine failure quickly enough to enter autorotation.
exam_tip: Rotor brake stops rotor after shutdown. Autorotation is survival mode on engine failure—upward air keeps rotor turning. Know that you must maintain rotor RPM in limits during autorotation.
key_facts: Rotor brake speeds rotor shutdown to ~20 seconds
Friction disc mechanism
Autorotation uses upward air flow to keep rotor turning
Governor systems maintain rotor RPM automatically
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the free-wheel or overrunning clutch in the main rotor system.
concept: The free-wheel clutch (also called an overrunning clutch) is a one-way mechanical coupling between the engine and the main rotor system. Under normal operation with engine power applied, the clutch locks and transmits power from the engine to the main rotor. However, if the engine fails, the clutch automatically disengages, allowing the main rotor to continue spinning from its own inertia without being driven by the failed engine. This is absolutely critical for helicopter safety—when an engine fails, the rotor blades' inertia keeps them turning long enough for autorotation to work, allowing controlled descent to a landing. Without the free-wheel clutch, a failed engine would try to drag the rotor down through the gearbox, and you'd lose lift instantly. The free-wheel is tested during pre-flight checks; if it fails to engage or disengage properly, the aircraft is unairworthy. This is one of the most important safety features in a helicopter.
exam_tip: Free-wheel clutch is essential for engine failure recovery. It allows rotor autorotation when power is lost. Understand that it automatically disengages and re-engages.
key_facts: One-way clutch between engine and main rotor
Locks during normal powered flight
Disengages automatically on engine failure
Allows rotor to continue turning for autorotation
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the concept of autorotation in helicopters.
concept: Autorotation is the helicopter's equivalent of a fixed-wing glide—when the engine fails, the rotor blades continue spinning using the aerodynamic forces of air flowing upward through them. Here's the physics: as the helicopter descends from engine failure, air flows upward through the rotor disc. This upward airflow creates lift on the blades, and critically, creates a force component that drives the rotor around. The lift is greatest near the tips and outer portions of the blade (where airflow velocity is higher), and this creates a net torque that keeps the rotor spinning. By manipulating collective pitch, you can control descent rate and rotor RPM. You descend at about 1,500–2,000 feet per minute (depending on weight and blade design), maintain rotor RPM in the allowable range, and land. Most helicopters can autorotate successfully from any altitude if height and rotor RPM are within limits. This is drilled extensively in training—autorotation from height is a critical emergency procedure that's regularly practised to landing.
exam_tip: Autorotation = rotor driven by upward air flow when engine fails. Descent rate about 1,500 ft/min. Must maintain rotor RPM in limits. Know this is trainable and survivable.
key_facts: Rotor driven by upward air flow after engine failure
Lift on blades creates torque that drives rotation
Descent rate ~1,500 ft/min (depending on weight)
Requires discipline with collective pitch control
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Describe the types of transmission systems used in different helicopter designs.
concept: Helicopter transmission systems vary based on aircraft size and power requirements. Light helicopters like the Robinson R22 use a single main gearbox with integrated tail rotor drive—compact and simple. Medium helicopters like the Airbus H125 might have more complex gearbox arrangements with additional reduction stages. Some larger helicopters use split-shaft systems where the engine connects to the gearbox through a transmission that can disconnect or modulate power transfer. Helicopters with multiple engines require combining the power from both engines into a single gearbox without one overpowering the other—this is done with sophistication to ensure smooth power sharing. NOTAR (no tail rotor) systems replace the tail rotor with a fan-driven system that blows air out the tail boom, eliminating the tail rotor drive shaft entirely. In training helicopters like the R22, your transmission is straightforward: engine to main gearbox with integrated tail rotor drive. More advanced types have redundancy and fail-safe features, but the principles remain the same.
exam_tip: Different helicopters have different transmission types. Light singles use simple integrated gearboxes. Know the basic types and why they differ.
key_facts: Light helicopters: simple integrated gearbox design
Medium/large: more complex with reduction stages
Twin-engine: require power-sharing mechanisms
NOTAR systems eliminate tail rotor drive shaft
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Modern helicopter blade construction and materials
concept: Modern rotor blades aren't what they used to be. You've got an aluminium spar running spanwise through the blade for structural strength, wrapped with composite materials (fibreglass, carbon fibre) for stiffness and lightness. The interior's often a honeycomb core to save weight without losing strength. The leading edge gets abrasion strips (leather or composite) to protect it from sand and weather erosion. All this design means blades that are stronger, lighter, and more fatigue-resistant than the old all-metal designs. R22 and R44 blades are variations on this theme.
exam_tip: Understand why modern materials matter: weight reduction means better performance, lower fuel burn, better hover.
key_facts: Aluminium spar provides spanwise structural strength
Composite materials (fibreglass, carbon fibre) wrap the spar
Honeycomb core reduces weight while maintaining rigidity
Abrasion strips (leather, composite) protect leading edges
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Rigid, semi-rigid, and fully articulated main rotor designs
concept: Helicopter designers use three main rotor approaches. Rigid rotors (rare these days) tie all blades directly to the mast—no flapping freedom, but severe vibrational forces transmitted to the airframe. Semi-rigid (R22, for example) pairs two blades, allows them to flap together, rocks side-to-side on a teetering hinge—lighter, cheaper to build, but prone to mast bumping if you push negative G. Fully articulated (larger helicopters, four-plus blades) gives each blade freedom to flap, lead-lag, and change pitch independently—smoother ride, more stable, but heavier, more complex. You need to know the limitations of whatever machine you're flying.
exam_tip: Know which design your helicopter has and its handling quirks. R22 is semi-rigid—avoid mast bump.
key_facts: Rigid: blades fixed to mast, high structural loads transmitted to fuselage
Semi-rigid: paired blades, teetering hinge, lighter but prone to mast bumping
Fully articulated: individual blade flapping and lead-lag, complex but smoother
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Construction features of main rotor blade attachments
concept: Blade attachment is where all the lifting force concentrates. You've got a root section that bolts or is welded to the hub. Hinges—flapping hinges, lead-lag hinges, feathering hinges—allow controlled movement. The attachment design has to handle immense aerodynamic forces, centrifugal loads (especially at the tip), and bending moments. Material choice is critical—stainless steel hinges, composite-wrapped roots, titanium fasteners. Engineers design these to flex and move with the aerodynamic loads while staying rock-solid. If you see any cracking around a hinge during inspection, it's a grounding item.
exam_tip: Understand why blade attachments are inspected so carefully. They're critical load paths.
key_facts: Root attachment transfers all aerodynamic and centrifugal forces
Flapping, lead-lag, and feathering hinges allow controlled blade movement
Materials chosen for fatigue resistance and strength (titanium, stainless steel)
Inspection of attachment areas critical; cracks are unairworthy
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Purpose of additional main rotor design features
concept: Every design feature on a rotor has a purpose. Some reduce vibration, some improve aerodynamic efficiency, some improve control authority. Features like blade twist (washout), blade sweep, and stabiliser bars all work together to manage the rotor's response to pilot input and environmental loads. Some features like delta-three hinges couple flapping to pitch—when a blade flaps up, it automatically increases pitch slightly, reducing flapping amplitude. Understanding why these features exist helps you understand how your helicopter responds.
exam_tip: Don't just memorise features—understand the 'why'. Questions test your reasoning.
key_facts: Washout (blade twist) distributes lift along span evenly
Blade sweep delays compressibility effects at advancing tip
Delta-three hinge couples flapping to pitch change, reduces amplitude
Stabiliser bar damps rotor flapping, improves control stability
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Description and function of main rotor design features (flapping hinges, pitch links, balancing tabs)
concept: Flapping hinges sit near the root and let blades move up and down as they move through the rotor disc. This reduces structural loads and lets individual blades adapt to gusts. Pitch links connect the swashplate to each blade's root, transmitting collective and cyclic input. Balancing tabs (like trim tabs on aeroplane wings) are small, adjustable surfaces on the blade trailing edge that help trim out vibration. Every component works together—if one's bent or stuck, it affects the whole system's balance and control response.
exam_tip: Know the difference between flapping hinges and pitch links. Questions test this distinction.
key_facts: Flapping hinges allow vertical blade movement, reduce structural loading
Pitch links transmit swashplate motion to blade pitch control
Balancing tabs adjust blade forces to reduce vibration
Worn or bent components cause tracking problems and vibration
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Static and dynamic blade balancing procedures
concept: An unbalanced rotor vibrates like a jackhammer and kills your aircraft—structural failure, control issues, instrument shake, all of it. Static balancing checks if a blade is heavier on one side; you'd hang the rotor and watch which side droops. Dynamic balancing runs the rotor at flight RPM and measures vibration with accelerometers. Modern helicopters get dynamically balanced before delivery and after major repairs. As a pilot, you might notice imbalance (unusually rough rotor) and you'd get maintenance to sort it. You're not doing the balancing, but you need to know what balanced means.
exam_tip: Know the difference: static is weight distribution, dynamic is rotational imbalance. Both matter.
key_facts: Static balance checks weight distribution of each blade
Dynamic balance measured during flight or on balance stand
Imbalance causes vibration, potential structural failure
Modern helicopters use accelerometers and laser tracking for dynamic balancing
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Main rotor mast rigging and alignment relative to vertical axis
concept: The mast isn't perfectly vertical—it's rigged slightly away from vertical to compensate for tail rotor drift and other aerodynamic effects. This angle's called rigging or pre-tilt. Get it wrong and the rotor disc tilts, causing control problems and inefficiency. If you're flying an R22 and notice the cyclic needs to be offset to maintain level flight, the mast alignment might be off and it's a maintenance check. The angle is typically just a couple of degrees, but it makes a difference in how the helicopter responds and where the CG needs to be.
exam_tip: Understand mast rigging affects rotor disc tilt and control feel.
key_facts: Mast rigged away from vertical to compensate for tail rotor drift
Incorrect rigging causes cyclic offset and control inefficiency
Rigging angle typically 2–3 degrees from vertical
Affects rotor disc tilt and natural trim position
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Chordwise and spanwise blade balancing methods
concept: Spanwise balancing ensures each blade section from root to tip has even weight distribution along its length. Chordwise balancing addresses fore-aft weight distribution (leading edge vs trailing edge). Both are critical. If the spanwise balance is off, one half of the rotor disc is heavier, and you get a 1-per-rev vibration that shakes the whole helicopter. Chordwise imbalance causes a different vibration signature. Balancing weights glued to the blade or inboard sections of the blade correct these issues. Modern helicopters use precision methods; older aircraft might need more frequent rebalancing.
exam_tip: Understand both types of imbalance cause vibration, but different kinds.
key_facts: Spanwise imbalance: uneven weight along blade length, 1-per-rev vibration
Chordwise imbalance: uneven fore-aft weight distribution
Balance weights glued to blade correct imbalance
Testing done with rotor at flight RPM using accelerometers
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Blade sweepback at tips and compressibility effects
concept: The advancing blade tip in forward flight approaches transonic speeds. Sweepback (trailing edge of the blade tip curves back) delays the onset of compressibility effects—shock waves forming and causing control problems. Without sweepback, you'd hit buffeting and loss of control at lower forward speeds. Modern helicopter blades, especially on fast machines, have pronounced sweepback. The R44 has modest sweepback; larger machines have more. It's a clever design feature that extends your speed envelope.
exam_tip: Know sweepback is an aerodynamic design fix, not structural. It helps at high speed.
key_facts: Advancing blade tip approaches transonic speed in forward flight
Sweepback delays shock wave formation (compressibility effects)
Extends speed envelope and reduces control problems
More pronounced on high-speed helicopter designs
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Blade washout and spanwise pitch distribution
concept: Washout is built-in blade twist—the pitch angle decreases from root to tip. Think of it this way: near the root, the blade moves slowly and needs a high pitch angle to generate lift. At the tip, the blade moves faster and needs less pitch to produce the same lift. Washout gives you even lift distribution across the span, which reduces vibration and improves control efficiency. If washout is worn out (worn blades sometimes lose it), the outer blade section stalls first and you lose control response. Washout is one reason blade limits exist—old blades get restrictions.
exam_tip: Washout = built-in twist, root pitch high to tip pitch low. Know why it matters.
key_facts: Washout: built-in blade twist from root to tip
Root has higher pitch angle than tip
Provides even lift distribution across blade span
Loss of washout due to blade wear reduces control authority
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Delta-three hinge function and flapping amplitude control
concept: Delta-three hinges couple blade flapping to pitch change. When a blade flaps up, the delta-three geometry automatically pitches the blade slightly. This reduces the flapping amplitude—the blade doesn't oscillate as wildly. It's an elegant passive control system that improves rotor stability without needing active feedback. Some designs use it extensively; others use only pitch links and mechanical damping. The effect is smoother flight and less vibration. If delta-three bearings wear, you notice increased flapping and rotor response becomes sluggish.
exam_tip: Delta-three couples flapping to pitch—it's a passive stability feature.
key_facts: Delta-three hinge couples upward flapping to pitch increase
Reduces blade flapping amplitude, improving stability
Passive control: no active input required
Worn delta-three bearings increase flapping, sluggish control response
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Offset pitch horn and flapping-to-pitch coupling in articulated rotors
concept: The pitch horn (the attachment point where pitch links connect to the blade) is offset from the flapping hinge axis. When a blade flaps, this offset geometry automatically changes the blade pitch. It's similar to delta-three but integrated differently in fully articulated systems. The offset reduces flapping amplitude and couples pitch control to blade motion. This coupling improves stability and control authority. Designers fine-tune the offset angle to get the desired control feel—too much and control gets sensitive, too little and the helicopter feels sluggish.
exam_tip: Offset pitch horn is an alternative to delta-three, used in articulated systems.
key_facts: Pitch horn offset from flapping hinge causes automatic pitch change with flap
Reduces flapping amplitude and improves control feel
Used in fully articulated rotor systems
Tuned offset angle affects control sensitivity and stability
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Rotor trimming controls and tracking procedures
concept: Rotor tracking means all blades pass through the same plane—if they don't, you get vibration and inefficiency. You trim the rotor using adjustable pitch links or trim tabs. Some helicopters have ground trim procedures where you measure blade height with a tape at hover and adjust pitch links to equalise heights. Others use chase helicopters or electronic tracking sensors. In flight, you're watching for vibration and control offset—if the cyclic needs to be seriously offset to stay level, tracking's probably off and you note it for maintenance. Proper tracking saves fuel, reduces wear, and improves control precision.
exam_tip: Tracking is about blade position uniformity. Questions test your understanding of why.
key_facts: All blades must track through same plane during rotation
Pitch link adjustment or trim tabs correct tracking errors
Poor tracking causes vibration and control imbalance
Checked during periodic maintenance with laser trackers or tape measurement
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Rotor stabilisation systems and flapping damping
concept: Different designs damp rotor flapping differently. Bell stabiliser bars sit above or below the main rotor disc at 90 degrees to the blades, with weights on the ends. As the rotor flaps, the bar resists the motion, damping oscillations. Hiller paddles work similarly—small aerofoils that generate forces opposing flapping. Offset flapping hinges and underslung rotors (rotor hub below the mast) use aerodynamics and geometry to reduce flapping naturally. Each approach has trade-offs—stabiliser bars add weight, articulated systems are complex. Your helicopter uses one of these systems, and it's why the rotor feels the way it does.
exam_tip: Know the stabiliser system your helicopter uses. It affects handling characteristics.
key_facts: Bell stabiliser bar damps flapping with weights and aerodynamic forces
Hiller paddles use aerofoil surfaces to oppose flapping
Offset hinges and underslung rotors reduce flapping through geometry
Each system affects helicopter handling feel and smoothness
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Ground resonance phenomenon in articulated rotor systems
concept: Ground resonance is a nasty phenomenon that happens with articulated rotors when you're on the ground. The blades go out of phase in their lead-lag motion (one blade leads forward, the adjacent one lags behind), and this imbalance rocks the fuselage. The fuselage bounces, which changes blade pitch and load, which pushes the resonance harder. The helicopter can literally bounce itself to pieces on the ground. It happens without warning and accelerates violently. The fix is immediate: either get the wheels off the ground (fly away) or shut down immediately. You never get caught between those options—if the rotor starts acting weird on the ground, power up and get airborne or cut power and secure the aircraft. Prevention: good tracking, balanced blades, smooth rotor engagement.
exam_tip: Ground resonance is catastrophic. Know the answer: fly away or land immediately.
key_facts: Out-of-phase blade lead-lag motion causes fuselage resonance on ground
Fuselage bouncing couples back into blade motion, self-amplifying
Can result in structural failure if not arrested immediately
Recovery: immediate lift-off or immediate shutdown, no in-between
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Mast bumping in semi-rigid rotor systems
concept: Mast bumping is an even worse scenario and it's unique to semi-rigid rotors like the R22. The rotor teeter hinge has limits. If you push the nose down hard (forward cyclic) while in a descent and negative G loads develop, the rotor disc cones up and the blade roots actually contact the mast. The moment they touch, there's catastrophic structural damage—the mast cracks, the blades can fail, and you're uncontrollable. It happens in seconds. The prevention is simple: never push forward cyclic aggressively, never allow negative G (pull the stick when you need forward motion, don't push), and avoid diving the aircraft. If you're in a descent and need to recover, pull aft cyclic gently. Mast bumping is the reason R22 pilots respect their aircraft and fly with discipline.
exam_tip: Mast bump happens with abrupt forward cyclic + negative G. Prevention: maintain positive G.
key_facts: Semi-rigid rotor teeter hinge has physical limits
Forward cyclic + negative G causes rotor cone to contact mast
Mast contact causes instant structural failure
Prevention: smooth control inputs, maintain positive G loads always
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Vibration types and frequency analysis in helicopter operations
concept: Vibration comes in three flavours. Low-frequency vibration (1-per-rev, one cycle per rotor revolution) comes from rotor tracking errors or blade imbalance. Medium-frequency vibration comes from component issues—a worn bearing in a blade pitch control or a crack starting in the rotor head. High-frequency vibration is usually the tail rotor out of balance, engine roughness, or transmission problems. You feel each type differently: low freq is a rhythmic shudder, medium is a buzz, high freq is a shake. Hearing the difference tells you where to look. If new vibration appears in flight, get the rotor RPM stable and note the frequency—it helps maintenance diagnose the problem.
exam_tip: Know the three vibration types and their sources. You might be asked to diagnose.
key_facts: Low-frequency (1-per-rev): rotor tracking, blade imbalance
Medium-frequency: component wear, internal cracks, bearing issues
High-frequency: tail rotor imbalance, engine roughness, transmission
Vibration frequency helps technicians diagnose the root cause
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Underslung rotor design and Coriolis effect reduction
concept: Some semi-rigid designs position the rotor hub below the mast instead of at the top—underslung. This geometry reduces the Coriolis effect (the apparent force that changes blade motion as they rotate). The result: less flapping and a smoother rotor response. It also reduces mast-bump tendency by changing the natural frequency of blade motion. Underslung rotors are more complex to build and maintain, but they give smoother handling. Some experimental high-speed helicopters use underslung designs to extend their speed envelope. The trade-off is weight and complexity; that's why most small machines like the R22 use more traditional designs.
exam_tip: Underslung rotor reduces Coriolis and flapping—it's an advanced design feature.
key_facts: Underslung rotor hub sits below mast
Reduces Coriolis effect, decreasing blade flapping motion
Improves rotor smoothness and control feel
Reduces mast-bump tendency in semi-rigid systems
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Tail rotor construction and design characteristics
concept: Tail rotors are mini rotors, typically two or four blades made from aluminium or composite, spinning at much higher RPM than the main rotor. The blades are smaller diameter and more highly twisted (washout) than main rotor blades because they operate at much higher tip speeds. The tail rotor generates thrust perpendicular to its plane of rotation to counter main rotor torque and provide yaw control. It's a brilliant simple solution: use the engine's excess torque to drive a small rotor that controls heading. Without it, you'd spin uncontrollably.
exam_tip: Remember: tail rotor counters torque and provides yaw control.
key_facts: Typically two or four blades, aluminium or composite
Smaller diameter, higher RPM than main rotor
Greater blade twist (washout) due to high tip speeds
Generates anti-torque thrust and yaw control authority
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Tail rotor strike tab and ground clearance protection
concept: The tail rotor's close to the ground, especially during low-hover manoeuvres. A strike tab is a small, expendable section on the blade tip—it's designed to hit the ground first if you're too low. The tab absorbs the impact, and you might bend or crack the tab, but the main blade structure survives. Without it, you'd break a blade on the ground, which could fail suddenly in flight. The strike tab is a small cost for a potentially catastrophic failure avoidance. After any tail rotor strike, the tab and potentially the blade need inspection and replacement. It's a clear signal to the pilot: you were too low.
exam_tip: Strike tab protects blade tip. A struck tab needs inspection and likely replacement.
key_facts: Strike tab is expendable blade section designed for ground contact
Absorbs impact energy, protecting main blade
Requires inspection and replacement after any strike
Indicates pilot error: improper altitude awareness during low hover
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Exposed tail rotor design and operational characteristics
concept: Most small helicopters like the R22 and R44 use exposed tail rotors—the blades spin open to air with no shroud. It's simple, effective, and provides full aerodynamic efficiency. The trade-offs: it's a serious hazard to personnel (a spinning rotor will slice you up), it's loud (no shroud to muffle noise), and it's vulnerable to collision damage if you get near obstacles. You're constantly aware of the tail rotor arc—especially around the aerodrome, you clear the area behind the helicopter and ensure no one approaches from behind until the rotor stops.
exam_tip: Exposed tail rotors are dangerous. Know the safety procedures.
key_facts: Simple and lightweight design
Full aerodynamic efficiency, no shroud losses
Serious personnel hazard if spinning
Higher noise signature compared to shrouded designs
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Fenestron shrouded tail rotor design and characteristics
concept: Fenestron is a shrouded multi-blade tail rotor—think of a mini propeller fan inside a duct. It looks like an old air-raid siren on the boom. The advantages: safer (no exposed spinning blades), quieter (the duct suppresses noise), and less vulnerable to damage. The disadvantages: heavier (extra structure), more complex (more blades to balance and maintain), and slightly less efficient (the duct creates friction losses). Large utility helicopters sometimes use Fenestron for safety around cargo loading areas. Small aircraft rarely do because the weight and complexity aren't worth the benefits at small scales.
exam_tip: Fenestron is the shrouded alternative, used where safety or noise is critical.
key_facts: Multi-blade fan inside shroud (duct)
Safer for personnel, quieter operation
More complex, heavier than exposed rotor
Used on utility and passenger helicopters in high-traffic areas
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Asymmetric tail rotor blade aerofoils and efficiency
concept: Tail rotor blades use asymmetric aerofoils (non-symmetrical cross-sections)—one surface is curved, the other flat or less curved. This design generates thrust more efficiently in one direction. The pitch control inputs change the blade angle to generate thrust forward or aft (in the rotor plane). Without asymmetric aerofoils, you'd need more blade area and higher RPM to get the same anti-torque force. The asymmetry also means there's a preferred direction; you get more efficient yaw authority in one direction. The R22's tail rotor is an excellent example of this efficiency.
exam_tip: Asymmetric aerofoils = efficient yaw control. Remember the concept.
key_facts: Asymmetric (non-symmetrical) blade cross-section
One surface curved, other flat or less curved
Generates thrust more efficiently in primary direction
Pitch control varies blade angle for forward and aft yaw thrust
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Alternative anti-torque systems: NOTAR and ducted fan designs
concept: The standard tail rotor isn't the only way to fight torque. NOTAR (No Tail Rotor) uses a jet of pressurised air blown along the boom from an intake at the rotor head. The air exits through slots along the boom and uses the Coanda effect (air clings to a curved surface) to push against the boom. Ducted fans are essentially shrouded rotors. These alternatives eliminate the tail rotor hazard and reduce noise, but they're heavier, more complex, and less efficient. They're used on large military and specialist helicopters where the benefits justify the costs. For general aviation, the exposed tail rotor remains the standard—it's simple, proven, and effective.
exam_tip: Know NOTAR exists and how it works, but understand it's not for small helicopters.
key_facts: NOTAR: pressurised air jet along boom using Coanda effect
Eliminates tail rotor personnel hazard
Heavier and more complex than tail rotor
Used on larger helicopters where benefits justify added cost
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Effect of tail rotor jammed or complete failure on helicopter control and recovery
concept: If your tail rotor jams or fails completely, you lose yaw control instantly. The main rotor torque is no longer balanced, and the fuselage wants to spin opposite the rotor direction. You can't stop it with pedal input. Your only option: enter autorotation. Get the nose down, increase forward speed if you can, and descend to a landing. Some helicopters might have enough cyclic authority to slip, and some forward speed helps stabilise the yaw, but you're committed to landing immediately. You declare emergency, brief passengers (if any), and execute a controlled landing on the nearest suitable surface. Tail rotor failure is rare but catastrophic if it happens in hover—you've got no options but to get down hard.
exam_tip: Tail rotor failure = immediate autorotation and landing.
key_facts: Complete tail rotor failure = loss of yaw control
Main rotor torque causes fuselage to spin
Recovery: immediate autorotation and controlled landing
Speed and descent help manage the spin tendency
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight

syllabus_desc: Collective pitch control mechanism and operation
concept: The collective is your main control for lift. Pull it up (towards you), and all rotor blades increase pitch equally, generating more lift and rising the helicopter. Push it down, and you descend. It's a vertical control. The collective's connected to a throttle cam linkage that automatically increases engine RPM as you pull collective and decreases RPM as you push it—this keeps rotor RPM constant as you change pitch. Without the automatic throttle correlation, you'd have to manually manage throttle while adjusting collective. Modern helicopters do this coupling automatically, and you focus on flying. The R44 has excellent collective feel because the mechanism is smooth and direct.
exam_tip: Collective = lift control. Understand the throttle linkage connection.
key_facts: Collective increases pitch on all blades simultaneously
Pull up for lift increase, push down for descent
Throttle cam automatically correlates RPM to collective position
Critical control for vertical authority and hovering precision
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Throttle cam linkage and governor function
concept: The throttle cam is a mechanical linkage (or electronic governor in modern machines) that couples collective and throttle automatically. You pull collective, the cam pulls the throttle proportionally, maintaining rotor RPM constant. Release it, throttle backs off. It's genius simplicity: you've got one hand free for cyclic and the other for collective; the engine keeps pace automatically. The governor (if installed) takes it further—it actively adjusts engine power to maintain exact RPM within a narrow band, even in wind or gusts. Some helicopters have manual throttle control where you manage throttle independently—that requires more discipline and flying skill.
exam_tip: Governor maintains constant RPM automatically. Manual throttle requires active management.
key_facts: Cam linkage mechanically couples collective and throttle
Governor (electronic or hydromechanical) actively maintains RPM
Constant RPM improves rotor efficiency and control stability
Manual throttle requires pilot attention and skill
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Twist grip throttle fine control and idle management
concept: Even with a governor, you've got a twist grip throttle on the cyclic (or sometimes a separate lever) for fine RPM adjustment. It lets you trim the engine RPM slightly—useful for idle management at reduced power, managing engine temperature, or fine-tuning performance in high-altitude or hot conditions. You rotate it to increase RPM in the idle range or reduce it to trim power. It's a subtle control that requires finesse—coarse inputs on the twist grip cause power swings. The R22 and R44 both use twist-grip throttles, and learning smooth throttle feel is part of developing as a helicopter pilot.
exam_tip: Twist throttle is fine control, used for idle and RPM trim.
key_facts: Fine RPM adjustment control independent of collective
Manages idle power and engine temperature
Requires smooth, progressive inputs to avoid power surges
Common on R22 and R44 helicopters
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Cyclic control and rotor disc tilting for direction control
concept: The cyclic tilts the rotor disc, and the disc tilts, the fuselage follows. Push forward, the disc tilts forward, helicopter flies forward. Pull back, flies backward. Left and right tilting inputs do the same laterally. The cyclic directly controls aircraft attitude and direction. It's intuitive once you get the hang of it. The beauty is the rotor's constantly changing blade pitch as it rotates (collective fixed, but cyclic modulates pitch cyclically), so you get smooth control without physically tilting the blades themselves. The helicopter follows the rotor disc tilt—that's the whole principle. Cyclic control is the heart of helicopter flying; it demands constant small inputs and sensitivity.
exam_tip: Cyclic tilts the rotor disc; helicopter follows disc tilt.
key_facts: Forward cyclic: rotor disc tilts forward, helicopter flies forward
Aft cyclic: rotor disc tilts back, helicopter flies back
Left and right cyclic: lateral disc tilt for lateral flight
Constant small inputs required for precise control
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: Tail rotor pedals and yaw control authority
concept: The tail rotor pedals (foot controls) change tail rotor blade pitch. Push right pedal, tail rotor pitch increases, pushes harder against the boom, yawing the helicopter right. Push left pedal, tail rotor reduces pitch or reverses, yaws left. The pedals are your yaw control, managing torque compensation. In hover, you're constantly making small pedal inputs to hold heading. In forward flight, the fuselage naturally tends to stay aligned (aerodynamic stability), and pedal inputs are more subtle. Pedal authority is limited—if you need extreme yaw rates, speed and cyclic inputs help. The pedals feel light and require delicate inputs, especially in calm conditions.
exam_tip: Pedals control tail rotor pitch, managing yaw. Understand torque compensation.
key_facts: Tail rotor pedals change tail rotor blade pitch
Right pedal increases tail rotor thrust, yaws right
Left pedal decreases tail rotor thrust, yaws left
Constant inputs required in hover; lighter inputs in forward flight
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, FAA Helicopter Handbook

syllabus_desc: State Pascal's principle.
concept: Pascal's principle is elegant: pressure applied to a fluid in a confined space is transmitted equally in all directions. Squeeze a closed container of hydraulic fluid anywhere, and the pressure increase appears everywhere else. This is the entire foundation of hydraulic systems. You apply force to a small piston, pressure multiplies and transmits through the fluid, and comes out the other end to move whatever actuator you need—landing gear, flap jacks, trim systems. No compressibility in liquid means direct, responsive control.
exam_tip: Pressure in confined fluid = same everywhere. Simple statement, but it's the principle that makes hydraulics work. Know how this enables mechanical advantage.
key_facts: Pressure applied = pressure transmitted equally
In all directions through fluid
No loss or reduction in pressure
Foundation of hydraulic systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe mechanical advantage, and how it can be gained hydraulically.
concept: Mechanical advantage means using a small force to move something heavy. Hydraulically, you achieve it with different-sized pistons. Say you apply force to a small input piston and it connects to a large output piston—the pressure's the same everywhere (Pascal), but force equals pressure times area. Small area input, large area output, and you've multiplied your force. Practical example: your landing gear extension might need thousands of pounds of force, but you get it from a small hydraulic pump that you can power with your engine. The pump doesn't need to be huge; it just needs to overcome pressure, which is modest. The area difference in cylinders does the heavy lifting.
exam_tip: Mechanical advantage = small force creates large force. Hydraulically: small input piston, large output piston. Know the formula: Force = Pressure × Area.
key_facts: Pressure same everywhere (Pascal)
Force = Pressure × Area
Small input piston, large output = force multiplication
Hydraulic pump doesn't need high force, just modest pressure
Different piston areas create advantage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of aircraft hydraulic services.
concept: Your aircraft's hydraulic system starts with a pump driven by engine power, usually via a gearbox or belt. The pump forces hydraulic fluid through filter and pressure relief valve (prevents excessive pressure) into a distribution manifold. From there, fluid goes to various actuators—landing gear cylinders, flap jacks, trim units, brake systems. Each actuator is a cylinder with a piston; the fluid pressure moves the piston, doing work. Used fluid returns to a reservoir for reuse. Redundancy's built in: two independent systems, separate pumps, separate fluid supplies. If one system fails, the other keeps you flying. The entire system relies on incompressible fluid and sealed lines; any leak loses system pressure and function.
exam_tip: Pump → filter → relief valve → actuators → return reservoir. Redundancy is critical—know why. Be ready to explain what happens if you lose a hydraulic system.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump provides pressure
Fluid flows to multiple actuators simultaneously
Pressure relief prevents overpressure
Dual systems for redundancy
Return to reservoir for reuse
Any leak = pressure loss = system failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) mechanical advantage; (b) how mechanical advantage can be gained hydraulically; (c) the principle of operation of typical aircraft hydraulic services.
concept: Mechanical advantage is the multiplying effect of force. A lever is mechanical advantage—push down on a long arm and lift a heavy load on the short arm. Hydraulically, you gain mechanical advantage through area ratios. If you've got a small pump pushing fluid through a small line into a large actuator cylinder, the pressure's the same throughout, but the force exerted is proportional to area. A pump with a 1-square-inch piston and a large actuator with a 4-square-inch piston gives you a 4:1 mechanical advantage—the pilot's light force input gets amplified into a powerful actuator output. That's why aircraft use hydraulics: to convert pilot input (light forces, small movements) into powerful control surface movement or landing gear extension. A typical system has a pump (driven by the engine or an electric motor), a pressure relief valve (to prevent over-pressure), accumulators (to smooth pressure spikes and provide emergency power), check valves (to prevent backflow), selector valves (to direct flow), and actuators (hydraulic cylinders that do the work: raise landing gear, lower flaps, move flight controls). The fluid is incompressible, so movement is immediate and precise—no slop.
exam_tip: Hydraulic advantage = pressure × area. Small pump input into large actuator = big force output. Systems include pump, relief, accumulators, check valves, selectors, and actuators. No compressibility = precise, immediate movement.
key_facts: Mechanical advantage: force multiplied by area ratio
Hydraulic pump: converts engine/motor power to fluid pressure
Pressure is constant throughout a closed system
Force output = pressure × actuator area
Pump pressure typically 1000–3000 psi in aircraft
Actuators convert hydraulic pressure into linear or rotary motion
Fluid is incompressible; movement is immediate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of using hydraulics to operate aircraft services.
concept: Hydraulics are the power distribution system of choice for modern aircraft. Why? First, high power density: you can transmit enormous forces through small tubing without heavy mechanical linkages. Deploying landing gear or lowering full flaps on a twin requires huge forces; hydraulics do it smoothly and efficiently. Second, precision: because fluid is incompressible, movement is repeatable and exact. A hydraulic landing gear extension is always the same; there's no slop or inconsistency. Third, safety: hydraulics can be routed through fireproof bulkheads and isolated with shutoff valves. A mechanical cable goes through the firewall and can corrode or fail; a sealed hydraulic line with isolation capability is safer. Fourth, light weight and space efficiency: compare the plumbing needed for mechanical flight controls (cables, pulleys, levers everywhere) versus a single hydraulic line and a small actuator. Modern aircraft are packed with systems; hydraulics let you fit them efficiently. Fifth, redundancy: you can run multiple hydraulic systems (left and right independent systems on a large twin) so single failure doesn't completely disable the aircraft.
exam_tip: Hydraulics offer: high power, precision, safety, light weight, efficiency, and redundancy. That's why they're universal on aircraft with complex landing gear and surface control systems.
key_facts: Hydraulics provide high force in compact size
Incompressible fluid = precise, repeatable movement
Sealed lines = safer than exposed cables or linkages
Can route through fireproof bulkheads
Light and space-efficient compared to mechanical systems
Allows multiple independent systems for redundancy
Isolatable with shutoff valves for safety
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between the three types of hydraulic oil.
concept: There are three main types of hydraulic fluid used in aviation: mineral oil-based fluids (traditional, compatible with most seals), phosphate ester fluids (resistant to fire, used where fire risk is high), and synthetic fluids (improved performance, temperature stability, longer life).
