Mineral oil is the standard in most general aviation aircraft because it's inexpensive, widely available, and works well with standard seals and components. Phosphate ester is expensive and used mainly in military, transport, and high-risk applications where a hydraulic fire could be catastrophic. Synthetics offer advantages in extreme temperature environments or where long fluid life is valued. Never mix hydraulic fluid types: they have different chemical properties and mixing can cause seal degradation and system failure. Your POH will specify exactly which fluid type your aircraft uses. When servicing hydraulics, use fresh fluid from a sealed container; never reuse or top up without inspection. Hydraulic fluid degrades with contamination and heat, so periodic fluid sampling (on larger aircraft) is standard practice.
exam_tip: Know the three types: mineral (standard, most GA), phosphate ester (fire-resistant, military/transport), synthetic (high-performance). Never mix types. Always use the exact type specified in your POH.
key_facts: Mineral oil-based: standard for GA, inexpensive, compatible with seals
Phosphate ester: fire-resistant, expensive, military/transport use
Synthetic: improved temperature stability, longer life, high-performance
Never mix hydraulic fluid types; causes seal and system damage
Use only fresh fluid from sealed containers
Hydraulic fluid degrades with contamination and heat
Follow POH specification exactly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (a) reservoirs; (b) pumps; (c) pressure regulators; (d) accumulators; (e) check valves and release valves; (f) selector valves; (g) actuators; (h) filters.
concept: The reservoir holds the bulk of the hydraulic fluid, provides a place for air bubbles to escape and contaminants to settle, and dissipates heat. Pumps are engine-driven or electric-driven and convert mechanical power into fluid pressure. Pressure regulators (relief valves) prevent over-pressure by opening if system pressure exceeds a set point (usually 2000–3000 psi), dumping excess flow back to the reservoir. Accumulators are like small pressure vessels that store pressurised fluid—they smooth out pressure spikes, provide emergency power if the pump fails, and absorb shock. Check valves allow flow in one direction only, preventing backflow. Selector (directional control) valves route hydraulic flow to different actuators depending on the pilot's input—left, right, up, down, etc. Release valves (not to be confused with relief valves) are manually operated and allow emergency decompression or manual lowering of systems if hydraulic power is lost. Actuators are the working end: hydraulic cylinders that extend and retract (landing gear, flaps, trim) or rotary actuators that rotate (flight controls). Filters catch contaminants to protect the system and maintain fluid cleanliness.
exam_tip: Each component has a job: reservoir holds fluid, pump pressurises it, regulator prevents over-pressure, accumulator provides smoothing and backup, check valves prevent backflow, selectors route flow, actuators do the work, filters keep it clean.
key_facts: Reservoir: stores fluid, allows air escape, dissipates heat
Pump: converts mechanical power to hydraulic pressure
Pressure regulator (relief): opens if pressure exceeds set point
Accumulator: stores pressure, smooths spikes, provides emergency power
Check valve: allows one-way flow, prevents backflow
Selector valve: directs flow to desired actuator
Release valve: allows manual decompression or emergency lowering
Actuator: hydraulic cylinder or rotary motor that performs work
Filter: removes contaminants, maintains fluid cleanliness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical fire detection system.
concept: Fire detection systems use sensors (usually thermocouples or continuous-loop detectors) strategically placed in engine bays, cargo holds, or other high-risk areas. The most common in piston aircraft are continuous-loop systems: a Inconel or stainless-steel tube runs through the engine bay, and inside the tube is a eutectic wire (a specially formulated alloy) that melts at a fixed temperature—say, 180°C. When that temperature is reached, the wire melts, breaking the electrical circuit and triggering the fire warning on the flight deck. A thermocouple system uses multiple small thermocouples wired in series; if any thermocouple sees a rapid temperature rise (the rate-of-rise detector) or an absolute temperature above a threshold, it triggers the warning. Modern systems use both: absolute temperature (190°C) and rate-of-rise (15°C per second) so you catch a fire quickly. The warning activates a light (usually red, with 'FIRE' label) and possibly a bell or buzzer. It's not foolproof—false alarms do happen from electrical faults—but it's your first and often best warning that you've got a fire. Once the warning sounds, you isolate the affected engine and execute the fire-out procedure (cutting fuel, electrical power, closing firewall shutoff valves, deploying fire bottles if equipped).
exam_tip: Fire sensors: thermocouples or continuous-loop detectors in engine bay. Trigger on absolute temperature or rapid rate-of-rise. Warning light on flight deck. Know where your fire warning sensors are and what temperature triggers them.
key_facts: Continuous-loop systems: eutectic wire melts at set temperature
Thermocouple systems: detect absolute temperature or rate-of-rise
Sensors placed in engine bay, cargo hold, or other high-risk areas
Temperature threshold typically 180–200°C
Rate-of-rise detector: 10–15°C per second triggers warning
Warning activates red light and possibly audible alarm
False alarms can occur from electrical faults
Pilot action on warning: isolate engine, cut fuel, execute fire-out procedure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Continuous loop fire detection system and cockpit warning
concept: Fire detection in helicopters uses a continuous loop system—a thermocouple wire runs around the engine bay. If temperature rises abnormally (fire), the loop detects the heat spike and sends a signal to a warning light in the cockpit. The system has a test circuit: during preflight, you activate the test and the warning light should illuminate, proving the loop's working. If you detect fire in flight (warning light + smell or visual confirmation), you declare emergency, head for the nearest suitable landing area, reduce power if safe, and land immediately. Post-landing, you exit the aircraft and move to a safe distance. Fire is the only emergency that sometimes demands immediate landing without regard to location—it's that critical.
exam_tip: Fire warning = immediate landing. Know the preflight test procedure.
key_facts: Continuous thermocouple loop around engine bay detects heat
Warning light in cockpit indicates temperature rise
Test circuit confirms loop integrity during preflight
Fire indication requires immediate landing and evacuation
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Explain any precautions to be taken when using the common fire extinguishing agents available.
concept: Common aircraft fire extinguishing agents are CO2 (carbon dioxide), Halon 1211, Halon 1301, and dry powder (mostly on ground equipment). CO2 is effective but heavier than air, so it can stratify and leave pockets of oxygen-rich air. It's also cold, which can shock a hot engine. Halon 1211 and 1301 are halogenated hydrocarbons that interrupt the chemical reaction of combustion—they're extremely effective and leave no residue. Halon is the preferred agent on modern aircraft because of its efficiency. However, Halon production is being phased out worldwide due to ozone depletion concerns, so supplies are dwindling and very expensive. Dry powder is used in ground-based systems (hangars, maintenance areas) because it's messy but cheap and effective. Precautions when using any extinguishing agent: ensure good ventilation because CO2 displaces oxygen (asphyxiation risk in confined spaces), wear protective equipment (gloves, eye protection), and follow manufacturer procedures exactly. Don't mix agents. If you deploy a fire bottle in flight, expect reduced visibility (Halon dissipates, but can cloud the cabin briefly), and be aware that some agents cool the engine significantly and can affect handling. After deploying any agent, plan an immediate landing—the fire might be suppressed but you want inspection and maintenance before flying again.
exam_tip: CO2: effective but cold, heavy, asphyxiation risk. Halon: effective, leaves no residue, expensive, phasing out. Dry powder: messy, ground-based. Precautions: ventilate, PPE, follow procedures, plan landing after deployment.
key_facts: CO2: effective, but heavy and cold, asphyxiation risk in confined spaces
Halon 1211/1301: very effective, no residue, preferred for aircraft
Dry powder: messy, ground-based, used in hangars and shops
Never mix fire extinguishing agents
Ventilation essential when using CO2
Personal protective equipment required (gloves, eye protection)
Expect visibility degradation with airborne agents
Plan immediate landing after in-flight agent deployment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe typical fire extinguishing systems.
concept: A typical in-flight fire extinguishing system on a light twin consists of one or more fire bottles (pressurised cylinders of Halon or CO2) mounted in an accessible but protected location, usually the fuselage or an engine bay bulkhead. The bottle is connected via a distribution line (usually a pipe or rigid tube) to the suspected fire location. A pushbutton on the flight deck activates the system: you press the button, a solenoid valve opens, and pressurised extinguishing agent flows down the pipe to disperse in the engine compartment. Some systems have multiple bottles with individual switches (one for each engine) so you can suppress fires on either engine independently. Manual override systems exist on some aircraft: if the electrical system fails, you can pull a mechanical lever or cable to open the valve manually. Ground-based systems in hangars are similar: a pull handle or push button releases the agent over a wide area. All fire extinguishing systems have inspection and maintenance requirements: hydrostatic testing of the bottle (every few years), checking the pressure gauge to ensure the bottle is charged, and verifying all valves and lines are clear. A discharged fire bottle is useless, so this is a critical pre-flight item. Once a bottle is deployed, it must be professionally refilled before the aircraft flies again.
exam_tip: Fire bottle: pressurised, solenoid-activated, distribution line to engine bay, pushbutton activation. Know where your bottle is, what agent it contains, and its pressure status. Discharged bottle = no-go.
key_facts: Fire bottle: pressurised cylinder of Halon or CO2, mounted in fuselage
Solenoid valve: electrically activated, releases agent on pushbutton
Distribution line: pipe routing agent to fire location
Multi-engine aircraft: separate bottle or selector for each engine
Manual override: mechanical lever or cable backup if electrical fails
Inspection: hydrostatic testing every 5 years, pressure check before flight
Discharged bottle: unserviceable, must be refilled by certified facility
Post-deployment: plan immediate landing, ground aircraft for inspection
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the associated limitations of typical fire extinguishing systems.
concept: Fire extinguishing systems are good, but they have real limitations. First, they only suppress the fire where the agent is directed; if the fire's in an area the distribution line doesn't reach, the agent won't help. Second, they work best on fuel fires or electrical fires; structural fires (where the airframe itself is burning) are harder to suppress and may reignite after the agent dissipates. Third, the agent cools the engine rapidly, and that thermal shock can damage components or cause subsequent failures. Fourth, once the agent is deployed, it's gone—you get one shot. If it doesn't work, you've got no backup. Some aircraft have multiple bottles to address this, but it costs weight and space. Fifth, the agent can obscure vision (CO2 fog, Halon vapour) making it harder to see instruments or navigate. Sixth, deployment doesn't address the root cause—if you've got an electrical short that's causing the fire, the agent suppresses it, but the short's still there waiting to reignite. That's why immediate landing and professional investigation are mandatory after any fire suppression. Finally, false alarms do happen from faulty detectors or electrical faults, and deploying the bottle unnecessarily uses up your one-shot protection.
exam_tip: Fire suppression limitations: limited coverage area, doesn't address root cause, one-shot only, thermal shock risk, vision degradation, possible reignition. Always land immediately after any fire warning, even if suppression deployed.
key_facts: Agent reaches only the area covered by distribution lines
Structural fires difficult to suppress and prone to reignition
Rapid cooling (thermal shock) risks engine damage
Single-shot deployment; no backup if first attempt fails
Halon/CO2 fog reduces visibility
Suppression treats symptom, not root cause
Electrical faults that cause fire still exist after suppression
False alarms from detector faults waste agent protection
Immediate landing and professional inspection mandatory after fire warning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Weight and balance terminology and centre of gravity definitions
concept: Weight and balance is essential knowledge. The datum is a reference line (usually at the rotor mast or a point forward of the aircraft), and we measure distances from it. The moment arm is the distance from the datum to a weight. Moment is weight times moment arm (tells you the effect of that weight on balance). Centre of gravity (CG) is where all the weight acts as a single point. The CG range is the envelope within which the CG must fall for safe flight—too far forward and control becomes heavy, too far back and you lose pitch authority. Longitudinal CG is fore-aft; lateral CG is left-right. The basic empty weight is the aircraft alone; max takeoff weight is everything at max limit; max landing weight might be less because fuel burns off. Index units (sometimes kilogram-inches, sometimes percentage of MAC) simplify calculations. AVGAS has specific gravity 0.72, so one litre weighs 0.72 kg. You need to know all this to load your helicopter safely.
exam_tip: Know every term. CG envelope violations can make the helicopter unflyable.
key_facts: Datum: reference line for distance measurements (typically at rotor mast)
Moment arm: distance from datum to a weight
Moment: weight × moment arm, indicates balance effect
Centre of gravity (CG): point where all weight concentrates
source: Wagtendonk Principles of Helicopter Flight, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Effect of exceeding weight limits on helicopter performance
concept: Exceed max takeoff weight and your helicopter suffers immediately. Reduced performance: longer takeoff, sluggish climb, lower hover ceiling. Structural stress: the airframe wasn't designed for that load—fatigue cracks develop. Longer approach speeds and shallower glide paths make landing difficult. You might not be able to clear obstacles during departure. Stability deteriorates. If you discover you're overweight after strapping in, you offload cargo or fuel until you're within limits—non-negotiable. The limits are there because engineers found the breaking point in testing and backed off a safety margin.
exam_tip: Overweight = degraded performance and structural risk. Calculate W&B before every flight.
key_facts: Exceeding max takeoff weight reduces climb performance
Structural loads exceed design limits, causing fatigue
Increased takeoff distance and approach speed
Reduced obstacle clearance and hover ceiling
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Weight and balance calculations and unit conversions
concept: You've got to calculate everything. Takeoff weight: basic empty weight + crew + fuel + cargo. Landing weight: same minus burnt fuel. Fuel weight: litres × specific gravity (0.72 for AVGAS, ~0.8 for AVTUR). Convert US gallons to litres (1 US gal = 3.785 L). Convert pounds to kilograms (1 lb = 0.454 kg). Every weight goes into the calculation; nothing's negligible. Then you calculate each item's moment, add all moments, divide by total weight to find CG position in inches or percentage from datum. Plot it on the CG envelope—it's gotta fall within the green band. If CG's outside limits, reposition cargo or offload weight until it's good.
exam_tip: Practice calculations. Real exams and check-rides test your arithmetic and reasoning.
key_facts: Takeoff weight = empty weight + fuel + crew + cargo
Fuel weight = litres × 0.72 (AVGAS specific gravity)
US gallons × 3.785 = litres; pounds × 0.454 = kilograms
CG position = Σ moments ÷ total weight
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Centre of gravity calculation methods and moment calculations
concept: Finding CG involves three steps: calculate each item's moment (weight × distance from datum), sum all moments, divide by total weight. For example, if the rotor mast is datum (0), the pilot at station 0 (at the mast) contributes zero moment. A 100 kg passenger at station 250 mm behind datum contributes 100 × 250 = 25,000 moment units. Fuel in the aft tank at station 350 adds its moment. All moments added, then divided by total weight, give you CG in millimetres from datum. Simple arithmetic, but you've gotta get it right.
exam_tip: Know the formula: CG = Σ moments ÷ total weight.
key_facts: Moment = weight × distance from datum
Sum all moments from all weights and tanks
CG (in mm) = Σ moments ÷ total weight
All calculations must fit within published CG envelope
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Graphical and tabular methods for centre of gravity determination
concept: You've got two ways to find CG: arithmetic (calculate moments and divide) or graphical (plot weight positions on a graph and find the balance point). Arithmetic's precise but tedious. Graphical's faster for quick rough calculations. Manufacturers often provide loading graphs with weight ranges on one axis and moment on the other; you plot each item, then find the CG by drawing a line and reading the position. Tabular methods use loading tables with CG offsets pre-calculated. Most modern work uses simple arithmetic, but you should recognise graphical methods if shown one.
exam_tip: Know both methods; questions might ask you to use a given graph.
key_facts: Arithmetic method: calculate moments, divide by total weight
Graphical method: plot weights and find balance point visually
Tabular method: use pre-calculated offsets from loading tables
Arithmetic is most common and accurate
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Finding CG position using moments and loading calculations
concept: This is the practical application. You've got a helicopter, you've got a crew, fuel, and cargo. Calculate the empty weight moment (given in the aircraft manual). Add crew moment, cargo moment, fuel moment. Sum all moments. Divide by total weight. Plot on the CG envelope. If it's out, move cargo or adjust fuel loadout until you're in the green. It's the calculation you do before every flight. The earlier calculations teach the principle; this one's the real-world task.
exam_tip: This is a check-ride task. Be fluent with the process.
key_facts: Use empty weight and moment from aircraft manual
Add moments from all crew, cargo, and fuel
Divide Σ moments by total weight for CG position
Verify CG falls within published envelope limits
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Loading and offloading effects on centre of gravity position
concept: Adding or removing weight shifts CG. Add weight forward of current CG, CG moves forward. Add weight aft, CG moves aft. This is intuitive. The further the weight from the current CG, the bigger the shift. Loading large fuel quantities in the aft tank shifts CG back significantly. You plan your loading order: fuel to the forward tank first, then aft, so CG stays in limits as you load. If you add cargo, you calculate the new CG and confirm it's still good. Offloading reverses the effect—drop cargo from the back, CG moves forward.
exam_tip: Understand the direction of CG shift with loading changes.
key_facts: Loading forward of current CG moves CG forward
Loading aft of current CG moves CG aft
Greater distance = greater CG shift
Fuel loading order affects CG progression during fuel load
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Loading to place CG at a specific station position
concept: Sometimes you need to load specific cargo and achieve a target CG. You work backwards: choose your desired CG, calculate the required total moment, then figure out what weights and positions give you that moment. It's algebra—solve for unknown weights or positions. Example: you've got 50 kg cargo to load, and you need to keep CG at the 250 mm station. Where should the cargo sit? Calculate what moment you need total, subtract the moment from everything else, divide by the cargo weight to find its station. This is more advanced but comes up on check-rides and in real situations where you're optimising fuel burn and performance.
exam_tip: This is harder than finding CG from given loadings. But examiners ask it.
key_facts: Work backwards from desired CG to find required weights/positions
Total required moment = desired CG × total weight
Calculate position of unknown weight: station = (required moment − other moments) ÷ unknown weight
Verify all weights and CG remain within limits
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Loading within CG limits and optimising weight distribution
concept: The practical challenge: load the helicopter safely and efficiently. You're balancing weight (more fuel = longer range), balance (CG must be in the envelope), and performance (CG position affects control feel). Forward CG: control is heavier, requires more cyclic pressure, uses more power, less efficient. Aft CG: lighter control, less power required, faster, but less stable. You want CG slightly forward of the aft limit for stability but not so forward you're working hard on the cyclic. This is where experience and judgment come in. The manual gives you the limits; you choose where in the envelope to operate.
exam_tip: Know the limits and why position matters for control feel and efficiency.
key_facts: CG forward of limit: heavier control, more power required
CG aft of limit: lighter control, less power, less stable
Optimal position usually mid-envelope or slightly forward
Calculate and verify before every flight
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Moving weight between stations to adjust centre of gravity
concept: If CG is out of limits, you move weight. Shift fuel from aft tank to forward tank, CG moves forward. Move cargo from the cabin to the cargo pod, CG might move aft (depending on pod location). The calculation: move weight W from station A to station B. The moment change = W × (B − A). If the result moves CG back in limits, you're sorted. This is practical problem-solving—you've got the load, now where do you arrange it?
exam_tip: Question format: 'CG is aft of limits, you've got to move weight. Where?'
key_facts: Moving weight forward of current position moves CG forward
Moving weight aft of current position moves CG aft
Moment change = weight × distance moved
Verify final CG is within envelope
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Effect of fuel consumption on centre of gravity position during flight
concept: As you fly, you burn fuel. If you've got more fuel in the aft tank, burning it shifts CG forward (removes aft weight). If forward tank has more, burning it shifts CG aft (removes forward weight). Smart loading manages this: you deliberately load more fuel aft initially so that as you cruise and burn fuel, CG drifts forward but stays in limits. On a long flight, this is critical planning. Some helicopters have automated fuel transfer systems; you select 'cruise' and the system keeps CG constant by moving fuel between tanks as you fly. Manual helicopters require you to manage crossfeed.
exam_tip: Understand fuel burn shifts CG. Plan loading so CG stays in envelope throughout flight.
key_facts: Burning aft-tank fuel moves CG forward
Burning forward-tank fuel moves CG aft
Load more fuel aft initially to manage CG drift during cruise
Use crossfeed or fuel transfer systems to maintain CG in limits
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual

syllabus_desc: Lateral CG displacement and cyclic control position adjustment
concept: CG doesn't just move fore-aft (longitudinal); it can shift side-to-side (lateral). If you load cargo unequally, CG shifts laterally. A lateral CG shift means you need cyclic offset to maintain level flight—you'd need to hold the cyclic slightly to one side to balance the asymmetrical load. It's not a safety issue (within limits, lateral CG offset is acceptable), but it's uncomfortable and tiring to fly. Most helicopters are fairly symmetric, so lateral CG is rarely a problem. But if you've got unequal cargo or passenger loading, understand that the cyclic will feel offset.
exam_tip: Lateral CG shifts require cyclic offset. Not unsafe, but uncomfortable.
key_facts: Asymmetrical loading (cargo or passengers) shifts lateral CG
Lateral CG offset requires cyclic control offset to maintain level
Not a safety issue within limits, but tiring to fly offset
Symmetric loading preferred to avoid lateral CG issues
source: Wagtendonk, Robinson R22 Manual
